comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A6.3: `gbf-store` — pinsets + GC

**Target crate**: `gbf-store`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-store/src/pinset.rs`, `gbf-store/src/gc.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 138 (pinsets/GC/eviction).

### Background

Without GC, the blob store grows without bound. Pinsets are named sets of blobs that survive GC (e.g., "the latest 5 builds", "the current calibration cohort"). GC walks pinsets, collects all referenced blobs (transitively, since blobs can reference other blobs by hash), and removes the unreferenced.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct PinsetName(pub Cow<'static, str>);

pub struct Pinset {
    pub name: PinsetName,
    pub roots: BTreeSet<Hash256>,
    pub annotation: Option<String>,
}

pub trait BlobReferences {
    fn referenced_blobs(&self) -> Vec<Hash256>;             // transitive references inside this blob's logical content
}

pub struct GcOptions {
    pub dry_run: bool,
    pub max_remove_per_run: Option<usize>,
}

pub struct GcReport {
    pub pinsets_walked: u32,
    pub blobs_kept: u32,
    pub blobs_removed: u32,
    pub bytes_freed: u64,
    pub removed: Vec<Hash256>,
}

pub fn run_gc(store: &BlobStore, pinsets: &[Pinset], opts: &GcOptions) -> GcReport;
```

The transitive reachability requires every blob format to implement `BlobReferences`. For unknown blob types, conservatively treat as "no outgoing refs" (won't pull dependent blobs into the keep set, but won't crash either).

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-store
cargo test -p gbf-store -- gc::pinned_blobs_survive
cargo test -p gbf-store -- gc::transitive_refs                    # A pinned blob's refs are kept
cargo test -p gbf-store -- gc::dry_run_removes_nothing
cargo test -p gbf-store -- gc::report_counts_correct
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §V.1: GcReport is structured JSON.
- §I.1: Pinset is typed.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
**Source-of-truth update — see RFC F-A6** (2026-05-03)

The 2026-04-26 description above is a sketch; **RFC `history/rfcs/F-A6-gbf-store-migrate.md` §§ 8–9 are the authoritative spec** for this task. Where the sketch and the RFC disagree, the RFC wins.

### Authoritative shape — pinsets (RFC §8)

**`gbf-store::pinset`:**
- `PinsetName(String)` — owned, validated at construction (no NUL, no path separators, no `..`, non-empty); validation also runs on deserialize
- `Pinset { name: PinsetName, roots: BTreeSet<Hash256>, annotation: Option<String> }` — `Serialize + Deserialize`
- `BlobReferences` typed trait — `fn referenced_blobs(&self) -> Vec<Hash256>` — for in-memory consumers that already hold a deserialized value
- `PinsetError`

`Pinset.roots` is `BTreeSet<Hash256>` (not `Vec<Hash256>`) so dedup is structural and iteration order is sorted (RFC §17 item 7). `PinsetName` is a validated owned `String`, **not** `Cow<'static, str>` (RFC §17 item 8 — pinset names become filenames; they need validation).

### Authoritative shape — GC (RFC §9)

**`gbf-store::gc`:**
- `run_gc(store, pinsets, refs, opts) -> Result<GcReport, GcError>`
- `GcOptions { dry_run, max_remove_per_run, sweep_stage_cache_indexes, unknown_reference_policy }`
- `UnknownReferencePolicy { Abort, TreatAsLeaf }` — **default is `Abort`**, NOT `TreatAsLeaf` (RFC §17 item 11). Treating unknown formats as leaves can delete blobs reachable only through an undecodable parent — not conservative. The default refuses to GC; consumers opt into `TreatAsLeaf` for formats known to have no outgoing references or for cache-like data.
- `GcReport { pinsets_walked: u64, blobs_kept: u64, candidate_blobs: u64, candidate_bytes: u64, blobs_removed: u64, bytes_freed: u64, removed: Vec<Hash256> }` — counters are `u64`. In dry-run mode `candidate_*` describes what *would* be removed; `blobs_removed`/`bytes_freed`/`removed` stay zero/empty.
- `BlobReferencesRegistry` + `BlobReferenceReader` — recognizer trait that inspects bytes and returns `Option<Vec<Hash256>>`. Passed as a parameter to `run_gc`, **not** a global (RFC §17 item 10).
- `BlobReferenceError`, `GcError`

### Removal order

`Hash256` ascending — `max_remove_per_run` selection is deterministic (RFC §17 item 14).

### Stage-cache index sweep

`GcOptions::sweep_stage_cache_indexes` enables removal of stale stage-cache index files (weak references; stale index → cache miss → recompute) — RFC §17 item 12.

### Why two ref traits

`BlobReferences` (typed trait) is for in-memory consumers that already hold a deserialized value. GC works on opaque bytes and uses `BlobReferenceReader` (returning `Option<Vec<Hash256>>`) through `BlobReferencesRegistry`. Pinsets carry only `Hash256`, so the GC-side trait must recognize-from-bytes rather than rely on a format tag (RFC §17 item 9).

### Test surface (RFC §§ 8.5, 9.4, 19)

`pinset_protection`, `unpinned_blob_removed`, `transitive_refs_via_registry`, `unknown_reference_policy_abort`, `unknown_reference_policy_treat_as_leaf`, `dry_run_populates_candidates`, `max_remove_per_run_honored`, `removal_order_is_hash_ascending`, `sweep_stage_cache_indexes_removes_stale`, `bytes_freed_matches_sum_of_lens`, `report_serde_round_trip`, `name_validation_*`, `deserialize_runs_validation`, `blob_references_trait_compiles`.

title: T-B1.6.10: CI workflow integration — nightly + on-merge heavy-gate runs
description: GitHub Actions workflow .github/workflows/f-b1-heavy-gate.yml. Scheduled nightly (07:17 UTC) plus on-merge for changes touching gbf-{codegen,runtime,emu,bench,report,verify,abi,asm}. Runs regen.sh + verify-packet.sh; uploads run_logs as 30-day artifact; files issue on scheduled-run failure. Prevents heavy gate atrophy between F-B1-related PRs.
close_reason: Implemented L5 review-packet infrastructure that is independent of full L4 closure. Evidence: gbf-report realism_report.v1 schema/self-hash validation; gbf-bench MatmulI8Bringup streaming-ROM runner and structural-count gates; companion artifacts cycle_attribution_n128.json, matmul_n128.map.json, tile_dump_index_n128.json, frame_trace_n128.json, and streaming ROM sidecar; docs/review/f-b1 packet docs/checklist; scripts/review/f-b1/{smoke,regen,verify-packet}.sh; CI wiring and ignored-test discipline meta-check. Current report honestly marks L4 open with frame_service_misses>0 and absent widget/scheduler timing samples.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.6.10: CI workflow integration — nightly + on-merge heavy-gate runs

**Target paths**:
- `.github/workflows/f-b1-heavy-gate.yml`
- `.github/workflows/f-b1-pr-fast.yml` (optional supplement to pre-commit hook)
- `scripts/ci/f_b1_capture_artifacts.sh`

### Background

The pre-commit hook runs `cargo test --workspace --all-features` on every commit. That's the **fast** path — it does NOT run F-B1's heavy `#[ignore]`d gates (per T-B1.6.9 discipline). The review packet's `regen.sh` runs the heavy path manually before each F-B1-related PR.

But: between PR merges, the heavy gates atrophy unless something runs them on a schedule. The realism report drifts. The hard gate at N=128 might silently break and we wouldn't know until the next F-B1-related PR.

This bead implements:

1. A **scheduled nightly** workflow that runs `regen.sh` + `verify-packet.sh` on `main`. If either fails, file an issue and notify.
2. An **on-merge** workflow that runs the same when a PR touching `crates/gbf-{codegen,runtime,emu,bench,report,verify,abi}/` or `history/rfcs/F-B1-*` lands.
3. An **artifact-capture** step that uploads the run logs (T-B1.6.11) and the regenerated report as a GitHub Actions artifact, even on success — useful for forensic comparison across runs.

### Implementation outline (`.github/workflows/f-b1-heavy-gate.yml`)

```yaml
name: F-B1 heavy gate

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "17 7 * * *"          # 07:17 UTC nightly
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - "crates/gbf-codegen/**"
      - "crates/gbf-runtime/**"
      - "crates/gbf-emu/**"
      - "crates/gbf-bench/**"
      - "crates/gbf-report/**"
      - "crates/gbf-verify/**"
      - "crates/gbf-abi/**"
      - "crates/gbf-asm/**"
      - "scripts/review/f-b1/**"
      - "history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md"

jobs:
  heavy-gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 90        # generous; N=128 + sweep + verify-packet
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable
      - uses: Swatinem/rust-cache@v2
      - name: Install hooks
        run: ./scripts/install-hooks.sh
      - name: Pre-flight (fast)
        run: |
          cargo fmt --check --all
          cargo clippy --workspace --all-features -- -D warnings
          cargo test --workspace --all-features  # the fast suite
      - name: Heavy gate — regenerate F-B1 review packet
        run: ./scripts/review/f-b1/regen.sh
      - name: Verify reproducibility
        run: ./scripts/review/f-b1/verify-packet.sh
      - name: Capture artifacts (always)
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: f-b1-heavy-gate-${{ github.run_id }}
          path: |
            docs/review/f-b1/artifacts/
            docs/review/f-b1/artifacts/run_logs/
          retention-days: 30
      - name: File issue on failure (scheduled runs only)
        if: failure() && github.event_name == 'schedule'
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const title = `F-B1 nightly heavy gate failed on ${process.env.GITHUB_REF_NAME}`;
            const body = `Run: ${process.env.GITHUB_SERVER_URL}/${process.env.GITHUB_REPOSITORY}/actions/runs/${process.env.GITHUB_RUN_ID}`;
            await github.rest.issues.create({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              title, body,
              labels: ['f-b1', 'ci-failure', 'heavy-gate']
            });
```

### Implementation outline (`.github/workflows/f-b1-pr-fast.yml`, optional)

Mirrors the pre-commit hook but in CI for branches that haven't installed the hook locally. Runs only the fast suite. Skipped if `--no-fb1-fast` label is present (escape hatch for pure docs PRs).

### Implementation outline (`scripts/ci/f_b1_capture_artifacts.sh`)

Wrapper that ensures the artifact directory layout is consistent regardless of who triggered the run:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
export RUST_LOG="info,gbf=debug"
export RUST_BACKTRACE=1
ROOT="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"
LOG_DIR="$ROOT/docs/review/f-b1/artifacts/run_logs"
mkdir -p "$LOG_DIR"
TS=$(date -u +%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ)
exec > >(tee "$LOG_DIR/run_$TS.log") 2>&1
echo "F-B1 heavy gate run $TS"
"$ROOT/scripts/review/f-b1/regen.sh"
"$ROOT/scripts/review/f-b1/verify-packet.sh"
echo "Run $TS complete."
```

### Logging discipline

- All Rust code in the heavy gate runs with `RUST_LOG=info,gbf=debug`. The `gbf=debug` namespace pulls in T-B1.0.9's typed event vocabulary.
- The captured log file is committed-in-spirit (uploaded as artifact, retention 30 days) but **not** to git — git only holds the bit-reproducible report and packet.

### Why this is load-bearing

Without scheduled CI, the heavy gates only run when someone manually triggers `regen.sh`. Between F-B1-related PRs (which may be weeks apart in M0.5 → M1 transition), regressions in the heavy paths go unnoticed. M1 inherits a possibly-rotted gate.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
# CI lint: workflow file is valid YAML
yamllint .github/workflows/f-b1-heavy-gate.yml
# Smoke: the nightly workflow has been triggered at least once on main
gh workflow view "F-B1 heavy gate" --ref main
```

(The acceptance gates here are necessarily about the workflow file's existence, well-formedness, and successful trigger — not Rust tests.)

### Failure mode and recovery

- Scheduled run fails → auto-filed issue with run URL.
- On-merge run fails → blocks the next PR? **No** — on-merge is post-merge, can't block. Instead it files an issue and notifies the team.
- The fast pre-commit hook remains the gating safeguard for PR-time regressions in the non-heavy paths.

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §14 (long-running test ownership; review packet scripts).

### Dependencies

- T-B1.6.5 (regen.sh + verify-packet.sh exist).
- T-B1.6.11 (run-log capture in regen.sh emits structured logs).

title: T8.3: Implement shadow compile pipeline
description: Shadow compile: (1) export EMA weights to temp ModelArtifact, (2) for each CompileRequest in ShadowCompilePolicy.requests: run ArtifactValidation -> StaticBudgetReport -> (optionally full compile), (3) for each workload: run ArtifactOracle on a few prompts, compare against ReferenceModelBundle, (4) if full compile: run ScheduleCostAnalysis. StaticBudgetReport alone catches most fit failures. Full compile is config-gated because it's expensive. Output: one CheckpointFrontierPoint per checkpoint.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- shadow::pipeline passes; Produces CheckpointFrontierPoint; Budget failure caught without crash; Timeout works

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/shadow.rs` (extend with pipeline)

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "shadow compile pipeline", "ArtifactValidation", "StaticBudgetReport", "conformance".

### Implementation Notes
Shadow compile pipeline (runs at intervals defined by ShadowCompilePolicy):
1. Export EMA weights via ExportVisitor -> ModelArtifact
2. Run ArtifactValidation (schema check)
3. Run StaticBudgetReport (does it fit?)
4. Optionally: full compile (if budget permits and compile is fast enough)
5. Run small conformance suite on specified workloads
6. Output: `CheckpointFrontierPoint` with quality/conformance/fit metrics

- Pipeline should be runnable both inline (blocking training) and async (background thread).
- Timeout: if shadow compile takes >5 minutes, abort and log warning.
- Output goes to `shadow_results/step_{N}/` directory.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- shadow::pipeline     # Tests pass
# Verify: pipeline produces CheckpointFrontierPoint
# Verify: budget failure is caught (fits=false) without crashing training
# Verify: timeout works
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §II.1: Shadow compile must not add >5min latency to training
- §V.1: All pipeline steps emit structured log events
- §V.2: Shadow compile is crash-safe — partial results are fine, training continues

title: T-B4.7: Projected bank-switch + SRAM-page-switch upper bounds
description:
## What
Project per-token bank-switch and SRAM-page-switch counts as
`ProjectedSwitchCount { upper_bound, expected_q16_16, decision_value, source }`.
Bringup/Default/Trace v1 use `upper_bound` for budget decisions. See RFC §7.6.

## Why
A single `u16` count hides whether it's a worst-case bound or a
hint-weighted estimate. The compound type makes the report scientifically
honest. `decision_value` separates "what we measured against" from "what
we projected" so a profile can opt into expected-value decisions later
without losing the worst-case info.

## Inputs
- T-B4.1 RoutingProjection (top-1, top-2, deterministic-once-per-token, etc.).
- T-B4.2 byte-math (for derived per-tile switch projections, where applicable).
- T-B2.13 ResolvedPolicyProduct (for risk policy controlling which
  decision_value is allowed).

## Outputs
- `pub struct ProjectedSwitchCount` (4 fields above).
- `pub enum SwitchProjectionSource { ConservativeStaticUpperBound, HintWeightedExpectedWithStaticCap, CalibrationClosedFormWithStaticCap }`.
- Default rule: `decision_value = upper_bound`.
- Profile may permit expected-value decision only when:
  - Profile explicitly allows it,
  - Expectation source is named,
  - Static upper bound still reported,
  - Risk policy permitting recorded in report.
- Bringup/Default/Trace v1 always use upper bound.

## Pitfalls
- `upper_bound` is ALWAYS present, even when expected-value decision is used.
- Bank switches per token are derived under a NAMED routing assumption
  (top-1, top-2 + tie-break, etc.). The model is recorded in the report.
- SRAM-page-switch math depends on sequence-state size and access pattern.
  Document the model.
- `_q16_16` is fixed-point unsigned; no floats.

## Acceptance
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_routing_model_named_in_report
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_switch_decision_uses_upper_bound_by_default
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_rejects_switches_per_token_over_cap
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_rejects_sram_page_switches_per_token_over_cap

## Refs
- RFC §7.6 ProjectedSwitchCount, §7.7 invariants.
- Depends on: T-B4.1.
- Blocks: T-B4.11.

title: T-B1.5: L4 — Cooperative yielding + 60 Hz frame gate (N=128)
description: Yield insertion at K-lane boundaries (KLaneFullTile default, KLaneRows4 fallback). VBlank-driven 4-hex-digit progress widget. Hard gate: frame_service_misses==0, scheduler_service_count==gated_frame_count (proves cooperative loop, not just ISR), yield_while_bank_lease_active_count==0, byte-exact output.
notes: RFC source of truth reconciled in PR. Implemented/proven so far: L0-L3 streaming matmul baseline with one loaded ROM per N, BankLease panel copies, tile-safe PC + existing DumpArena-shaped host read/resume, N={32,64,96,128} byte-exact vs gbf-verify, and an honest realism report. Still open and intentionally not claimed: ROM-side F-A3 harness mailbox/control-plane pause/resume, scheduler-visible yield insertion within compute quanta, liveness progress_epoch integration, VBlank/video_commit frame-service widget, real gbf-emu VBlank/Widget/Scheduler/Yield frame events, ordering/cycle attribution from real events, and L4 frame_service_misses==0 hard gate. Current artifact reports frame_service_misses>0 and omits absent L4 timing samples.
close_reason: Completed in F-B1: cooperative N=128 L4 frame gate is green with KLaneRow, runtime-generated VBlank handler, zero frame misses, scheduler/widget service count == gated_frame_count, max_no_progress_frames=1, max unyielded/lease hold 17059 mcycles <= frame minus 128. Evidence: cargo test -p gbf-emu --lib f_b1_l4 -- --ignored --nocapture (3 passed, 116.79s), scripts/review/f-b1/regen.sh, gbf-debug safe-point script.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.5: L4 — Cooperative yielding and 60 Hz frame gate (N = 128)

### Background

L4 is the cooperative-scheduling stress test. The kernel must yield often enough that a VBlank-driven UI widget updates **every gated frame** at N = 128. The gate is **hard**: `frame_service_misses == 0` and `yield_while_bank_lease_active_count == 0` and the byte-exact match still holds.

### Why the gate is structural, not aspirational

If the cooperative scheduler cannot host sustained compute at the headline N, then M1's quantized dense kernel — which does *more* work per token — definitely cannot. The gate's purpose is to surface that cost ceiling **before** M1 commits to model-size choices that depend on it. Soft-gating defeats the milestone.

If after iterating on yield quantum (KLaneFullTile → KLaneRows4), table cache locality, and scheduler tunables the gate cannot hold at N = 128, the bead does not silently close. Either iterate further or land an RFC amendment that records the measured ceiling as the deliverable.

### Frame gate semantics — load-bearing

The naive frame gate ("widget tick happens before next VBlank") can be passed by an ISR-driven widget update even if the cooperative scheduler is starving. That would prove the wrong thing. The corrected gate requires both:

- `WidgetTickDispatched(F)` exists for every gated frame F.
- **`SchedulerServicedFrame(F)` exists** for every gated frame F (proves the cooperative loop, not just the ISR).
- Both occur strictly before `VBlankFired(F+1)`.

The renamed metric is `frame_service_misses`, not `vblank_misses`. The hardware VBlank signal isn't what's failing if this fires; the cooperative service is.

### Yield quantum — load-bearing

Yielding only at K-tile boundaries (4096 products per quantum) is too coarse. F-B1 separates storage tile (16×16×16) from yield quantum:

- Default: `KLaneFullTile` = 16×16 = 256 products per quantum.
- Fallback: `KLaneRows4` = 4×16 = 64 products per quantum.

The chosen quantum is recorded in the realism report's `runtime_knobs`.

### Critical safety invariant: no yield while lease active

This is a separate gate from the frame gate. The codegen lowering must structure the loop as: lease A → copy panel → release A → lease B → copy panel → release B → assert no lease active → enter compute/yield region. Yielding under a lease lets the scheduler/ISR observe ambiguous ROMX state.

### What L4 proves

- Yield insertion at K-lane boundaries works without breaking byte exactness.
- The progress widget ticks once per gated frame, driven by the cooperative scheduler, not just by the ISR.
- `SchedulerServicedFrame(F)` exists for every gated frame F.
- `frame_service_misses == 0` over the headline N=128 run.
- `max_no_progress_frames <= 1`.
- `yield_while_bank_lease_active_count == 0`.
- Byte-exact output still holds under cooperative scheduling.

### Subtask plan

- **T-B1.5.1** Yield insertion in matmul lowering (KLaneFullTile + KLaneRows4 paths).
- **T-B1.5.2** Liveness counter integration (`progress_epoch` advances per yield quantum).
- **T-B1.5.3** VBlank progress widget (4-hex-digit, tile-based, video_commit-driven).
- **T-B1.5.4** gbf-emu frame events (VBlankFired, WidgetTickDispatched, SchedulerServicedFrame, YieldReturnedToScheduler).
- **T-B1.5.5** Frame trace ordering test + cycle attribution rule.
- **T-B1.5.6** L4 hard gate suite (heavy, ignored — owned by review script).

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- f_b1_progress_widget_tile_render_known_vector
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- f_b1_progress_widget_ticks_once_per_vblank
cargo test -p gbf-emu -- f_b1_frame_event_trace_orders_vblank_and_widget
cargo test -p gbf-emu -- --ignored f_b1_l4_n128_no_frame_service_misses
cargo test -p gbf-emu -- --ignored f_b1_l4_liveness_no_progress_frames_bounded
cargo test -p gbf-emu -- --ignored f_b1_l4_output_matches_reference
```

### Hard gate (closure condition)

```text
frame_service_misses == 0
widget_update_count == gated_frame_count
scheduler_service_count == gated_frame_count
max_no_progress_frames <= 1
byte_exact_match == true
yield_while_bank_lease_active_count == 0
```

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §2.6, §7.8, §8.5, §9.

### Dependencies

- T-B1.4 (L3) — full tiling with streamed output.
- **F-A5** (Bank0 cooperative runtime) — provides scheduler, video_commit, VBlank handler. If not closed, T-B1.5.3 owns the minimal subset needed for F-B1.

title: F-B17: StageCache integration across all stages
description: Wire every transformative compiler stage into the two-level StageCache from gbf-store. Each stages output is content-addressed; --resume-from layered on top. Mostly mechanical wrapper work once individual stages exist.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B15: StageCache integration across all stages

### What this is

Wire every transformative compiler stage into the two-level `StageCache` from `gbf-store` (`planv0.md` engineering rule 20). Each stage's input includes a stable hash of its predecessors and inputs; output is content-addressed and stored. `--resume-from <stage>` is layered on top.

### Why this matters

Iteration speed. A typical training run shadow-compiles dozens of checkpoints; without stage caching, each does the full pipeline. With caching, only the parts that actually changed re-run.

### Design sketch

For each stage:
1. Compute `StageKey` from typed inputs (no untyped `Display` / `format!`-based hashing).
2. `StageCache::get(key)` — return cached output if present.
3. Run the stage.
4. `StageCache::put(key, output)`.

Stage-by-stage integration; each pass implementer adds the caching wrapper.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A6.2 StageCache (infrastructure)
- F-B0..F-B13 (each stage individually)

### Confidence: HIGH. Mostly mechanical wrapper work once stages exist.

title: T2.2: Emit RuntimeChromeBudget from runtime shell build
description: After the runtime/UI shell is assembled (Bank0 layout + common banks), emit a RuntimeChromeBudget artifact. Requires: Bank0 occupancy from PlacedRom, common-bank occupancy, expert-bank template with reserved slack. Output: chrome_budget.json alongside the shell build products. The runtime_nucleus_hash should match what goes into KernelCalibrationBundle so stale budgets are cross-checkable.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check passes; chrome_budget.json emitted after runtime build; Budget reflects actual runtime nucleus size; JSON deserializes into RuntimeChromeBudget

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-runtime` (or wherever runtime shell build lives)
**Target file(s)**: Runtime build pipeline — search for runtime/UI shell assembly code. Output: `chrome_budget.json`.

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "runtime shell", "chrome budget", "Bank0", "RuntimeNucleus".

### Implementation Notes
- After the runtime/UI shell is assembled (boot, interrupts, scheduler, joypad, text, keyboard, video_commit, panic, trace, harness, persistence modules), measure how much ROM space each bank class has consumed.
- Emit a `RuntimeChromeBudget` artifact as `chrome_budget.json`:
  - Bank0Free: bytes remaining in bank 0 after runtime nucleus
  - CommonBank: bytes remaining in each common bank after shared kernels/embeddings
  - ExpertBank: full capacity of each expert bank (minus bank header/stubs)
  - wram_reserved: WRAM bytes used by runtime
  - sram_reserved: SRAM bytes used by runtime
- This is the **build-specific** budget that the trainer needs for preflight.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check --workspace                           # Compiles
# Verify: chrome_budget.json is emitted after runtime build
# Verify: budget reflects actual runtime nucleus size, not hardcoded values
# Verify: JSON deserializes into RuntimeChromeBudget from gbf-policy
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §V.1: Structured JSON output, not human-readable text
- §IV.3: Reproducible — same runtime code produces same budget

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: EXTERNAL DEPENDENCY WARNING: This task requires the runtime/UI shell (gbf-runtime) to be assembled first, which is a separate subsystem not tracked in the training-contract beads. Options: (1) Stub with a hardcoded chrome_budget.json for testing, implement real emission when gbf-runtime is ready. (2) Defer until gbf-runtime work is scoped. Recommend option 1 — create a mock/fixture budget for now.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Refinement from 2026-04-26 model/runtime interface session

### Reference shell composition (pinned)

The first `RuntimeChromeBudget` emitted from a real runtime shell build must use the **minimal + UI** module set:

- Included: `boot`, `interrupts`, `scheduler`, `banking`, `joypad`, `text`, `keyboard`, `video_commit`.
- Excluded (do not link into the reference shell): `persistence`, `trace`, `harness`, `panic`.

This matches the configuration the trainer pins against (see F2 / bd-1hv decisions and T2.4 `ReferenceShellSpec`). The emitter records the included module set in `RuntimeChromeBudget.reference_shell_modules` (see T2.1 refinement comment) so drift is detectable.

### Hash contract

`runtime_nucleus_hash` covers:
- Linked module set (`reference_shell_modules`).
- Encoded section bytes for Bank0 nucleus (boot + ISR + scheduler + banking + UI + text + keyboard + video_commit).
- `CompileProfile` id (so a profile change forces a new hash).
- The build's `gbf-runtime` source version stamp.

The hash must match what `KernelCalibrationBundle.runtime_nucleus_hash` records, otherwise calibration is invalidated against this budget.

### Slack accounting

`RomBudgetSlot.reserved_slack` for Bank0Free includes a named reservation for each future module the reference shell does not yet ship (`persistence`, `trace`, `harness`, `panic`). T2.4 defines those reserved byte counts. The emitter must subtract them from `usable_bytes` and record the breakdown in `BuildReports` so it is auditable.

### Pre-runtime: synthetic budget path

Until the real runtime shell exists, training uses a synthetic reference `RuntimeChromeBudget` (T2.6) under a stable, explicitly-marked hash. T2.2 (this task) is the *real* emission path; T2.6 is the pre-runtime stand-in. The two must produce the same `RuntimeChromeBudget` schema so trainer code is not branchy.

### EXTERNAL DEPENDENCY note (unchanged)

This task still requires the runtime/UI shell to exist. The earlier comment's option (1) "stub with hardcoded chrome_budget.json" is now formalized as T2.6 (synthetic reference budget) and is no longer ad hoc.

title: T-A1.9: ROM builder — .gb assembler with cartridge header
description: Assembles encoded sections into a real .gb file: cartridge header at 0x100-0x14F (Nintendo logo, MBC type, ROM/RAM size, header + global checksums), ROM padded to power-of-two, bank packing (bank N at N*0x4000). Output must boot in SameBoy/BGB.
close_reason:
Completed T-A1.9 ROM builder and tiny_rom artifact path.

ASM checklist:
- Boundary: rom::assemble_rom consumes EncodedSection plus PlacedSection; it does not lower symbolic branches or encode Instrs except the internal cartridge header section through encode_section.
- Header path: the cartridge header is materialized as typed Instr/DataBlock items in an internal LegalizedSection, then routed through the encoder. User sections overlapping the header range are rejected.
- Bank mapping: ROM0 maps directly and ROMX bank N maps as N*0x4000 + (cpu-$4000); non-ROM placements are rejected.
- Fit checks: bank range, section/header collisions, section/section collisions, encoded-size mismatches, and missing $0150 entry coverage are typed RomAssemblyError variants.
- Determinism: unused bytes are 0xFF; checksums and packet artifacts are byte-stable.
- Raw bytes: no public raw executable escape hatch added.

Claim-to-gate matrix:
- Header checksum -> rom::header_checksum_known_vector
- Global checksum -> rom::global_checksum_round_trip
- Nintendo logo -> rom::nintendo_logo_present
- ROM size power-of-two -> rom::power_of_two_size
- RAM size header bytes -> rom::ram_size_header_bytes
- ROMX bank offset -> rom::bank_n_at_correct_offset
- Unused 0xFF fill -> rom::unused_regions_are_ff
- Determinism -> rom::deterministic
- Collision/fit hard errors -> rom::user_header_range_rejected, rom::overlapping_sections_are_rejected, rom::section_size_mismatch_is_rejected, rom::entry_point_is_required, rom::invalid_rom_size_for_layout
- Tiny ROM reproducibility -> ./scripts/review/f-a1/verify-packet.sh and checked docs/review/f-a1/artifacts/tiny_rom.*

Gates run: cargo test -p gbf-asm --all-features (94 passed); cargo clippy -p gbf-asm --all-features -- -D warnings; ./scripts/review/f-a1/verify-packet.sh.
Deferred: live emulator boot validation and production BankLease/BankGuard ABI remain owned by follow-up features per the RFC.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A1.9: ROM builder — `.gb` assembler with cartridge header

**Target crate**: `gbf-asm`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-asm/src/rom.rs` (new)
**Reference**: `planv0.md` §"EncodedRom" (line 1565), Pan Docs cartridge header layout.

### Background

The output of M0 needs to be **a real `.gb` file** that boots in SameBoy/BGB. That requires:
1. A valid cartridge header at `$0100–$014F` (Nintendo logo, title, MBC type, ROM size, RAM size, header checksum, global checksum).
2. ROM padded to a power-of-two size (32 KiB minimum, MBC5 supports up to 8 MiB).
3. Bank packing — each `PlacedSection` written at its bank's offset within the ROM blob.

This task is small (mostly transcription) but load-bearing: every M0 demo and every later compiled ROM goes through it.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct CartridgeHeader {
    pub title: [u8; 16],                      // padded with zeros
    pub mbc_type: MbcType,                    // MBC5 for our targets
    pub rom_size_kib: u32,                    // 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192
    pub ram_size_kib: u32,                    // 0, 8, 32, 128, 256
    pub destination_code: u8,
    pub old_licensee_code: u8,                // 0x33 — use new licensee
    pub new_licensee_code: [u8; 2],
    pub mask_rom_version: u8,
}

pub fn assemble_rom(
    encoded_sections: &[(EncodedSection, PlacedSection)],
    header: &CartridgeHeader,
    target: TargetProfileId,
) -> Result<Vec<u8>, RomAssemblyError>;
```

Header checksum (`$014D`) is the standard `(x = 0; for i in $0134..=$014C: x = x - rom[i] - 1) & 0xFF`. Global checksum at `$014E–$014F` is bigendian sum of all bytes except those two.

The Nintendo logo at `$0104–$0133` is fixed bytes (well-known). Hardcode it.

Sections placed in bank 0 go to `0x0000..=0x3FFF` of the rom blob. Sections in bank N go to `N * 0x4000`. Unused regions are filled with `0xFF` (canonical).

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-asm
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- rom::header_checksum                    # Matches Pan Docs algorithm
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- rom::global_checksum
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- rom::power_of_two_size                  # ROM size always 2^n
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- rom::nintendo_logo_present              # Bytes at $0104-$0133 are correct
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- rom::section_placement                  # Section in bank N appears at N*0x4000
cargo run -p gbf-asm --example tiny_rom                          # Produces a .gb that boots in SameBoy
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `MbcType` and `RomAssemblyError` are enumerated; bad inputs fail loudly.
- §IV.3: Bit-stability — same inputs -> identical .gb bytes.

title: T-B2.13: Stage 0.5 constraint-frame merge resolver
description:
## What
Implement `resolve_policy(validation: &ValidationProduct) -> Result<ResolvedPolicyProduct, PolicyResolutionStageFailure>`
in `gbf-codegen::stages::policy`. Collect typed `ConstraintFrame`s from six
sources, merge them by typed rules, return a `ResolvedPolicyProduct` or
emit a failed `policy_resolution.json` report. See RFC §7.4.

## Why
Linear procedural resolution is too easy to make non-monotone or
non-deterministic. Defining resolution by frame collection + merge rules
makes correctness independent of incidental mutation order — and makes
the merge invariants testable.

## Inputs
- T-B2.4 ValidationProduct (carries `ValidatedInputs` + identity hashes).
- T-B2.12 CompileProfileSpec fixtures.
- T-B2.1 diagnostic taxonomy.
- T-B2.3 (envelope) and T-B2.19 (policy_resolution.v1 schema, partial).
- `gbf-policy::compile::{ResolvedCompilePolicy, CompileKnobs, ...}`.

## Outputs
- `pub struct ConstraintFrame` with `source`, `evidence`, `defaults`,
  `hard_bounds`, `preferences`, `locks`.
- `pub struct ResolvedPolicyProduct` with `policy`, `input_hashes`,
  `artifact_validation_self_hash`, `report`,
  `policy_resolution_self_hash`, `policy_resolution_canonical_bytes_hash`.
- `pub struct PolicyResolutionStageFailure` with `report`, `diagnostics`.
- The merge meet operation for `hard_bounds` (knob's declared partial order).
- Lock + override application in correct order.
- Monotonicity verifier.

## Pitfalls
- Frame ordering is *implementation-recommended*, not correctness-defining.
  The merge rules define correctness.
- Locks apply *after* profile frames and *before* compile-request overrides
  — locked-knob overrides are hard diagnostics
  (`PolicyKnobLockedAndOverridden`).
- Compile-request overrides may NEVER loosen a bound. There is no
  profile-time relaxation surface (§2.13).
- Calibration may set values or tighten thresholds, but never weaken hard
  CompileRequest constraints.
- A failure here emits a *failed* `policy_resolution.json`, NOT a mutation
  of `artifact_validation.json` (§2.1, §7.9.3).

## Acceptance
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_resolve_policy_target_defaults_seed_global
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_resolve_policy_profile_defaults_tighten_bounds
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_resolve_policy_hints_apply_within_bounds
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_resolve_policy_constraints_tighten_bounds
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_resolve_policy_overrides_only_tighten
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_resolve_policy_calibration_data_drives_pressure_thresholds
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_resolve_policy_rejects_locked_knob_override
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_resolve_policy_rejects_out_of_bounds_value
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_resolve_policy_failure_emits_policy_resolution_failure_report
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_resolve_policy_failure_does_not_mutate_artifact_validation_report

## Refs
- RFC §7.4 resolution model, §2.1 emission policy, §7.9.3 worked example.
- planv0 §"The compile-request boundary" (line ~630).
- Depends on: T-B2.4, T-B2.12.
- Blocks: T-B2.14, T-B2.15, T-B2.16, T-B2.17, T-B2.19, T-B4.1.

title: F2: RuntimeChromeBudget — Build-Specific Bank Capacity Contract
description: Define RuntimeChromeBudget/RomBudgetSlot/BudgetSlotClass so the trainer reasons about actual free bank capacity after the UI/runtime shell build, not a stale 16 KiB constant. Wire into gbf-train preflight alongside DeployabilityEnvelope.
acceptance_criteria: All T2.x tasks closed; cargo test -p gbf-policy -- budget passes; cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight passes

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F2: RuntimeChromeBudget — Build-Specific Bank Capacity Contract

### Background

The plan repeatedly talks about "bank-sized experts" and "15.5 KiB budget" but this number is a hand-waved approximation. In reality, the free space in each bank class depends on the specific runtime shell build: how much Bank0 space the scheduler/ISR/UI/keyboard/text renderer/video commit queue consume, how much common-bank space embeddings/router/classifier/shared LUTs occupy, and how much expert-bank slack is reserved for stubs and metadata.

### The problem

Without RuntimeChromeBudget, the trainer has two bad options:
1. Use a static constant (e.g., 15.5 KiB) that's wrong for every actual build
2. Parse the build's map.json to reverse-engineer available space

Both are fragile. The first silently produces experts that don't fit. The second couples the trainer to the compiler's output format.

### Design

RuntimeChromeBudget is emitted as a structured artifact from the runtime/UI shell build. It contains:
- `target` and `profile` (so the trainer knows which build this is for)
- `runtime_nucleus_hash` (so stale budgets are detectable)
- `rom_slots`: a Vec of `RomBudgetSlot`, each describing a bank class (Bank0Free, CommonBank, ExpertBank) with `usable_bytes`, `reserved_slack`, and supported `placement_caps`
- `wram_reserved` and `sram_reserved` for hot/persistent memory budgets

The trainer's preflight checks expert payload sizes against the smallest ExpertBank slot's usable_bytes, not a hardcoded constant. This automatically adapts as the runtime shell grows or shrinks.

### Why "chrome" in the name

"Chrome" is the browser-industry term for the UI frame around content. Here it means: the runtime shell is the chrome, and the model inference is the content. The budget tells you how much room the content has after the chrome is installed.

### Confidence: HIGH

This is a clean, simple contract that prevents a real failure mode. The only reservation is timing — you need a working runtime shell build before you can emit it, so this becomes practical around M2-M3.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions (Feature)

**This is a feature bead.** Implement via child tasks T2.1 through T2.3.

**Target crate(s)**: `gbf-policy` (types), `gbf-runtime` (emit), `gbf-train` (preflight)
**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "RuntimeChromeBudget", "chrome", "bank capacity".

### Feature Definition of Done

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- budget                 # Type tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight               # Preflight tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-test -- budget_tests             # Integration tests pass
```

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Architecture decisions from 2026-04-26 model/runtime interface session

The model/runtime interface for respecting the GB memory map was reviewed against planv0.md. Four decisions were committed; this comment records them so future agents do not re-litigate.

### 1. Reference runtime shell composition (pinned for pre-training)

The shell used to emit the canonical pre-training `RuntimeChromeBudget` is **minimal + UI**:

- Included: `boot`, `interrupts`, `scheduler`, `banking`, `joypad`, `text`, `keyboard`, `video_commit`.
- **Excluded** from the reference shell: `persistence`, `trace`, `harness`, `panic`.

When any excluded module ships in production, `RuntimeChromeBudget` changes — Bank0Free shrinks, `wram_reserved` and `sram_reserved` grow. Models trained against the current reference shell must be re-preflighted (or retrained) at that point. This is captured in T2.4 (`ReferenceShellSpec` with explicit module set) and T2.5 (CI drift gate on `runtime_nucleus_hash`).

### 2. Slack reservation policy

`reserved_slack` and `wram_reserved` / `sram_reserved` carry **named** reservations for known-future modules so the budget does not silently shift when those modules land. T2.4 defines `ReferenceShellSpec` with a per-module slack table covering `persistence`, `trace`, `harness`, `panic`. The shell emitter records "future-reserved" bytes alongside actually-consumed bytes; preflight subtracts both.

This is option (a) of "reserve named slack now" vs "retrain on shell expansion." The intent is to absorb at least one shell expansion without invalidating training.

### 3. Preflight cadence

Two checkpoints, both required:

- **Pre-training pin.** Trainer captures `runtime_nucleus_hash` from the chosen reference `RuntimeChromeBudget` at training start. Drift mid-training is a hard failure (T2.5 — CI gate).
- **Per-export re-validate.** Export pipeline re-runs preflight against the *current* `RuntimeChromeBudget`. If the production shell has drifted from the training-time reference, export refuses with diagnostic listing both hashes (T2.7).

Pre-runtime training is supported via a deterministic synthetic reference budget under a stable, marked hash (T2.6) so F2 is useful at M1–M2 before a real runtime shell exists.

### 4. `runtime_nucleus_hash` is load-bearing

The hash field in `RuntimeChromeBudget` is the single drift detector between training-time and production-time. Any change to the runtime nucleus binary (or its config) bumps the hash. Drift is gated by CI (T2.5), not informally tracked.

### Cross-references

- WRAM layout (fixed overlay region + ArenaPlan-managed remainder) and the BringUp `CompileProfile` (with hard switch cap) are tracked under F11.
- Sequence-state experimentation (LinearState vs BoundedKv on the same training scaffold) is tracked under F12.
- `wram_reserved: u16` becomes structured `WramReserved { overlay, hot_arena_floor, total }` — see T11.1 and the comment on T2.1.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Session 2026-05-06: matched-deployed-bytes parity rule

F2 RuntimeChromeBudget gains a downstream consumer in F13 (Dense Baseline Track): when comparing dense vs MoE on the same `ExpertBank` budget, the dense FFN's `d_ff` scales up to consume the bytes that experts would have used. The parity-comparison logic computes "deployed bytes available for FFN/expert weights" once from the budget, then enforces that any compared dense run uses approximately that byte count via `TernaryLinearQat::compute_byte_cost`.

No type changes here. The rule is a comparison-time invariant, not a budget-time one.

Also: the new F14 ModelSizeProfile registry (in gbf-policy) cross-validates against this budget at preflight time. T2.3 (wire RuntimeChromeBudget into preflight) gains a check that the active `ModelSizeProfile`'s expert byte cost ≤ smallest `ExpertBank` slot's `usable_bytes`.

title: T-B2.0b: gbf-artifact + gbf-workload schema absorption for F-B2 consumption
description:
## What
Fill the stub modules in `gbf-artifact` and `gbf-workload` with the minimal
schema F-B2 Wave-2 tasks consume. Today, the following modules are still
`//! Module stub.` on disk but every Stage-0 validation class lists them
as inputs:

- `gbf-artifact/src/manifest.rs` -- stub
- `gbf-artifact/src/aux.rs` -- stub
- `gbf-artifact/src/lowerings.rs` -- stub
- `gbf-workload/src/manifest.rs` -- stub

Schema-only deliverables (logic is owned by the consuming F-B2 tasks):

- `gbf-artifact::manifest`:
  - `ArtifactManifest` { schema_version, semantic_core_hash, components,
    lineage, required_features, ... }
  - `ManifestInvariant` (closed enum used by `ManifestInvariantViolated`)
  - `ArtifactSchemaVersion`, `ArtifactFeature`, `LineageId`
- `gbf-artifact::aux`:
  - `ArtifactAux` { golden_vectors, checkpoint_schema, conformance_envelope,
    reference_observation_cache_digest, interaction_bundle, ... }
  - `SidecarKind` (closed enum used by `ArtifactAuxSidecarMissing` /
    `ArtifactAuxSidecarDigestMismatch`)
- `gbf-artifact::lowerings`:
  - `TargetDataLoweringArtifact` (per-shard packed lowering, manifest hash)
  - `LoweringShard`, `LoweringShardRef`, `LoweringManifest`
  - `PackerVersion` newtype, `DataLoweringProfileId`
  - Per-shard round-trip API skeleton (logic in T-B2.7)
- `gbf-artifact` (top-level or new module):
  - `HintBundle` { facts: ExportFacts, preferences: CompilePreferences,
    constraints: BuildConstraints }
  - `BuildConstraints` (the missing third leg; ExportFacts + CompilePreferences
    already exist as real modules)
  - `EvidenceScope` (used by `HintProvenanceInconsistent`)
- `gbf-workload::manifest`:
  - `WorkloadManifest`, `WorkloadManifestRef`, `WorkloadId`
  - `GoldenVectorRef`, `GoldenVectorId` (or confirm these live in
    `gbf-artifact::golden`; align refs)
- `gbf-policy::compile` (extension of T-B2.0):
  - `CalibrationBundleSet`, `CalibrationBundle`, `CalibrationLayer`,
    `CalibrationSetRef`, `BootstrapCalibrationBundle` (with declared
    `CalibrationConfidenceClass::None`)
  - These could alternatively go in `gbf-policy::calibration` if a new module
    is preferred. `gbf-hw::calibration::CalibrationConfidenceClass` is
    already real and consumed.

## Why
RFC §2.12 lists `gbf-policy` types F-B2 must introduce, and we created T-B2.0
for those. But the same RFC sections (§7.3 validation classes, §7.4.5 schema)
treat `ArtifactManifest`, `ArtifactAux`, `TargetDataLoweringArtifact`,
`WorkloadManifestRef`, `HintBundle`, and `CalibrationBundleSet` as
already-existing inputs -- and they are not. No active F-A or F-G or F-E
task owns their schema definition; the closest is `bd-209g`, a P2 *consumer*
task that waits for these contracts to exist.

Without this task, the first Wave-2 implementer (T-B2.4 / bd-3j65) hits a
wall the moment they try to import `gbf_artifact::manifest::ArtifactManifest`
and silently absorbs the schema work into their PR, blowing scope.

## Inputs
- `gbf-foundation::{Hash256, BlobRef, SemVer, ByteCost, ids}`.
- T-B2.0 (bd-558z) -- `gbf-policy` core types this task may reference.
- `gbf-hw::calibration::CalibrationConfidenceClass` (already real).
- `gbf-artifact::core::ArtifactCore` (already real).
- `gbf-artifact::weight_plan::TernaryWeightPlan` (already real).
- `gbf-artifact::export_facts` and `preferences` (already real, used to
  assemble `HintBundle`).
- planv0 §"ArtifactManifest should include" (~line 714).
- planv0 §"TargetDataLoweringArtifact is deterministic and derived"
  (~line 731).
- planv0 §"HintBundle is load-bearing" (~line 738).

## Outputs
- All types listed above with `serde::{Serialize, Deserialize}`,
  `deny_unknown_fields`, explicitly tagged enums.
- Round-trip serde tests for every type.
- Deny-unknown-fields tests.
- Module exports compile cleanly with `-D warnings`.
- A short fixture builder helper in `gbf-test` or per-crate `test_support`
  so Wave 2 can construct synthetic inputs without reinventing them.

## Pitfalls
- DO NOT include validation logic. This is schema-only -- the validators
  in T-B2.5 (manifest invariants), T-B2.6 (aux sidecars), T-B2.7 (lowering
  round-trip), T-B2.9 (hint provenance + workload refs), T-B2.8 (calibration
  binding) do that work.
- DO NOT extend the artifact stratum beyond what F-B2 needs. Lexical /
  decode / interaction full schemas belong to F-G (Epic G); add only the
  minimum stubs F-B2 dereferences (e.g. `InteractionBundle` may be a
  one-field struct).
- DO NOT introduce `gbf-migrate` paths. Lossy migration is deferred to
  F-A6b; T-B2.5 owns the lossless in-memory adapter registry separately.
- `BuildConstraints` is the missing leg of HintBundle; do not silently
  alias it to `CompilePreferences`.
- For `CalibrationBundleSet`, the constituent `CalibrationBundle` carries
  `target_profile_hash`, `kernel_set_hash`, `packer_version`,
  `calibration_schema_hash`, and the declared confidence -- these are the
  scalars T-B2.8 (calibration freshness) compares.

## Acceptance
- `gbf-artifact::manifest_round_trip`
- `gbf-artifact::aux_round_trip`
- `gbf-artifact::lowerings_round_trip`
- `gbf-artifact::hint_bundle_round_trip`
- `gbf-workload::manifest_round_trip`
- `gbf-policy::calibration_bundle_set_round_trip`
  (or `gbf-policy::compile::calibration_*_round_trip` depending on module choice)
- All five crates (`gbf-artifact`, `gbf-workload`, `gbf-policy`,
  `gbf-foundation`, `gbf-hw`) compile cleanly with `-D warnings`.
- Bootstrap calibration bundle constructible with `CalibrationConfidenceClass::None`.

## Refs
- RFC §2.12 (current-code note + must-introduce list -- this task absorbs
  the artifact/workload halves the RFC implicitly assumes existed).
- RFC §7.3 validation classes (every class lists these types).
- RFC §7.4.5 ArtifactValidationReportBody (uses ManifestInvariant, etc.).
- planv0 §"ArtifactManifest should include" (~714).
- planv0 §"TargetDataLoweringArtifact" (~731).
- planv0 §"HintBundle" (~738).
- bd-209g -- downstream consumer; will be unblocked once this lands.
- T-B2.0 (bd-558z) -- sibling foundational task for `gbf-policy`.
- Wave 1 in RFC §8.5 (this is Wave 1.5 conceptually).
- Blocks: T-B2.3, T-B2.4, T-B2.6, T-B2.7, T-B2.8, T-B2.9, T-B2.18.
close_reason: Superseded by per-crate split T-B2.0a..T-B2.0f

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Retired (2026-05-07): superseded by per-crate split

This lumped task is being superseded by six properly-scoped Wave-0 schema
absorption tasks (T-B2.0a..T-B2.0f), one per crate/module gap. The split
gives each task a tight blast radius and lets the Wave-0 batch execute in
parallel.

See RFC §2.14 (Schema absorption from upstream stubs) and §12.1 (Bead
inventory) for the new task IDs and deliverables.

title: F11: CompileProfile + WRAM Layout — BringUp, Overlay Reload, Switch Cap
description: Define the CompileProfile registry and the WRAM layout contract: WramLayoutPolicy (fixed overlay region + ArenaPlan-managed remainder), OverlayReloadPolicy (PerExpertSwitch default), and the named BringUp profile with a hard max_bank_switches_per_token cap. Production profiles are deferred until F12 sequence-state experimentation resolves.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F11: CompileProfile + WRAM Layout Contract

### Background

The plan (planv0.md) repeatedly refers to `CompilePreferences`, `BuildConstraints`, and a "WRAM hot arena" without ever pinning down the *layout contract* that bounds them. Two specific gaps surfaced in the 2026-04-26 model/runtime interface session:

1. The default residency strategy for expert hot kernels is **WRAM overlay** (per-expert-switch reload). Overlay competes with the hot arena for 8 KiB of WRAM, so the split must be a contract, not a vibe.
2. There is no named `CompileProfile` in the codebase. Preflight, switch-cap enforcement, and policy gating all need named profiles to refer to. Decision from the session: define **`BringUp` only for now**; production profiles wait until F12 (sequence-state experimentation) resolves.

### The problem this solves

Without F11:
- Preflight can't tell the trainer how much WRAM is actually free for the activation/accumulator/scratch arena, because overlay size is implicit.
- The "hard switch cap per profile" decision has nowhere to live. `max_bank_switches_per_token` exists as a `BuildConstraints` field (planv0.md) but is not bound to a named profile.
- `OverlayPlan` (planv0.md compiler-pipeline section 8.5) has no input shape — the compiler can't know how big to size the overlay region.

### Design

Add three policy types to `gbf-policy`:

```rust
pub struct WramLayoutPolicy {
    pub overlay_bytes: u16,
    pub continuation_bytes: u16,
    pub stack_bytes: u16,
    pub hot_arena_bytes_min: u16,
}

pub enum OverlayReloadPolicy {
    PerExpertSwitch,  // default
    PerSlice,
    PerLayerStep,
}

pub struct CompileProfile {
    pub id: CompileProfileId,
    pub wram_layout: WramLayoutPolicy,
    pub overlay_reload: OverlayReloadPolicy,
    pub max_bank_switches_per_token: u16,
    pub sequence_state: SequenceSemanticsRef,
}
```

`RuntimeChromeBudget.wram_reserved` is restructured into `WramReserved { overlay, hot_arena_floor, total }` so preflight can subtract overlay independently from runtime-internal use.

`OverlayPlan` (T11.4) becomes the IR slot that consumes `WramLayoutPolicy.overlay_bytes` and produces a residents list.

### Decisions committed in 2026-04-26 session

- WRAM overlay is the **default** expert-kernel residency strategy.
- Overlay reload policy: **per expert switch**.
- WRAM split: **fixed overlay (per profile constant) + computed remainder via `ArenaPlan`**.
- Switch cap: **hard per-profile constraint**, not just a soft training prior.
- Named profiles: **`BringUp` only**; production profiles deferred until F12 settles.

### Confidence: HIGH for types, MEDIUM for BringUp constants

The type design is straightforward and tightly constrained by F2's existing budget contract. The concrete `BringUp` constants (overlay byte size, switch cap value) are starting hand-picked numbers that calibration (gbf-bench) and shadow_compile data will refine. Provenance must be recorded in T11.2's profile definition.

## Agent Instructions (Feature)

**This is a feature bead.** Implement via child tasks T11.1–T11.4.

**Target crate(s)**: `gbf-policy` (types + BringUp), `gbf-train` (preflight integration), `gbf-codegen` (OverlayPlan stub).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "OverlayPlan", "WRAM hot arena", "WRAM overlay", "CompileProfile", "max_bank_switches_per_token". Also see the 2026-04-26 session comment on bd-1hv.

### Feature Definition of Done

All child tasks (T11.1–T11.4) closed AND:
```bash
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- profile               # CompileProfile + BringUp tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- wram_layout            # WramLayoutPolicy round-trip tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight::wram         # WRAM preflight checks pass
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- overlay_plan          # OverlayPlan IR slot exists
```

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Session 2026-05-06: ModelSizeProfile interaction

F11 owns `CompileProfile` (deploy-time placement). The 2026-05-06 amendment introduces a sibling registry **`ModelSizeProfile`** (new feature F14, parent under bd-1rb, lives in `gbf-policy` next to `CompileProfile`).

The two registries are orthogonal but cross-validate:
- `CompileProfile` constrains WRAM layout, switch cap, overlay reload.
- `ModelSizeProfile` constrains `(d_model, d_ff, n_blocks, n_experts)` for the model topology.

`ModelSizeProfile::Toy0` always pairs with `CompileProfile::BringUp`. Other pairings TBD as production profiles emerge.

T11.3 (Wire CompileProfile through CompileRequest + preflight) gains a new check: the active `CompileRequest` must reference a registered `(ModelSizeProfile, CompileProfile)` pair, and the model topology built from the profile must satisfy the deploy profile's expert-byte and switch-cap caps. Reject otherwise at preflight.

This does not block T11.1 / T11.2 / T11.3 from landing as written. The cross-validation is added in the F14 wiring task once both profile types exist.

title: T-B1.3.1: BringupRomWindowPlan + RomResidency + Bank0 used/free byte reporting
description: ROM window plan with named PermanentBank0 (no magic-number residency epoch) and SwitchableRom variants. Validates: kernel + table are PermanentBank0; A/B are SwitchableRom with distinct nonzero banks; lengths match N×N; table is 1024 bytes; Bank0 fits without overlapping reserved regions. Reports bank0_used_bytes/bank0_free_bytes.
close_reason: Implemented L2 cross-bank BankLease path per F-B1 RFC. Evidence: BringupRomWindowPlan records Bank0 resources plus A/B switchable banks; generated L2/L3 streaming ROMs acquire/release BankLease via gbf-runtime banking lowering instead of raw MBC writes; validator rejects raw MBC writes, leaked/unknown/duplicate leases, and yield while a lease is active; emulator gates f_b1_l2_cross_bank_matmul_matches_reference and f_b1_l3_streaming_rom_nonzero_offsets_match_reference_n32 pass; N=64 byte-exact cross-bank run is in docs/review/f-b1/artifacts/realism_report.v1.json from gbf-test f_b1_regen. ASM checklist: symbolic pre-layout BankLease ops lower through audited runtime banking; raw MBC writes are excluded by typed effect validation and tests.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.3.1: `BringupRomWindowPlan` + `RomResidency` + Bank0 used/free byte reporting

**Target crate**: `gbf-codegen`
**Target file**: `gbf-codegen/src/window.rs`

### Background

`BringupRomWindowPlan` records where each F-B1 ROM resource lives. It is deliberately **simpler than M1's RomWindowPlan** — no overlay, no residency epochs, no hot/cold ranking. M1 expands; F-B1 sets the load-bearing precedent that "Bank0 permanence is named, not encoded as a magic numeric epoch."

### Types

```rust
pub struct BringupRomWindowPlan {
    pub kernel: RomResidency,
    pub multiply_table: RomResidency,
    pub operand_a: RomResidency,
    pub operand_b: RomResidency,
}

pub enum RomResidency {
    PermanentBank0 { offset: u16, length: u16 },
    SwitchableRom  { bank: RomBankId, offset: u16, length: u16 },
}
```

### Why named `PermanentBank0` (not `ResidencyEpoch(0)`)

A magic-number sentinel ("0 means Bank-0-resident") would force every reader to know the convention. A named variant makes Bank0 permanence syntactically obvious. M1's full residency-epoch model can subsume `PermanentBank0` later by mapping it to a designated epoch.

### Validation rules

- `kernel` is `PermanentBank0`.
- `multiply_table` is `PermanentBank0`.
- A and B are `SwitchableRom`.
- A bank ≠ B bank.
- A and B banks ≠ 0.
- A length and B length match `N × N`.
- Multiply table length is exactly 1,024 bytes.
- Bank0 permanent resources fit alongside reserved vectors/header/runtime sections.
- Bank0 layout must be reportable: report `bank0_used_bytes` and `bank0_free_bytes`.

### Why Bank0 reporting matters

"Kernel too large for Bank0" is one of the things F-B1 wants to learn. If the kernel + table + runtime doesn't fit, that is a load-bearing finding for M1. Surfacing it in the report — not hiding it behind a switchable-bank kernel that breaks banking soundness — is the whole point.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_rom_window_plan_distinct_banks
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_rom_window_plan_bank0_resources
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_rom_window_plan_rejects_same_bank
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_rom_window_plan_rejects_bank0_operand
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_rom_window_plan_reports_bank0_used_free
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_rom_window_plan_rejects_bank0_overflow
```

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §7.5.

### Dependencies

- T-B1.2.2 (`RomBankId`).
- T-B1.0.4 (multiply table — needs to know its size).

title: T10.6a: Standard loss term gradient flow tests (z-loss, balance, range, zero)
description:
Per-loss-term gradient flow verification for the standard (non-temporal) loss terms:

**For each of: λ_balance, λ_zrouter, λ_range, λ_zero:**
- Create a tiny model + synthetic input where the term should produce non-zero loss
- Compute the loss term in isolation (all other λ=0)
- Backward pass
- Verify: gradient is non-zero on the expected parameters
- Verify: gradient is zero on parameters the term should NOT affect
- Log: term name, loss value, grad norm per parameter group

**Specific gradient flow tests:**
- λ_balance: gradient should flow only through router logits, NOT through expert weights
- λ_zrouter: gradient should flow only through router logits
- λ_range: gradient through activations at expert boundaries, proportional to distance outside safe range
- λ_zero: gradient through pre-threshold expert weights, proportional to absolute value (L1-style)

**Independence test:**
- Enable each term one at a time, verify total loss equals that term
- Enable all four terms, verify total loss equals sum (linearity)

**Numerical stability tests:**
- Very large router logits (>100): verify z-loss doesn't overflow
- Very small expert usage (one expert gets 99%): verify balance loss is finite and large
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- loss_tests passes; Each loss term has gradient flow test; No NaN/Inf on extreme inputs; Independence verified

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-test`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-test/tests/loss_tests.rs`

**Reference**: Bead descriptions for T5.2 (bd-1b3), T5.3 (bd-1p3), T5.4 (bd-11s).

### Implementation Notes
Per-loss-term gradient flow verification:
- **z-loss**: gradient w.r.t. router logits is non-zero, correct direction (reduces logit magnitude)
- **load-balance loss**: gradient encourages uniform routing
- **activation range penalty**: zero gradient within bound, non-zero outside
- **ternary zero regularizer**: gradient pushes weights toward 0 or ±scale

Also:
- **Independence test**: each loss term's gradient only affects its expected parameters
- **Numerical stability tests**: extreme inputs (very large/small values) don't produce NaN/Inf

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo test -p gbf-test -- loss_tests             # All pass
# Verify: each loss term has gradient flow test
# Verify: no NaN/Inf on extreme inputs
# Verify: independence — z-loss gradient doesn't affect weights, only router logits
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §III.2: Gradient flow tests are the unit-level verification for loss terms
- §I.4: Verifiable — gradient is non-zero (or zero) as expected, exit 0/1

title: T12.3: Implement BoundedKv sequence block under SequenceSemanticsSpec

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-model/src/sequence/bounded_kv.rs` (new).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search "BoundedKv", "bounded-KV causal", "SramPagePlan". Also F12 (bd-144) and T12.1 (bd-ctl).

### Implementation Notes

Implement a `BoundedKvBlock` that satisfies `SequenceBlock`:

- **State shape**: KV slab sized by `(max_context, kv_bytes_per_token)`. Grows with token position up to `max_context`; bounded above.
- **Update rule**: causal attention with bounded KV (sliding-window or fixed-context truncation). Per-token compute: O(context).
- **Deploy memory**: KV slab is paged through SRAM via `SramPagePlan` (planv0.md compiler-pipeline section 7). Hot tiles get copied into WRAM, worked on there, written back at commit boundaries. This block must not assume the whole KV fits in WRAM.
- **Numerics**: same QAT compatibility as LinearState. Use F1 QAT modules at expert input/output, residual boundaries, and deployable norm.
- **Export facts**: populate `SequenceExportFacts` with `bytes_per_token = kv_bytes_per_token`, `bytes_per_layer = max_context * kv_bytes_per_token`, and the SequenceSemanticsSpec::BoundedKv variant.

### Loss / phase compatibility

The same loss terms (z-loss, balance, range, switch, distillation) and phase scheduler must work without code branching outside the block dispatch — this is the F12 contract. If a loss term needs sequence-state-specific behavior, it goes through a `SequenceBlock`-trait method, not a match statement on the variant.

### What "good enough for v1" looks like

- Tiny tests instantiate it with `max_context=32, kv_bytes_per_token=8`.
- Gradient flows through bounded attention (smoke test).
- ExportVisitor (T1.6) produces CanonicalTensors for its parameters.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                                   # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::bounded_kv             # Forward + grad smoke tests
cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence_block::bounded_kv       # Implements SequenceBlock trait
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings                    # No warnings
# Verify: state_size().bytes_per_token > 0 (state grows with tokens)
# Verify: state_size().bytes_per_layer == max_context * kv_bytes_per_token
# Verify: A tiny BoundedKvBlock instantiates under the same TrainPhaseSpec config as LinearState
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: Implementing `SequenceBlock` enforces parity with LinearState at the type level.
- §III.1: Compile error if BoundedKv accidentally bypasses the SequenceBlock trait.
- §VI.1: Block lives in `gbf-model::sequence::bounded_kv`.

title: T3.1: Define TernaryWeightPlan types in gbf-artifact
description: Define TernaryWeightPlan, ScaleGranularity (PerTensor/PerOutputRow/PerGroup(u16)), ScaleFormat (Q8_8/Q4_4/Pow2), ThresholdPlan (FixedQ8_8/AnnealedGlobalThenPerOutputRow/LearnedPerGroup(u16)) in gbf-artifact as part of the QuantSpec extension. These are pure data types with serde support. Include a compute_byte_cost(&self, rows: u32, cols: u32) -> ByteCost method.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-artifact exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- weight_plan passes; compute_byte_cost returns correct values for PerTensor, PerOutputRow, PerGroup
close_reason: TernaryWeightPlan, scale/threshold plans, weight encodings, and byte-cost tests implemented in gbf-artifact.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-artifact`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-artifact/src/weight_plan.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "TernaryWeightPlan", "ScaleGranularity", "ScaleFormat", "ThresholdPlan", "compute_byte_cost".

### Implementation Notes
Define these types:
```rust
pub struct TernaryWeightPlan {
    pub encoding: WeightEncoding,
    pub scale_granularity: ScaleGranularity,
    pub scale_format: ScaleFormat,
    pub threshold: ThresholdPlan,
}

pub enum ScaleGranularity { PerTensor, PerOutputRow, PerGroup(u16) }
pub enum ScaleFormat { Q8_8, Q4_4, Pow2 }
pub enum ThresholdPlan { FixedQ8_8, AnnealedGlobalThenPerOutputRow, LearnedPerGroup(u16) }
```

Key method:
```rust
impl TernaryWeightPlan {
    pub fn compute_byte_cost(&self, rows: u32, cols: u32) -> ByteCost { ... }
}
```

Byte math (from the epic comment):
- Ternary2 base: `ceil(rows * cols / 4)` bytes (2 bits per weight)
- Scale overhead depends on granularity:
  - PerTensor: 2 bytes (one Q8_8 scale)
  - PerOutputRow: `rows * size_of(scale_format)` bytes
  - PerGroup(g): `ceil(rows * cols / g) * size_of(scale_format)` bytes
- Total = ternary_bytes + scale_bytes

Derive: `Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize`

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-artifact                         # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- weight_plan            # Unit tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-artifact -- -D warnings          # No warnings
# Verify: compute_byte_cost returns correct values for known inputs
# Test: PerOutputRow with 64 rows, 128 cols => ternary=2048, scales=128 (Q8_8=2 bytes each -> 64*2=128)
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: ScaleGranularity/ScaleFormat/ThresholdPlan as enums — no stringly-typed configs
- §III.2: Property tests for byte cost monotonicity (more rows/cols => more bytes)

title: T-A6.1: gbf-store — content-addressed blob store + integrity
description: Source of truth: RFC history/rfcs/F-A6-gbf-store-migrate.md §§4-6. Implement T-A6.0 foundation BlobRef/BlobCodec plus gbf-store::blob and gbf-store::integrity. Required APIs include BlobStore::open/put/put_expect/put_streaming/put_as/get/get_streaming/get_ref/exists/remove/list_blobs/path_for/cleanup_tmp, DurabilityMode, BlobStoreError, verify_integrity, verify_all, verify_reachable, and IntegrityReport. Use atomic tmp-to-canonical writes, verifying idempotence, typed errors, and blobs/sha256/<ab>/<hash> layout.
close_reason: Completed F-A6.1: populated BlobRef/BlobCodec, implemented BlobStore atomic/content-addressed APIs and integrity verification. Gates: cargo test -p gbf-foundation -p gbf-store; cargo clippy --workspace --all-features -- -D warnings; cargo test --workspace --all-features.

comments.author: bkase
bkase
comments.text:
## Moved acceptance owner from bd-22o

`bd-22o` now proves byte-stable JSON serde for the in-memory `ExportedQatArtifact` boundary. Directory/CAS transport for an `ExportedQatArtifact` or its successor artifact envelope is owned here, together with the store/archive layer: write artifact bytes into the `blobs/sha256` layout, load them through the artifact/store loader, re-export them, and compare the resulting directory blobs byte-for-byte.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
**Source-of-truth update — see RFC F-A6** (2026-05-03)

The 2026-04-26 description above is a sketch; **RFC `history/rfcs/F-A6-gbf-store-migrate.md` is the authoritative spec** for this task. Where the sketch and the RFC disagree, the RFC wins.

### Authoritative shape (RFC §§ 4–6)

**`gbf-foundation::blob` (precondition T-A6.0, folded into this PR's first commit):**
- `BlobRef { hash: Hash256, len: u32, codec: BlobCodec }`
- `BlobCodec { Raw, Zstd }` with `#[serde(rename_all = "snake_case")]`
- both `Copy + Clone + Debug + Eq + PartialEq + Hash + Serialize + Deserialize`
- re-exported from `gbf-foundation/src/lib.rs`

**`gbf-store::blob`:**
- `BlobStore` + `BlobStore::open`
- `put(&self, bytes: &[u8]) -> Result<Hash256, BlobStoreError>` — atomic, content-addressed-idempotent (with verification of any pre-existing canonical file)
- `put_expect(expected, bytes)` — verifying form for archive extraction
- `put_streaming` for blobs above an inline limit
- `put_as(&self, bytes, codec) -> Result<BlobRef, ...>` — convenience that tags the codec
- `get`, `get_streaming`, `get_ref(BlobRef)`, `exists`, `remove`, `list_blobs`, `path_for`, `cleanup_tmp`
- `DurabilityMode { ReadAfterReturn, Full }` — distinguishes single-process crash safety from full power-loss directory-entry durability
- `BlobStoreError` enum carrying `LenMismatch`, `HashMismatch`, `BlobTooLarge`, `ExistingBlobCorrupt`, `NotFound`, `Io`
- atomic write protocol: `tmp/<random>` → fsync(file) → rename; `open()` does **not** delete tmp files (another process may be mid-write); `cleanup_tmp(max_age)` is the explicit cleanup operation
- canonical layout: `blobs/sha256/<ab>/<hash>` where `<ab>` is the first two hex chars of the hash (lowercase)

**`gbf-store::integrity`:**
- `verify_integrity(store, hash) -> Result<(), IntegrityError>`
- `verify_all(store) -> Result<IntegrityReport, IntegrityError>`
- `verify_reachable(store, roots, refs)`
- `IntegrityReport { blobs_checked: u64, mismatches: Vec<Hash256>, missing: Vec<Hash256> }`

**Cargo.toml cleanup (in scope for this PR):**
- Remove `gbf-artifact = { path = "../gbf-artifact" }` from `gbf-store/Cargo.toml`
- Add `sha2.workspace = true` and `tempfile.workspace = true` (production deps)
- Move `serde_json.workspace = true` to `[dev-dependencies]`

**Crate root:**
- Add `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` to `gbf-store/src/lib.rs`

### Test surface (RFC §§ 5.5, 6.3, 19)

See RFC §§ 5.5 and 6.3 for the full test list (round-trip, atomic-write crash safety, idempotence, two-char prefix layout, `put_expect` hash mismatch, `LenMismatch`, integrity corruption detection, `verify_all`/`verify_reachable`, JSON round-trip of `IntegrityReport`).

### Resolved questions impacting this task

RFC §17 items 1, 2, 4, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23, 24, 25 govern this task. Notably:
- 19: existing canonical files are re-hashed before being treated as a hit; corrupt files surface as `BlobStoreError::ExistingBlobCorrupt`.
- 21: `Result<_, BlobStoreError>`, **not** `io::Result<_>`.
- 22: `DurabilityMode` is two-valued; default is `ReadAfterReturn`.
- 24: `fs::rename` `AlreadyExists` is treated as success only after re-hashing.

title: Epic D: Runtime Beyond M0 — Persistence, Harness, Trace, Drift, Fault, SchedulePack
description: Runtime modules beyond the M0 reference shell: persistence (versioned SRAM record protocol), full harness control plane (StepSlice/RunUntilCheckpoint/DumpArena/InjectFault/PowerCut), trace pipeline, RuntimeDriftMonitor with automatic mode demotion, fault policy table + recovery + BootValidationPlan, SchedulePack mode switching. F-D6 multi-mode cadence is BLOCKED on oracle question.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Epic D: Runtime Beyond M0

### What this is

The runtime modules **not** in the M0 reference shell: persistence, harness control plane, trace pipeline, drift monitor, fault policy, and `SchedulePack` mode switching. These are the modules that turn the M0 cooperative skeleton into a production runtime.

### Why this matters

M0 (Epic A, F-A5) ships a runtime nucleus that boots and yields. M1 oracles + M2 first ROM run on it. But long-running interactive use needs:
- **Persistence** (so save state survives a power-cut).
- **Harness** (so tests can `StepSlice` / `RunUntilCheckpoint` / `InjectFault` / `PowerCut` from the host).
- **Trace** (so observability is structured and bounded).
- **Drift monitor** (so the runtime can demote modes under unexpected load).
- **Fault policy** (so faults trigger graded recovery rather than freezing).
- **SchedulePack mode switching** (so a build can carry multiple `RuntimeMode`s and switch between them at safe boundaries).

Per the F2 reference shell decision (T2.4 `bd-37r`), Persistence / Trace / Harness / Panic each have *reserved slack* in the chrome budget but are NOT in the pinned reference shell. When this epic's features land, the reference shell must be re-evaluated.

### Scope (six features)

- **F-D1 — Persistence**: `PersistHeader`, `PersistGroupCommit`, `PersistKind`, `PageState`, double-buffered SRAM record protocol with explicit commit boundaries. Per `planv0.md` lines 1718–1788.
- **F-D2 — Harness Control Plane**: full implementation of `HarnessOp` set (`StepSlice`, `RunUntilCheckpoint`, `DumpArena`, `InjectFault`, `PowerCut`, `SetSession`, `GetState`).
- **F-D3 — Trace Pipeline**: trace pages in SRAM, ring-buffer drain, host-side parser, `TraceBudget` enforcement, `trace_loss_report.json`.
- **F-D4 — Drift Monitor**: `RuntimeDriftMonitor` + `DriftTrigger` + `DriftAction` (`ShrinkSlices`/`DropTrace`/`DemoteMode`).
- **F-D5 — Fault Policy Table + Recovery**: per-`FaultDomain` action mapping + `BootValidationPlan` runner + cold-start logic.
- **F-D6 — SchedulePack Mode Switching**: runtime side of `SchedulePack` — `ModeSwitchPolicy`, legal-switch-point gating, `Safe` mode, `SafeModeTrigger`s.

### Open question for the oracle (the second one we drafted)

How many `RuntimeMode`s should `SchedulePack` carry across milestones? Single-mode in v1 (just `Steady`)? Two-mode (`Steady` + `Debug`)? Three (`Interactive` + `Steady` + `Trace`)? Plus `Safe` as a fallback. F-D6 sizing depends on the answer.

### Critical path

```
F-A5 (Bank0 nucleus) ──→ all of F-D1..F-D6
F-A3 (gbf-abi types) ──→ F-D1 (PersistKind), F-D2 (HarnessOp), F-D3 (TraceEvent), F-D4 (DriftAction), F-D5 (FaultPolicy)
F-B11 (GbSchedIR) ────→ F-D6 (SchedulePack consumer)
```

### Confidence: HIGH on F-D1/F-D2/F-D3/F-D5, MEDIUM on F-D4/F-D6

Drift monitor + mode switching depend on production workload data we don't have yet. F-D6 specifically blocked on the SchedulePack-mode oracle question.

title: F16: Multi-Corpus Training Data Preparation — TinyStories → Gutenberg → enwiki8 progression
description: Per-corpus training data preparation for the dense baseline (F13) and v0_success acceptance gate (F-C4). Three corpora staged in increasing difficulty: TinyStories (~100MB simple narrative — easy first dataset for sanity checks), Project Gutenberg (canonical English public-domain text — medium standard benchmark), enwiki8 (100MB Wikipedia char-level — the canonical char-level LM benchmark). Each corpus integration delivers: deterministic download/license-checked manifest via F-G1 CorpusManifest, normalization through F-G2 charset_v1 with unmappable-rate validation, deterministic train/val/test splits, and a 5-gram Kneser-Ney baseline scored on held-out for the v0_success bpc gate. Cross-corpus contamination check ensures no leakage. Dense baseline (F13) and any MoE run train against the active corpus + are scored against that corpus's n-gram baseline.

## F-E6: Constrained autotune

### What this is

Explicit knob set (tile sizes, kernel residency, slice ceilings) per `planv0.md` line 318. Pareto frontier across `CompileObjective` axes. Selection by objective + risk policy.

The plan limits autotune to a small explicit knob surface to keep tuning honest. Random hyperparameter sweeps are excluded.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct AutotuneKnobs {

    pub residency_choices: BTreeMap<KernelInstanceId, Vec<KernelResidency>>,
    pub slice_ceiling_choices: Vec<u32>,
}

pub fn run_autotune(knobs: &AutotuneKnobs, objective: &CompileObjective)
    -> ParetoFrontier;
```

- F-B14 FeasibilityRefinementLoop (overlaps; autotune feeds CompileKnobs)

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A3.6: `SemanticCheckpointId` + `CompactCheckpointId` + `SemanticCheckpointSchema`

**Target crate**: `gbf-abi`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-abi/src/checkpoint.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 271, line 1209 (ObservationPlan consumes SemanticCheckpointSchema), engineering rule 7 ("harness uses SemanticCheckpointId, not magic addresses").

### Background

`SemanticCheckpointId` is the durable id used across the harness, oracles, runtime, and reports to refer to a logical checkpoint in the model's execution (e.g., "after layer 3 router decision"). `CompactCheckpointId` is the smaller fixed-width id used in trace events and InferenceState for space efficiency. `SemanticCheckpointSchema` is the exported mapping between them (`planv0.md` line 1240).

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct SemanticCheckpointId(pub Cow<'static, str>);

#[repr(transparent)]
pub struct CompactCheckpointId(pub u16);

pub struct SemanticCheckpointSchema {
    pub schema_version: u16,
    pub checkpoints: Vec<CheckpointEntry>,
}

pub struct CheckpointEntry {
    pub semantic: SemanticCheckpointId,        // e.g. "layer.3.router.post_top1"
    pub compact: CompactCheckpointId,
    pub stratum: SemanticStratum,              // Denotation | Artifact | Operational
    pub source_op: Option<&'static str>,
}

pub enum SemanticStratum {
    Denotation,
    Artifact,
    Operational,
}
```

The schema is exported by the build (it's part of `BuildReports` per `planv0.md` line 1577 — `semantic_checkpoint_schema.json`). The harness, oracles, and runtime read it to translate between compact and semantic ids.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-abi
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- checkpoint::serde_round_trip
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- checkpoint::compact_id_unique           # Schema validation: no two entries share a compact id
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- checkpoint::stratum_exhaustive          # All 3 strata legal
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: SemanticStratum is exhaustive; matches the three-stratum framing in planv0.md.
- §VI.1: One schema type, used by oracles + harness + runtime + reports.
