comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-D3: Trace Pipeline

### What this is

`TraceEvent` ring buffer in SRAM, drain-to-host channel, `TraceBudget` enforcement, `trace_loss_report.json`, `trace_perturbation_report.json`. ROM emits structured trace events; host harness drains them.

### Why this matters

Observability per `§V.1`. Without structured traces, debugging a long inference run on cartridge means cycle-counter staring.

### Design sketch

`gbf-runtime/src/trace.rs` (currently a stub). Lock-free ring buffer (single-producer, single-consumer) in SRAM. Drain via harness's `DumpArena`. Drop policy honored under budget pressure. Perturbation reports under `ObservabilityMode::Invariant` builds.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A3.7 TraceEvent + TraceBudget
- F-D2 harness (drain channel)
- F-F1 build reports (trace_loss_report.json, trace_perturbation_report.json)

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T3.3: Export scale tensors as first-class CanonicalTensors
description: Scale tensors (per-row or per-group) must be exported as named CanonicalTensors in ArtifactCore, not silently baked into packed representations or left as float metadata. The ExportVisitor (T1.6) must emit: one ternary weight CanonicalTensor + one scale CanonicalTensor per projection. Scale tensors themselves are fake-quantized during training (to the declared ScaleFormat) and exported in that quantized form.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export passes; Separate CanonicalTensor for weights and scales; Deterministic tensor naming
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

- Artifact contract: ternary scale data is exported as a named `CanonicalTensor` with `CanonicalTensorKind::TernaryScale`, `TensorElementType::Q8_8`, deterministic `<projection>.scale` identity, and a `TernaryQuantEntry::scale` reference from the projection. `ArtifactCore::new` validates scale kind, element type, and shape against the quant plan.
- Differentiable Burn path: no new Burn path in this bead. Current scale state is owned by `TernaryLinearQat` and Burn adapter tests already prove export-from-trained-state for the supported Q8_8 path. Non-Q8_8 scale tensor formats are moved to `bd-38om`.
- Tests proving it: `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export`, `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core`, `cargo check -p gbf-model`, `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings`, `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`, `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`.
- Acceptance movement: bd-g90 already introduced the basic separate scale tensor export. bd-209 adds the stronger regression oracle requested by its acceptance: deterministic scale naming, raw Q8_8 scale payload checks, and reconstruction of projected quantized weights from exported weight plus scale tensors. Non-Q8_8 ScaleFormat encodings are rejected today and moved to `bd-38om`.

## Review Feedback Disposition

- Intent review: agreed that bd-209 is now a proof/regression bead because bd-g90 introduced the base ExportVisitor behavior. Closure names bd-g90 as prior owner and bd-209 as the stronger oracle.
- Intent/design/evidence review: agreed that the task wording over-implied all `ScaleFormat` values. Added `artifact_core_rejects_declared_non_q8_8_scale_formats_until_tensor_encoding_exists` and opened `bd-38om` for Q4_4/Pow2 tensor encodings.
- Semantic review: no code findings. It confirmed the current visitor emits separate `projection.weight` and `projection.scale` tensors and artifact validation checks kind/type/shape.
- Evidence review: agreed evidence only covers current per-output-row Q8_8 export. Closure marks that as supported today and moves non-Q8_8 support to `bd-38om`.

## Feedback Harness Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| bd-209 duplicated bd-g90's base export claim | The ready bead lagged behind implementation introduced by an earlier bead | Added QAT closure guide: proof beads must name the earlier owner and add a stronger regression oracle | `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` now reconstructs projected weights from exported tensors | `.agents/skills/qat-bead-closure/SKILL.md`; `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` |
| Non-Q8_8 scale formats could be implied without tensor support | Public `ScaleFormat` variants outpaced `TensorElementType` encodings | Added QAT closure guide: declared quant plan must match artifact tensor encoding, reject unsupported variants | `artifact_core_rejects_declared_non_q8_8_scale_formats_until_tensor_encoding_exists` | `.agents/skills/qat-bead-closure/SKILL.md`; `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core` |
| Round-trip oracle was vague | Acceptance did not define what reconstruction must match | Closure narrows oracle to current Q8_8 path: exported weight + scale tensors reconstruct `export_canonical().projected_weights()` | Direct reconstruction assertion in `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` |
| Training fake-quant wording exceeded export target files | The bead mixed export representation with training scale schedule/format support | Mark non-Q8_8 training/export format support moved to `bd-38om`; current Burn path remains existing Q8_8 export-from-trained-state | No new Burn sensor in bd-209; existing hook runs Burn adapter suite | `bd-38om`; `cargo test --workspace --all-features` hook |

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Per-output-row Q8_8 ternary scales | supported | supported by existing Burn adapter export tests | supported as first-class `CanonicalTensor` | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export`; hook `cargo test --workspace --all-features` |
| Deterministic `<projection>.scale` naming | supported through `ArtifactPath::join("scale")` | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` |
| Reconstruct projected weights from exported tensors | supported for Q8_8 per-row | not applicable | supported in export regression test | `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` |
| Q4_4/Pow2 scale formats | rejected/moved | moved to `bd-38om` | rejected/moved to `bd-38om` | `artifact_core_rejects_declared_non_q8_8_scale_formats_until_tensor_encoding_exists`; `bd-38om` |
| Per-group Q8_8 artifact shape validation | not produced by current scalar exporter | moved to future non-default plan support | schema validation supported if supplied directly | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core` |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Export emits separate named weight and scale tensors per ternary projection | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` | none | Checks `projection.weight` and `projection.scale` as separate canonical tensors |
| Scale tensor stores quantized Q8_8 payload, not float metadata | `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` | none | Checks raw Q8_8 scale payload and element type |
| Exported weight plus scale tensors reconstruct deployed quantized projection | `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` | none | Reconstructs projected weights from exported tensors and compares to `export_canonical()` |
| ArtifactCore rejects unsupported declared scale formats | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core` | none | Rejects Q4_4/Pow2 until `bd-38om` adds encodings |
| gbf-model remains Burn-free and QAT backend seams stay contained | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Architecture invariant still passes |
| QAT closure/harness rules stay satisfied | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py` | none | Harness passed before closure and will be rerun after sync |

## No-Future Variant Rule

bd-209 accepts and proves only the current per-output-row Q8_8 scale export path. Non-Q8_8 scale formats are rejected at the artifact boundary today and owned by `bd-38om`. Per-group production export is not claimed by this bead.

- §IV.3: Byte-identical output for same weights — naming is part of the identity

Non-Q8_8 ScaleFormat encodings are rejected today and moved to `bd-38om`.

## Review Feedback Disposition

- Intent review: agreed that bd-209 is now a proof/regression bead because bd-g90 introduced the base ExportVisitor behavior. Closure names bd-g90 as prior owner and bd-209 as the stronger oracle.
- Intent/design/evidence review: agreed that the task wording over-implied all `ScaleFormat` values. Added `artifact_core_rejects_declared_non_q8_8_scale_formats_until_tensor_encoding_exists` and opened `bd-38om` for Q4_4/Pow2 tensor encodings.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-E3: KernelCalibrationBundle production

### What this is

Per-kernel measured profiles: `MeasuredKernelProfile` per `KernelSpecId` and configuration (tile shape, residency). Tied to `kernel_impl_hash` + `runtime_nucleus_hash` so changes invalidate.

### Why this matters

Kernel-level cost dominates inference time. Without measured profiles, `ScheduleCostAnalysis` can't claim `EvidenceClass::Measured` for any kernel; everything is `Heuristic` and `RiskPolicy` rejects.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub fn measure_kernels(kernels: &KernelRegistry, target: &TargetProfile, runtime_nucleus_hash: Hash256)
    -> KernelCalibrationBundle;
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-H1, F-H2 KernelRegistry + impls
- F-E2 platform calibration (correlates)

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T10.3: QAT module unit tests (F1 coverage)
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for all QAT modules in gbf-model/src/qat/:

**TernaryLinearQat tests:**
- forward() with known 4x4 weight matrix: verify fake-quant output matches manual ternary projection + scale multiply
- backward() gradient flow: verify STE gradient is non-zero for pre-threshold weights, zero where expected
- Hardness transitions: Off -> forward is identity; Soft -> output has temperature-dependent noise; Hard -> output is exact ternary projection
- export_canonical() round-trip: export -> unpack ternary bytes -> recompute forward -> must match fake_quant_forward() to within float rounding
- Per-row scale export: verify exported scale tensor matches the learned scales in Q8_8 format
- Threshold annealing: verify threshold values change correctly across training steps with known annealing schedule

**ActFakeQuant tests:**
- Clipping: verify values outside range are clipped, inside are passed through
- Gradient: verify gradient is 1.0 inside range, 0.0 outside (straight-through estimator)
- Clip range export: verify exported clip bounds match the module's current range

**NormApproxQat tests:**
- AffineClipLut: verify output matches reference RMSNorm -> affine -> clip -> LUT lookup
- TileRmsThenAffineClip: verify tiled computation matches full computation within declared tolerance
- LUT content export: verify exported LUT entries match the module's computed LUT

**Top1RouterQat tests:**
- Top-1 selection: verify argmax of routing logits matches selected expert
- z-loss computation: verify against manual formula on known logits
- Low-rank factorization: verify d_model->rank->n_experts output ≈ full-rank d_model->n_experts within expected error
- Expert dropout: verify dropout mask is applied during training, removed during eval

**ExpertBlockQat tests:**
- Full forward: tiny 8x16x8 expert block, verify output shape and non-trivial values
- Composition: verify ExpertBlockQat(x) = down(act(up(x))) + residual for known inputs

All tests log: module name, test case, expected vs actual, tolerance, pass/fail.
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- qat_tests passes; Every QAT module has >=3 test cases; Gradient flow verified; Export round-trip verified; Hardness transitions tested
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

**Artifact contract:** bd-21i adds gbf-test regression coverage for the existing pre-export QAT module contracts. It verifies TernaryLinearQat export_canonical reconstruction and NormApproxQat export params, but it does not claim ArtifactCore byte round trips, serialized artifact identity, ArtifactOracle agreement, or compiler/runtime lowering. Artifact materialization and agreement remain with bd-g90, bd-12c, and bd-22o.

**differentiable Burn path:** Covered for the existing gbf-train Burn adapters: TernaryLinearBurnQat, ActFakeQuantBurnQat, and NormApproxBurnQat. gbf-test now enables the gbf-train burn-adapter feature so these integration tests exercise real autodiff gradients. Router/expert Burn adapter gradient tests are moved to bd-1ptv, the existing live owner for Top1RouterQat and ExpertBlockQat Burn adapters.

**Tests proving it:** See the claim-to-gate matrix below.

**Acceptance moved:** The Off/Soft/Hard hardness API and threshold annealing acceptance from the original bead text are moved to bd-2uw. Artifact byte round trips and cross-backend export determinism are moved to bd-g90, bd-12c, and bd-22o. Router/expert Burn adapter gradients are moved to bd-1ptv. The standard batch/token router load-balance loss is not claimed here; bd-21i only pins the scalar Top1RouterQat token balance proxy, while the standard MoE objective remains with bd-1b3. Per-test structured log adoption is not claimed by this unit-test bead; structured logging adoption remains with bd-3mrj after the bd-3aq framework.

title: T-B16.2: Define CompileKnobOverrides + typed selectors
description: CompileKnobOverrides with disabled_optional_probes (semantic observations NEVER listed), forced_kernel_residency, forced_recompute, reduction_ceiling_overrides, tile_class_overrides. Typed selectors: KernelSelector, ValueSelector, ReductionSelector, TileSelector, SliceClass (Micro/Frame/TokenBoundary/TraceHeavy). Plus ReductionSiteId / SectionId. Selectors exist precisely so repair proposals do not smuggle untyped strings or pass-local indices. Monotone semantics on overrides documented on the type.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B14.2: Define `CompileKnobOverrides` + selector types

**Target crate**: `gbf-policy` (overrides + most selectors), `gbf-asm` (`SectionId`, `ReductionSiteId` if compiler-emitted)
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-policy/src/knobs/overrides.rs`, `gbf-policy/src/knobs/selectors.rs`
**Reference**: F-B14 oracle resolution; T-B14.1 for the parent type.

### Background

Global policy knobs are insufficient. Repair often needs to say "this specific value recomputes" or "this specific kernel must be overlay-resident." Targeted override maps with **typed selectors** carry those decisions. Selectors are typed precisely so proposals don't smuggle untyped strings or pass-local indices.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct CompileKnobOverrides {
    /// Optional operational probes disabled by trace demotion.
    /// Semantic observations are NEVER listed here.
    pub disabled_optional_probes: BTreeSet<TraceProbeId>,

    /// Per-kernel residency requirements inserted by RomWindowPlan/OverlayPlan repair.
    pub forced_kernel_residency: BTreeMap<KernelSelector, KernelResidency>,

    /// Pure values forced from Materialize to Recompute. Effect-free only.
    pub forced_recompute: BTreeSet<ValueSelector>,

    /// Site-specific range-plan ceilings.
    pub reduction_ceiling_overrides: BTreeMap<ReductionSelector, ReductionPlanCeiling>,

    /// Tile classes remaining legal for a selector after candidate narrowing.
    pub tile_class_overrides: BTreeMap<TileSelector, BTreeSet<TileCandidateClass>>,
}

pub enum KernelSelector {
    KernelSpec(KernelSpecId),
    LayerExpert { layer: LayerId, expert: ExpertId },
    Section(SectionId),
}

pub enum ValueSelector {
    Value(ValueId),
    AliasClass(AliasClassId),
}

pub enum ReductionSelector {
    Site(ReductionSiteId),
    Layer(LayerId),
}

pub enum TileSelector {
    Kernel(KernelSpecId),
    Layer(LayerId),
    SliceClass(SliceClass),
}

pub enum SliceClass {
    Micro,
    Frame,
    TokenBoundary,
    TraceHeavy,
}
```

`SectionId` (gbf-asm T-A1.2) and `ReductionSiteId` (new — emitted by RangePlan in F-B5; introduce here as a typed newtype `pub struct ReductionSiteId(pub u32)` if not already defined). `KernelSpecId` from F-H1; `ValueId` / `AliasClassId` / `LayerId` / `ExpertId` from gbf-foundation / gbf-ir.

### Monotone semantics on overrides

Repair may **insert** new selectors or move an existing selector to a **later value** in its declared order. It may not delete entries or move a selector backward.

For `tile_class_overrides`, monotone means **subset removal**: the new `BTreeSet` ⊆ the previous one. For `disabled_optional_probes`, monotone means **superset addition**.

These rules are implemented in T-B14.6 (loop controller), but document them on the type.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-policy
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- overrides::serde_round_trip
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- overrides::monotone_insert         # Inserting a selector at higher rank is allowed
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- overrides::monotone_no_delete      # Deletion is rejected
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- selectors::id_exhaustive           # SliceClass enum is closed
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: Selectors are typed; no untyped strings reach the repair loop.
- §VI.1: Selector vocabulary defined once in gbf-policy.

title: T10.13: Export determinism and cross-backend agreement test
description:
Tests that the export pipeline produces byte-identical artifacts for the same model weights.

**Determinism test:**
- Create a tiny model with fixed random seed
- Export via ExportVisitor -> ModelArtifact, record ArtifactCore content hash H1
- Reset model to same weights (reload from checkpoint)
- Export again -> record hash H2
- ASSERT: H1 == H2

**Serialization round-trip test:**
- Export ModelArtifact to directory form
- Read it back via artifact loader
- Re-export to directory form
- Verify byte-for-byte identical files in blobs/sha256/

**Canonical tensor determinism:**
- For each CanonicalTensor in ArtifactCore: serialize to bytes, hash, deserialize, re-serialize, verify hash unchanged
- Includes ternary weight tensors, scale tensors, embedding tensors, router weights

**Production packer vs independent reference packer:**
- Export tiny model via production ExportVisitor
- For each ternary weight tensor: repack using gbf-verify's independent reference packer
- ASSERT: byte-identical packed output for all tensors
- If ANY diverge: log tensor name, first divergent byte offset, hex context around divergence

**Scale tensor quantization determinism:**
- Export scale tensors in Q8_8 format
- Verify the quantized scale values match: round_to_nearest(float_scale * 256) as u16
- Verify no NaN or Inf values in exported scales

All failures log: artifact hash, tensor name, byte offset of divergence, hex dump.
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- export_determinism passes; Hash equality across runs; Serialization round-trip byte-identical; 100 random packer agreement tests
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

**Artifact contract:** This bead adds `gbf-test/tests/export_determinism.rs` for the current `ExportedQatArtifact` / `ArtifactCore` boundary. It proves deterministic tiny fixture export, stable serialized `ExportedQatArtifact` JSON bytes for equivalent exports, stable `ArtifactCore::semantic_hash()`, stable `CanonicalTensor::content_hash` after serde round-trip, and reference-packability of exported canonical ternary weight/scale tensors. It also adds a fixture helper so the tiny export traversal can be reused with alternate router/expert sources.

Directory-form artifact transport, content-addressed `blobs/sha256` loading, and production target packed bytes are not implemented by the current export boundary and are not claimed here.

**differentiable Burn path:** This bead does not add a new differentiable training path. It proves the supported Burn adapter handoff path for `Top1RouterBurnQat` and `ExpertBlockBurnQat`: converting the tiny router/expert block through Burn-owned trained state and then exporting through the scalar `ExportVisitor` yields byte-identical `ExportedQatArtifact` output for identical weights. Full train/checkpoint-to-export coverage remains owned by `bd-1wd`.

**Tests proving it:**

- `cargo test -p gbf-test --test export_determinism`
- `cargo test -p gbf-test -- export_determinism`
- `cargo test -p gbf-test --lib fixtures_make_tiny_artifact_is_valid_and_deterministic`
- `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`
- `cargo clippy -p gbf-test -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --check --all`
- `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`

**Acceptance movement:** Yes. Review found three original acceptance criteria ahead of the current repo surface:

- Original: export/read/re-export directory form and compare `blobs/sha256` files. New owner: `bd-1yd` for archive/directory transport and `bd-1jy` for the content-addressed blob store/integrity layer. Current replacement gate: JSON serde round-trip of `ExportedQatArtifact`.
- Original: compare production packed ternary bytes to the independent reference packer. New owner: `bd-1pc6`, clarified with a moved-acceptance comment, because `ExportVisitor` currently emits canonical `I8`/`U16` tensors and no production target materializer emits comparable packed bytes yet. Current replacement gate: exported canonical ternary tensors are reference-packable and unpack back to the exported payload.
- Original: full cross-backend train/checkpoint/export agreement. New owner: `bd-1wd`. Current replacement gate: supported Burn router/expert trained-state handoff produces byte-identical scalar export for identical tiny weights.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Deterministic tiny fixture export from identical scalar weights | supported | not applicable | supported | `export_determinism_tiny_model_reexports_identical_core_and_bytes` |
| Supported Burn router/expert trained-state handoff for identical tiny weights | supported | supported | supported through scalar `ExportVisitor` | `export_determinism_supported_burn_router_and_expert_handoff_matches_scalar_tiny_export` |
| Embedding/classifier/dense FFN Burn handoff | supported | moved | moved | `bd-1wd` |
| `ExportedQatArtifact` serde JSON round-trip | supported | not applicable | supported | `export_determinism_exported_qat_artifact_json_round_trip_is_byte_identical` |
| Artifact directory/CAS round-trip and `blobs/sha256` byte identity | not applicable | not applicable | moved | `bd-1yd`, `bd-1jy` |
| `CanonicalTensor` serde round-trip and content-hash stability | supported | not applicable | supported | `export_determinism_canonical_tensors_round_trip_and_hashes_are_stable` |
| Exported canonical ternary tensors reference-pack and unpack losslessly | supported | not applicable | supported | `export_determinism_canonical_ternary_tensors_are_reference_packable` |
| Production target packed ternary bytes vs independent reference bytes | not applicable | not applicable | moved | `bd-1pc6` |
| Independent Q8.8 scale formula for tiny exported expert scales | supported | not applicable | supported | `export_determinism_canonical_ternary_tensors_are_reference_packable` |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Same deterministic tiny model exported twice yields identical `ArtifactCore::semantic_hash()` and serialized `ExportedQatArtifact` bytes | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test export_determinism` | none | Covered by `export_determinism_tiny_model_reexports_identical_core_and_bytes` |
| Supported Burn router/expert trained-state handoff exports byte-identically to scalar tiny export for identical weights | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test export_determinism` | `gbf-test` depends on `gbf-train` with `burn-adapter` | This is a supported-adapter handoff test, not a full train/checkpoint backend test |
| `ExportedQatArtifact` serde JSON round-trip is byte-identical | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test export_determinism` | none | Directory/CAS artifact transport is moved to `bd-1yd`/`bd-1jy` |
| Every tiny export `CanonicalTensor` preserves serde bytes and reconstructed content hash | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test export_determinism` | none | Uses `CanonicalTensor::new` as the artifact invariant gate |
| Exported canonical ternary tensors are independently reference-packable and unpack back to canonical payloads | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test export_determinism` | none | Production target packed byte agreement is moved to `bd-1pc6` |
| Tiny exported expert Q8.8 scales match an independent row-threshold average formula and `gbf-verify` Q8.8 quantization | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test export_determinism` | none | The review-requested raw-scale self-comparison was replaced with an independent formula over source float rows |
| Tiny export fixture helper still preserves previous artifact determinism | `cargo test -p gbf-test --lib fixtures_make_tiny_artifact_is_valid_and_deterministic` | none | The helper removes duplicated traversal from the new integration test |
| QAT architecture and closure sensors remain clean | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`; `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py` | none | Run before closure |

## No-future

This bead accepts no future artifact transport, target packing, or backend-training variant as implemented. The current tests are scoped to the implemented `ExportedQatArtifact` JSON/semantic boundary, supported Burn router/expert handoff, and canonical ternary tensor reference packability. Directory/CAS artifact transport, production packed target bytes, cryptographic content-addressed hashes, and full train/checkpoint-to-export agreement remain moved to their named owner beads.

## Feedback Harness Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Directory/blob acceptance was ahead of APIs | Bead text mixed future store transport with current in-memory artifact export | State the current artifact boundary before claiming reproducibility; move directory/CAS claims to the store transport owner | Current test name says JSON round-trip; future directory round-trip must live in store tests | `bd-1yd`, `bd-1jy` |
| Production/reference packed-byte agreement was overclaimed | `ExportVisitor` emits canonical tensors, not production target bytes | Do not call reference-packability production byte agreement until a production materializer emits comparable bytes | Current test only asserts canonical tensors are reference-packable; future byte comparison belongs to the slow-reference cross-check owner | `bd-1pc6` |
| Scale test initially reused production raw values | Test oracle copied the same raw Q8.8 state export uses | Compute expected scales from independent row/threshold math, then quantize through `gbf-verify` | `export_determinism_canonical_ternary_tensors_are_reference_packable` | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test export_determinism` |
| Cross-backend wording was too broad | Only router/expert Burn adapters have current tiny export handoff coverage | Name the supported adapters explicitly; move full train/checkpoint export agreement | Narrowed test name and support matrix row | `bd-1wd` |
| New untracked test file could be missed by reviewers | Plain `git diff` omits untracked files | Before subagent review, make new files visible with intent-to-add or name them explicitly | Manual workflow note; no stable automated sensor yet | Review protocol |

title: T7.5: Implement low-rank router projection
description: Factor the router's d_model -> n_experts projection as two matrices: d_model -> rank and rank -> n_experts. Default rank = min(n_experts/4, 8). For d_model=128, n_experts=16, rank=4: saves ~1.5 KiB of common-bank space. The low-rank projection also acts as implicit regularization, preventing the router from memorizing token-expert assignments. Both matrices stay at higher precision (not ternary) since they're small and live in common banks.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router::lowrank passes; Output shape [batch, n_experts]; Full-rank fallback when None

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-model/src/qat/router.rs` (extend Top1RouterQat)

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "low-rank router", "d_model -> rank -> n_experts", "router projection".

### Implementation Notes
- Factor router's projection as: `d_model -> rank -> n_experts` (two smaller matrices instead of one large one).
- Default rank: `min(n_experts / 4, 8)`.
- Both projection matrices stay at higher precision (NOT ternary).
- `pub struct LowRankRouter { proj_down: Linear, proj_up: Linear }`
- Forward: `logits = proj_up(proj_down(x))` where proj_down: [d_model, rank], proj_up: [rank, n_experts].
- Config: `router_rank: Option<usize>` — None means full-rank fallback.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router::lowrank # Tests pass
# Verify: output shape is [batch, n_experts]
# Verify: rank < n_experts (otherwise pointless)
# Verify: full-rank fallback works when router_rank is None
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: Option<usize> for rank — None means full-rank, not a magic sentinel value
- §II.3: Minimal implementation — two Linear layers, nothing more

## REVISION PASS — Cross-cutting testing & logging discipline applied

This bead now blocks on one or more of the new discipline beads added in the audit pass. Specifically:

- **T-B1.0.8** — test fixture infrastructure (synthetic AsmIR, frame traces, expected outputs).
- **T-B1.0.9** — typed tracing event vocabulary + subscriber-level test capture (per AGENTS.md "Logging helper closure must cite subscriber-level capture for event shape").

### Required additions for this bead

1. **Logging discipline**: every event this bead emits or consumes uses T-B1.0.9's typed `FB1TraceEvent` enum, never raw stringly fields. Tests use `CapturedTrace::capture(...)` to assert event shape, not just count.

2. **Test fixtures**: this bead consumes synthetic fixtures from T-B1.0.8 instead of reinventing them. If this bead introduces a new synthetic fixture, it should be added to T-B1.0.8's catalogue with a one-line description in the README.

4. **`run_latest.log.summary.json` cross-reference**: if this bead's behavior is observable in the heavy-gate run (i.e., it emits typed events that the run-log captures), the summary's `tracing_event_counts` field gains an entry for those event types. The diff in that file across runs is the regression signal.

### What did not change

The bead's original specification, types, validation rules, and acceptance gates remain authoritative. This comment adds the discipline overlay; it does not replace anything.

title: T-B4.10: RuntimeChromeBudget binding + missing-budget hard reject
description:
## What
Bind `RuntimeChromeBudget` as a Stage 2 invocation input (NOT part of
CompileRequest). When absent, hard-reject with
`BudgetMissingRuntimeChromeBudget` and emit the only Stage-2 report shape
that allows `runtime_chrome_budget_hash = None`. Implement the
effective-cap arithmetic. See RFC §2.9, §2.13 (effective_cap math), §7.9.5.

## Why
RuntimeChromeBudget is emitted by the runtime-shell build, not the
compiler. It's a separate input from CompileRequest because compile
requests are cheap to vary across runtime-shell builds. Missing budget
is fail-closed because we'd otherwise fall into "synthesizing dummy
budgets" — exactly the shift-left antipattern this chunk avoids.

## Inputs
- T-B4.9 BudgetFailure taxonomy (`BudgetMissingRuntimeChromeBudget`,
  `BudgetWramPeakExceeds`, etc.).
- T-B2.18 bringup-*.chrome_budget.json fixtures.
- `gbf-policy::budget::RuntimeChromeBudget`.

## Outputs
- `pub struct CompileInvocationInputs { artifact_ref, compile_request, target_profile, runtime_chrome_budget: Option<RuntimeChromeBudget> }` (added to gbf-policy if not yet present).
- Stage 2 entry point handles `runtime_chrome_budget == None` by emitting
  a static_budget.json with `outcome=Failed`,
  `runtime_chrome_budget_hash = None`, `runtime_chrome_budget = None`,
  `decision.fits = false`, exactly one `BudgetMissingRuntimeChromeBudget`
  diagnostic.
- Effective cap arithmetic: `effective_cap_bytes: i64 = usable_bytes - reserved_slack`.
  No relaxation term (RFC §2.13).
- All operands widened to i64; do not saturate or wrap.

## Pitfalls
- The "no budget" report is the ONLY Stage-2 report shape allowed to set
  `runtime_chrome_budget_hash` to None — see RFC §7.7 invariant.
- DO NOT mutate the source `RuntimeChromeBudget` in flight. Bringup uses a
  *different* budget file (`bringup-*.chrome_budget.json`) per §2.13.
- Underflow: if `reserved_slack > usable_bytes`, the effective cap is
  negative and the relevant placement check fails. Don't saturate to 0.

## Acceptance
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_rejects_missing_runtime_chrome_budget
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_missing_runtime_chrome_budget_emits_failure_report_without_budget_hash
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_uses_reserved_slack_in_effective_cap

## Refs
- RFC §2.9, §2.13 effective_cap, §7.7 missing-budget invariant, §7.9.5 worked example.
- Depends on: T-B4.9, T-B2.18.
- Blocks: T-B4.11.

title: T-A8.7: Predicate persistence — closure-shaped vs stringified-source predicates
description: Two predicate kinds: closure-shaped (JS function reference for in-script breakpoint conditions; lives only for current invocation), stringified-source (text serialized into the session file; re-parsed on next invocation; survives session round-trips). add_breakpoint accepts both; the persistence decision is explicit, not inferred.
close_reason: Implemented in F-A8 gbf-debug PR; gates recorded in closure comments. Parent feature closes in this same PR.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## 2026-05-04 amendment — F-A7 predicate mapping

F-A7 has merged. F-A7's trap dispatcher exposes `Predicate::{Always, Closure(Box<TrapPredicate>), Source(String)}` (see `gbf-emu/src/trap.rs`), but `predicate_matches` returns `TrapPredicateError::SourceRequiresEvaluator` when asked to evaluate `Predicate::Source` directly — F-A7 cannot itself host a JS evaluator. F-A8 therefore uses this mapping (RFC §3.3.5):

- `gb.add_breakpoint(addr)` (no predicate): registers `Predicate::Always` with F-A7; persists as `PersistedPredicate::None`.
- `gb.add_breakpoint(addr, fn)` (closure): wraps the JS closure in a Rust `Box<TrapPredicate>` that calls back into QuickJS, registers as `Predicate::Closure`; **not** persisted (warning emitted).
- `gb.add_breakpoint(addr, "expr")` (string): wraps the source in a Rust closure that re-evaluates the expression in a read-only QuickJS scope on every fire, registers as `Predicate::Closure`; persists as `PersistedPredicate::StringifiedSource(s)`. F-A7's `Predicate::Source` exists for export-friendly persistence shape (see `PredicateSpec::Source`) but F-A8 does not register `Source` directly because F-A7 cannot evaluate it.

The read-only predicate scope is `{ regs, pc, access, cycle, view, symbol(name), symbolInBank(name, bank) }` — F-A7's `TrapContext` carries `regs/pc/access/cycle/view`; `symbol`/`symbolInBank` are F-A8 additions resolving through the in-session `SessionSymbolTable`. `TrapAction` is always `HaltAndReport` (F-A8 always wants the run loop to stop and surface the hit).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: Closed by F-A8 implementation. Source of truth: RFC §§3.1.5, 3.3.5. Implemented explicit predicate split: no predicate and stringified-source persist; closure predicates are invocation-local, warn with predicate_not_persisted, and never become unconditional persisted breakpoints. run_until_breakpoint now evaluates predicates before surfacing traps; closure predicates see the documented temporary predicate scope and read-only gb view. Gates: cargo test -p gbf-debug --test e2e string_predicate_round_trips_without_becoming_unconditional; cargo test -p gbf-debug --test e2e closure_predicate_is_invocation_local_and_warned; workspace clippy/test.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.0.9: Structured tracing event vocabulary + subscriber-level test capture infra

**Target paths**:
- `crates/gbf-runtime/src/trace/events.rs` (typed event vocabulary)
- `crates/gbf-runtime/src/trace/subscriber.rs` (test-time subscriber + assertion helpers)

### Background

AGENTS.md is explicit: **"Logging helper closure must cite subscriber-level capture for event shape and move real producer/dashboard adoption to a named owner bead when no executable producer exists."**

F-B1's runtime + emulator + harness emit many distinct events:

- **Banking**: `BankLeaseAcquired`, `BankLeaseReleased`, `MbcRegisterWrite`, `BankShadowUpdate`.
- **Yielding**: `YieldCheckpoint`, `LivenessProgressEpochAdvance`, `SchedulerResumed`.
- **Tile lifecycle**: `TileEntered`, `TileCompleteSafePoint`, `HarnessDumpRequested`, `HarnessResumeAcknowledged`.
- **Frame**: `VBlankFired`, `WidgetTickDispatched`, `SchedulerServicedFrame`, `YieldReturnedToScheduler`.
- **Codegen-time**: `ComputeBringupRequestImported`, `LowerToIrComplete`, `LowerToAsmIrComplete`, `RomEmitted`.
- **Validators**: `ValidatorAccepted`, `ValidatorRejected{rule, evidence}`.

Without a typed vocabulary, each producer invents its own field names; consumers (the test subscriber, the realism-report aggregator, the future M1 schedule oracle) end up doing string-matching. AGENTS.md prohibits this.

### Typed vocabulary

```rust
// gbf-runtime/src/trace/events.rs
#[derive(Debug, Clone, serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize)]
#[serde(tag = "kind")]
pub enum FB1TraceEvent {
    BankLeaseAcquired   { bank: u16, mcycle: u64, lease_id: u32 },
    BankLeaseReleased   { bank: u16, mcycle: u64, lease_id: u32 },
    MbcRegisterWrite    { register: u16, value: u8, mcycle: u64 },
    YieldCheckpoint     { mcycle: u64, quantum: YieldQuantumKind, products_completed: u32 },
    LivenessProgress    { mcycle: u64, progress_epoch: u32 },
    TileEntered         { mt: u16, nt: u16, mcycle: u64 },
    TileCompleteSafePt  { mt: u16, nt: u16, mcycle: u64, source_addr: u16 },
    HarnessDumpReq      { mcycle: u64, addr: u16, len: u16 },
    HarnessResumeAck    { mcycle: u64 },
    VBlankFired         { frame: u32, mcycle: u64 },
    WidgetTickDispatched{ frame: u32, mcycle: u64 },
    SchedulerServiced   { frame: u32, mcycle: u64 },
    YieldReturned       { frame: u32, mcycle: u64, remaining_frame_mcycles: u32 },
    ComputeReqImported  { request_hash: String },
    LowerToIrComplete   { ir_hash: String },
    LowerToAsmIrComplete{ asmir_hash: String },
    RomEmitted          { rom_sha256: String, bytes: u32 },
    ValidatorAccepted   { rule: &'static str, scope: String },
    ValidatorRejected   { rule: &'static str, scope: String, evidence: String },
}
```

These are the **structured fields** on `tracing` events; producers emit via:

```rust
tracing::info!(
    target = "gbf::fb1",
    event = "BankLeaseAcquired",
    bank = bank,
    mcycle = mcycle,
    lease_id = lease_id,
);
```

A converter walks captured events back into `FB1TraceEvent` for assertions.

### Test subscriber

```rust
// gbf-runtime/src/trace/subscriber.rs
pub struct CapturedTrace {
    events: Vec<FB1TraceEvent>,
}

impl CapturedTrace {
    pub fn capture<F: FnOnce()>(f: F) -> Self;
    pub fn count(&self, predicate: impl Fn(&FB1TraceEvent) -> bool) -> usize;
    pub fn first(&self, predicate: impl Fn(&FB1TraceEvent) -> bool) -> Option<&FB1TraceEvent>;
    pub fn ordered_pairs(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = (&FB1TraceEvent, &FB1TraceEvent)>;
    pub fn assert_lease_acquire_release_balance(&self) -> Result<(), TraceAssertionError>;
    pub fn assert_no_yield_while_lease_active(&self) -> Result<(), TraceAssertionError>;
    pub fn assert_widget_tick_per_gated_frame(&self, gated: Range<u32>) -> Result<(), TraceAssertionError>;
}
```

The capture helper installs a `tracing::Dispatch` for the duration of the closure, decodes structured events, and returns them.

### Why this is load-bearing

Every L2..L5 test in F-B1 needs at least one of:

- "exactly N lease acquires happened"
- "no yield happened while a lease was held"
- "widget ticked once per gated frame"
- "the validator emitted ValidatorRejected with rule=X for fixture Y"

Doing this with stringy event field names creates 30+ places where a test could be silently wrong because it's matching the wrong field. Centralizing the vocabulary and the assertion helpers eliminates that whole class of bug.

### Logging discipline (per AGENTS.md)

- Each producer call site uses the typed event helper, never raw `tracing::info!("BankLease…")` strings.
- Subscriber-level capture in tests proves event *shape*, not just event *count*.
- The test that validates the validator-rejection event *includes* the reason string in the captured event and asserts it (subscriber-level capture for event shape).

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- trace::events_serde_round_trip
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- trace::subscriber_captures_emitted_event_shape
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- trace::subscriber_helpers_assert_lease_balance
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- trace::subscriber_helpers_detect_yield_under_lease
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- trace::subscriber_helpers_detect_missed_widget_tick
```

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §9.1 (frame events), §11.4 (banking correctness).
AGENTS.md (logging-bead guidance).

### Dependencies

- F-A3 (`TraceEvent` infrastructure — base channel).

### Forward links

Consumed by **all** T-B1.x test beads that capture events. Especially:
- T-B1.3.4 (no-yield-under-lease)
- T-B1.3.5 (lease metrics)
- T-B1.5.4 (frame events)
- T-B1.5.5 (frame trace ordering)
- T-B1.6.3 (aggregation)

title: T-B4.2: Per-expert payload byte math (Ternary2/Binary1/SparseTernaryBitplanes)
description:
## What
Implement per-expert payload byte math owned by F-B4 (NOT trusted from
the QuantGraph producer). Computes `payload_bytes = weight_bytes +
scale_bytes + metadata_bytes` from `rows`, `cols`, and `TernaryWeightPlan`
using checked u64 arithmetic. Absorbs `bd-w80` (T3.4 model-side byte math).
See RFC §7.6 byte-math formulas.

## Why
Trusting producer-supplied byte totals lets a buggy export propagate
silent miscalculations into placement. Stage 2's job is to be a validator,
not a data forwarder. Per the bd-w80 handoff, the full multi-expert
StaticBudgetReport lives here; bd-w80 just proved the model-owned single-
expert case.

## Inputs
- T-B4.1 ExpertProjection (rows, cols, metadata_bytes, plan).
- `gbf-artifact::TernaryWeightPlan` (`ScaleGranularity`, `ScaleFormat`).

## Outputs
- `fn expert_payload_bytes(expert: &ExpertProjection) -> Result<ByteBudget, BudgetMathError>`.
- Ternary2: `weight_bytes = ceil(rows * cols / 4)`.
- Binary1: `weight_bytes = ceil(rows * cols / 8)`.
- SparseTernaryBitplanes: `pos_bp + neg_bp + sparse_metadata_bytes`
  per `TernaryWeightPlan`'s sparse layout name.
- Scale bytes by `ScaleFormat`: `Q8_8 = 2`, `Q4_4 = 1`,
  `Pow2 = 1` (target-profile-overridable).
- Scale count by `ScaleGranularity`: `PerTensor = 1`, `PerOutputRow = rows`,
  `PerGroup(g) = ceil(rows / g)`.
- All math uses checked `u64`; report rejects values exceeding declared widths.
- Reuse bd-w80's tests as canonical.

## Pitfalls
- DO NOT use saturating or wrapping arithmetic. Overflow is a real failure
  mode and must be reported as `BudgetMathError::Overflow`, not silently truncated.
- `Pow2 scale_bytes` may be overridden by target profile — read from
  `TargetProfile`, not hardcoded.
- Scale count for `PerGroup(g)` rounds UP; don't truncate.

## Acceptance
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_computes_ternary2_payload_bytes_from_shape
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_computes_scale_bytes_by_granularity
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_rejects_overflow_in_byte_math (with very large rows/cols)

## Refs
- RFC §7.6 byte-math formulas.
- bd-w80 (absorbed; reuse its single-expert tests).
- planv0 model-side expert sizing line ~966.
- Depends on: T-B4.1.
- Blocks: T-B4.3, T-B4.4, T-B4.8.

title: T-B2.11: Stage 0 returns full diagnostic set in one pass
description:
## What
Wire Stage 0's class-1..10 implementations into a single dispatcher that
runs them in order, accumulates all diagnostics within a class before
moving on, and short-circuits only when continuing would dereference
unverified state. See RFC §7.3 ordering rule.

## Why
A single-class-per-pass design forces builds to fail-iterate-fail-iterate.
Reviewers benefit from seeing the full diagnostic set in one report —
then they can fix all the things at once. The ordering matters because
some classes (e.g. payload validation) are unsafe to run if an earlier
class (semantic core hash) fails.

## Inputs
- T-B2.5..T-B2.10 (all class implementations).
- T-B2.4 (ValidatedInputs scaffolding, ValidationProduct/Failure).

## Outputs
- The `validate_artifact_and_request` entry point as the single pass.
- Fixed ordering 1 → 10.
- Within a class, accumulate all diagnostics; emit them in the report.
- Short-circuit only when an unverified value would be dereferenced
  (e.g. don't validate aux sidecars if manifest hash is wrong).
- Successful return: `ValidationProduct` with `outcome=Passed`,
  empty diagnostics.
- Failed return: `ValidationStageFailure` with `outcome=Failed`, ≥1 Hard
  diagnostic, and as much identity recorded as possible.

## Pitfalls
- DO NOT short-circuit at the first error within a class. Accumulate.
- DO short-circuit between classes when continuing is unsafe.
- The class ordering is fixed; do not reorder for performance.
- The first run must produce *all* its diagnostics; the StageCache replay
  on identical inputs preserves them (RFC §2.6).

## Acceptance
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_validate_returns_all_diagnostics_in_one_pass
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_validate_short_circuits_when_continuing_is_unsafe
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_validate_emits_failure_report_for_bad_schema
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_validate_accepts_canonical_fixture (happy path)

## Refs
- RFC §7.3 ordering rule.
- §7.9 worked examples 7.9.1, 7.9.2.
- Depends on: T-B2.5, T-B2.6, T-B2.7, T-B2.8, T-B2.9, T-B2.10.
- Blocks: T-B2.20, T-B2.21.

title: T2.3: Wire RuntimeChromeBudget into gbf-train preflight
description: Update gbf-train preflight to accept both DeployabilityEnvelope (target-family, static) and RuntimeChromeBudget (build-specific, dynamic). Expert payload sizes are checked against min(expert_slot.usable_bytes - expert_slot.reserved_slack) across ExpertBank slots. Bank0Free slot capacity informs whether shared kernels can be co-resident. Emit clear diagnostic: 'Expert FFN up+down for d_model=128 d_ff=224 Ternary2 PerOutputRow Q8_8 = 7424 bytes; ExpertBank slot = 15872 bytes usable, 384 reserved -> 15488 available. PASS with 8064 bytes margin.'
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight passes; Oversized experts return fits_envelope=false; Falls back to DeployabilityEnvelope when no budget

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/preflight.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "preflight", "DeployabilityEnvelope", "RuntimeChromeBudget", "expert payload sizes".

### Implementation Notes
- Update gbf-train preflight to accept BOTH:
  1. `DeployabilityEnvelope` (always available — conservative static limits)
  2. `RuntimeChromeBudget` (available after runtime build — precise per-bank limits)
- Preflight checks:
  - Expert payload size vs ExpertBank slot `usable_bytes`
  - Total model size vs available ROM
  - WRAM/SRAM usage vs budget
  - Norm plan compatibility
  - Placement profile compatibility
- Output: `DeployabilityReport` with `fits_envelope: bool`, `hard_failures`, `risk_flags`.
- Preflight runs BEFORE training starts (fail-fast) and optionally during shadow compile.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                         # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight              # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings          # No warnings
# Verify: preflight with oversized experts returns fits_envelope=false
# Verify: preflight with no budget falls back to DeployabilityEnvelope
# Verify: preflight with budget uses precise per-slot checking
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.4: Preflight is THE verifiable reward gate — exit 0 means model will fit
- §V.3: Hard failures include structured error with expert id, required bytes, available bytes

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Refinement from 2026-04-26 model/runtime interface session

### Two preflight cadences (both required)

Preflight against `RuntimeChromeBudget` runs at two distinct points; do not collapse to one.

**A. Training-start pin.**
- `gbf-train` captures the active `RuntimeChromeBudget` (real or synthetic per T2.6) at training start.
- Records `runtime_nucleus_hash` and `reference_shell_modules` into the run manifest.
- Subsequent training steps assert the captured hash is unchanged (T2.5 enforces this in CI for the production shell, but the training run also self-checks).
- Hash drift mid-training = hard failure; trainer refuses to continue. The fix is either (i) re-pin to the new hash and accept the budget shift, or (ii) revert the runtime shell change.

**B. Per-export re-validation.**
- Export pipeline (driven from F4 `HardenAndSelect` / F8 shadow_compile) re-runs the same preflight against the **current** `RuntimeChromeBudget`.
- Diagnostic on hash drift names both hashes and the per-slot byte deltas:
  ```
  Pre-training: runtime_nucleus_hash=0xAB12... ExpertBank.usable_bytes=15488
  At export:    runtime_nucleus_hash=0xCD34... ExpertBank.usable_bytes=15104
  Delta: -384 bytes per ExpertBank (persistence module added).
  ```
- Re-validation failure on hard fits (`fits_envelope=false`) blocks export. Risk-only flags are surfaced but not blocking.

T2.7 owns the per-export re-validation step; T2.3 (this task) owns the training-start pin and the re-runnable preflight implementation that both cadences share.

### What the preflight checks against `RuntimeChromeBudget`

- Per-expert payload bytes ≤ `min(ExpertBank.usable_bytes − reserved_slack)` across `ExpertBank` slots.
- Shared kernel + embedding + router + classifier total ≤ sum of `CommonBank.usable_bytes`.
- Hot kernels needing Bank0 residency ≤ `Bank0Free.usable_bytes − reserved_slack`.
- Per-token sequence-state bytes ≤ `sram_reserved` budget.
- Hot-arena demand from `ArenaPlan` ≤ `wram_reserved.hot_arena_floor`.
- Required overlay payload size ≤ `wram_reserved.overlay` (the per-`CompileProfile` overlay constant, see F11/T11.1).
- `CompileProfile.max_bank_switches_per_token` is satisfiable given declared MoE topology + tile plan (this is a soft check pre-training, hard check at export when scheduling exists).

### Diagnostic format

Existing diagnostic stays. Add a `WramFitReport` block:
```
WRAM split: overlay=512, hot_arena_floor=4096, total reserved=4608.
ArenaPlan demand: hot_arena=3712 -> PASS with 384 bytes margin.
Overlay fit: largest expert kernel=380 bytes -> PASS with 132 bytes margin.
```

And a `SwitchBudgetReport` block once F11/T11.x lands.

title: Epic C: Oracle Stack — DenotationalOracle + ArtifactOracle + ScheduleOracle + Conformance
description: The three oracles per the three-stratum framing: DenotationalOracle (target-independent reference), ArtifactOracle (canonical frozen artifact), ScheduleOracle (scheduled execution refines artifact semantics). ConformanceEnvelope is the regression gate between Denotation and Artifact. The approximation relation lives in conformance.json, never inside any single oracle.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Epic C: Oracle Stack

### What this is

The three oracles from `planv0.md` §"Three oracles" (line 295) plus the conformance tooling that ties them together. Per the three-stratum framing (line 35):
- `DenotationalOracle` defines target-independent reference meaning (consumes `ReferenceModelBundle`).
- `ArtifactOracle` defines exact canonical semantics of the deployed quantized artifact (no tiling/bank/layout).
- `ScheduleOracle` proves scheduled execution refines artifact semantics (runs `GbSchedIR` over named arenas, continuation ABI, reduction plans, semantic-checkpoint schema).

The approximation relation lives in `ConformanceEnvelope` / `conformance.json` — between Denotation and Artifact — not inside any oracle.

### Why this matters

Without oracles, "is this build correct?" becomes "did it produce some bytes." Oracles produce structured ground truth at three levels that lets every stage of the compiler be checked against a reference. They are also what keeps `gbf-train`'s shadow-compile honest — a checkpoint that drifts from reference is detected before it ships.

### Scope (four features)

- **F-C1 — DenotationalOracle**: runs the `ReferenceProgram` from `ReferenceModelBundle`. Owns `ReferenceNumericProfile`, `DeterminismClass`, `ReductionOrderPolicy`. Per planv0.md correction: slow reference impls live in `gbf-verify`, not here.
- **F-C2 — ArtifactOracle**: runs the frozen artifact in canonical logical form. Same quantization, same logical LUT semantics, same decode semantics, same `SequenceSemanticsSpec`. NO tiling, bank, or concrete layout assumptions.
- **F-C3 — ScheduleOracle**: runs `GbSchedIR` over named arenas + continuation ABI + reduction plans + `SemanticCheckpointSchema`. Proves scheduled execution semantics before assembly + ROM layout.
- **F-C4 — ConformanceEnvelope + conformance.json**: regression-gate file. Records quality/degradation between Denotation and Artifact under named workloads. First-class regression gate per engineering rule 18.

### Out of scope (deferred)

- Slow reference algorithms (pack/unpack, LUT eval, decode/RNG) → Epic F (`gbf-verify`).
- Workload manifests they consume → Epic G + Epic E.
- Build reports including oracle output → Epic F.

### Critical path

```
F-A3 (gbf-abi types) ──→ F-C1, F-C2, F-C3 (all need SemanticCheckpointId + traces)
F-B3 (GbInferIR) ────→ F-C2 (ArtifactOracle compares against)
F-B11 (GbSchedIR) ───→ F-C3 (ScheduleOracle compares against)
F-C1 + F-C2 ────────→ F-C4 (ConformanceEnvelope is between them)
```

### Confidence: MEDIUM

The shapes are clear. The hardest part is making the three oracles run against the same checkpoints with bit-exact agreement at appropriate strata, especially when `DeterminismClass` is `ApproximateUpToReductionOrder`.

title: Fix QAT export artifact identity and validation
description: Address review feedback on bd-g90: activation range/eval semantics must be part of ArtifactCore identity, ArtifactCore must validate QuantSpec tensor references and tensor kinds/shapes, semantic_hash must use canonical bytes instead of serde JSON, and bd-g90 cross-backend claims must be narrowed to bd-22o/bd-1ptv ownership.
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

**Artifact contract:** Tightened the bd-g90 artifact boundary. `ActivationQuantEntry` now carries activation range semantics and eval passthrough behavior, so deployable activation behavior is part of `ArtifactCore::semantic_hash()`. `ArtifactCore::new` now canonicalizes ordering and validates `QuantSpec` tensor references against `CanonicalTensor` ids, kinds, element types, ranks, and shapes. `semantic_hash()` now uses a domain-separated canonical byte encoder over tensor content hashes and quant metadata instead of serde JSON.

**differentiable Burn path:** Not implemented here. This bead only fixes the backend-independent artifact contract. Direct Burn router/expert adapter export handoff remains with bd-1ptv. Cross-backend byte agreement remains with bd-22o.

**Tests proving it:** See the claim-to-gate matrix below.

**Acceptance movement:** The original bd-g90 wording about byte-identical export regardless of training backend is explicitly narrowed. This bead proves deterministic scalar/artifact identity and validation. Cross-backend agreement remains owned by bd-22o because that bead owns the test that compares exports across backend/checkpoint paths. Direct Burn trained-module walking/export handoff remains owned by bd-1ptv.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Activation range semantics in artifact identity | supported | not applicable | supported | `artifact_core_activation_ranges_are_part_of_identity` |
| Activation eval passthrough export semantics | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_encodes_activation_eval_passthrough_in_core_quant` |
| `ArtifactCore::new` tensor reference validation | supported | not applicable | supported | `artifact_core_rejects_missing_quant_tensor_references` |
| Tensor kind/type/shape validation for `QuantSpec` refs | supported | not applicable | supported | `artifact_core_rejects_wrong_quant_tensor_kind`, `artifact_core_rejects_wrong_scale_shape` |
| Canonical semantic hash independent of serde JSON float rendering | supported | not applicable | supported | `artifact_core_canonical_hash_preserves_float_sign_bits` |
| Ordered export accumulators with insertion-time duplicate rejection | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_rejects_duplicate_activation_paths` |
| Golden export hash snapshot | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_has_committed_golden_core_hash` |
| All current `QatModuleRef` variants exercised | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_supports_each_qat_module_ref_variant` |
| Dead `NormExportParams::None` future variant | rejected | not applicable | rejected | `cargo check -p gbf-model`, enum variant removed |
| Cross-backend byte-identical export proof | moved | moved | moved | bd-22o |
| Direct Burn router/expert export handoff | not applicable | moved | moved | bd-1ptv |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Activation ranges now affect `ArtifactCore` identity | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core::tests::artifact_core_activation_ranges_are_part_of_identity` | none | Same tensor set with different activation range hashes differently |
| Eval passthrough is exported as artifact semantics | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_encodes_activation_eval_passthrough_in_core_quant` | none | Same activation with eval passthrough differs from quantized eval mode |
| `ArtifactCore::new` rejects missing tensor refs | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core::tests::artifact_core_rejects_missing_quant_tensor_references` | none | QuantSpec cannot point at absent tensor ids |
| `ArtifactCore::new` rejects wrong tensor kinds and scale shapes | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core::tests::artifact_core_rejects_wrong_quant_tensor_kind core::tests::artifact_core_rejects_wrong_scale_shape` | none | Covers kind/type and per-output-row scale shape validation |
| Semantic hashing preserves f32 sign-bit distinctions | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core::tests::artifact_core_canonical_hash_preserves_float_sign_bits` | none | Prevents JSON-style float normalization from collapsing +0.0 and -0.0 |
| Export output has a committed golden hash | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_has_committed_golden_core_hash` | none | Guards the current canonical export fixture |
| Every QAT module ref variant is exercised | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_supports_each_qat_module_ref_variant` | none | Exercises ternary, activation, norm, router, expert, expert block, shared dense, and dense projection refs |
| Float payload rejection covers NaN, Inf, and -Inf across float tensor kinds | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- tensor::tests::canonical_tensor_rejects_non_finite_float_payloads_for_all_float_kinds` | none | Covers mid-tensor non-finite values |
| Removed dead norm export variant still compiles across model/train | `cargo check -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter` | `burn-adapter` | Confirms downstream adapter code uses the live norm variants |
| QAT closure and architecture sensors pass | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Guards closure shape and Burn containment |
| Target crates are clippy clean | `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings`; `cargo clippy -p gbf-artifact -- -D warnings` | none | Checks the changed crates |

## No-future Acceptance

No unsupported export semantics remain silently accepted in this patch. Eval passthrough is encoded in `ActivationQuantEntry`; invalid activation ranges, missing tensor references, wrong tensor kind/type/shape combinations, malformed norm plans, and dead norm export variants are rejected. This bead does not accept target-specific packed layouts, direct Burn module walking, or cross-backend export agreement as completed behavior.

title: T-B4.5: Accumulator maxima projection (i16/i32 safety)
description:
## What
Project worst-case accumulator magnitudes per `ReductionSiteId` from
quant ranges. Compute `i16_safe`, `i32_safe`. Hard-reject !i32_safe.
See RFC §7.6 accumulator math.

## Why
The earlier "just use 16-bit accumulators" advice becomes precise here.
A reduction site that overflows i32 cannot be made safe by tile choice;
that's a topology/quant-spec issue. F-B7 RangePlan needs `i16_safe`/`i32_safe`
per site to choose between SingleI16, ChunkedI16, RenormLoop.

## Inputs
- T-B4.1 ReductionSiteProjection.

## Outputs
- `pub struct AccumulatorBound { site, projected_max_abs: u64, i16_safe, i32_safe }`.
- Math (checked u128 internally):
  - `raw_product_bound = input_max_abs_q * weight_max_abs_q`.
  - `sum_bound = term_count * raw_product_bound`.
  - `projected_max_abs = sum_bound + bias_max_abs_q.unwrap_or(0)`.
- Reject if not representable in u64.
- `i16_safe = projected_max_abs <= i16::MAX`.
- `i32_safe = projected_max_abs <= i32::MAX`.
- Stage 2 fails hard on `!i32_safe` (`BudgetAccumulatorOverflow`).
- `!i16_safe` is recorded but not by itself a Stage-2 hard failure
  (RangePlan handles it) UNLESS active policy locks `ReductionPlanCeiling::SingleI16Only`,
  in which case it's hard.

## Pitfalls
- u128 internally; u64 in the public field — reject what doesn't fit.
- The `i16_safe == false` interplay with locked SingleI16Only requires
  reading the resolved policy (T-B2.13 product).
- Bias is optional and must be summed only when present.

## Acceptance
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_rejects_accumulator_exceeds_i32
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_records_i16_safe_for_range_plan
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_hard_fail_on_i16_only_lock_with_unsafe_site

## Refs
- RFC §7.6 accumulator math.
- F-B7 RangePlan reads i16_safe (forward-compat).
- Depends on: T-B4.1.
- Blocks: T-B4.11.

title: F-D2: Harness Control Plane
description: Full implementation of HarnessOp (StepSlice/RunUntilCheckpoint/DumpArena/InjectFault/PowerCut/SetSession/GetState). Host-driven slice-granularity control over the ROM via SRAM command/result blocks.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-D2: Harness Control Plane

### What this is

Full implementation of the `HarnessOp` set (`planv0.md` correction): `StepSlice`, `RunUntilCheckpoint`, `DumpArena`, `InjectFault`, `PowerCut`, `SetSession`, `GetState`. Host writes commands at the harness SRAM block; ROM polls and executes at safe-point boundaries; results returned with seq match.

### Why this matters

Without harness, integration tests are bespoke shell-out to emulators. With it, the runtime is interactive at slice granularity from any host (CI, debugger, fuzz driver).

### Design sketch

`gbf-runtime/src/harness.rs` (currently a stub). Polling loop in scheduler-integrated check; per-op handler functions. `PowerCut` is *simulated* — toggles a flag the persistence module honors as a torn-write boundary.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A3.3 HarnessCommandBlock / HarnessResultBlock
- F-A5.2 scheduler (poll integration)
- F-D1 persistence (PowerCut interaction)

### Confidence: HIGH on structure, MEDIUM on `InjectFault` semantics

title: T-A6.2: gbf-store — two-level StageCache
description: Source of truth: RFC history/rfcs/F-A6-gbf-store-migrate.md §7. Implement one physical gbf-store::stage_cache over BlobStore with StageKey, ComponentDigestSet, StageId, ComponentId, FeatureFlag, StageCacheKey, StageCacheEntry, compose_key, StageCache, and StageCacheError. compose_key must use canonical length-prefixed encoding over stage id, sorted shard-local digests, global hash, sorted feature flags, and SemVer pass_version. Missing/stale cache index returns Ok(None).
close_reason: Completed F-A6.2: implemented deterministic StageKey/StageCache over BlobStore with typed StageCacheKey, canonical length-prefixed encoding, stale-index miss behavior, and tests. Gates: cargo test -p gbf-store --test f_a6 stage_cache; workspace clippy/test.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A6.2: `gbf-store` — two-level `StageCache`

**Target crate**: `gbf-store`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-store/src/stage_cache.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 2511 ("two-level: shard-local keys derived from named component digests where legality is local, and whole-build keys where legality is global").

### Background

The StageCache caches the output of every transformative compiler pass. Hits skip the pass. Cache keys must:
- Capture every input that would change the output.
- Decompose into shard-local + global so a change to one component doesn't blow up everyone's cache.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct StageCache {
    blob: BlobStore,
}

pub struct StageKey {
    pub stage_id: PlanningStage,                          // from gbf-asm T-A1.2
    pub shard_local: ComponentDigestSet,                  // hashes of just-this-shard inputs
    pub global: Hash256,                                  // build-wide hash (for stages whose legality is global)
    pub feature_flags: BTreeSet<FeatureFlag>,
    pub pass_version: SemVer,
}

pub struct ComponentDigestSet {
    pub components: BTreeMap<ComponentId, Hash256>,
}

impl StageCache {
    pub fn get(&self, key: &StageKey) -> io::Result<Option<Vec<u8>>>;
    pub fn put(&self, key: &StageKey, payload: &[u8]) -> io::Result<Hash256>;
    pub fn invalidate_shard(&self, component: ComponentId);    // optional bulk invalidation
}

pub fn compose_key(key: &StageKey) -> Hash256;                 // canonicalized hash
```

The composition: `compose_key` serializes the StageKey deterministically (sort the BTreeMap, etc.) and sha256s the result. Two equivalent keys must produce the same hash regardless of construction order.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-store
cargo test -p gbf-store -- stage_cache::shard_invalidation        # Touching one component doesn't invalidate the whole build
cargo test -p gbf-store -- stage_cache::deterministic_keys         # Same logical key -> same hash regardless of insertion order
cargo test -p gbf-store -- stage_cache::feature_flag_sensitivity   # Different flag set -> different key
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §IV.3: Determinism is asserted.
- §I.1: StageKey is structured; no untyped strings as cache keys.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
**Source-of-truth update — see RFC F-A6** (2026-05-03)

The 2026-04-26 description above is a sketch; **RFC `history/rfcs/F-A6-gbf-store-migrate.md` §7 is the authoritative spec** for this task. Where the sketch and the RFC disagree, the RFC wins.

### Authoritative shape (RFC §7)

**`gbf-store::stage_cache` exports:**
- `StageCache` (one physical cache layered over `BlobStore`)
- `StageKey { stage_id, shard_local: ComponentDigestSet, global: Hash256, feature_flags: BTreeSet<FeatureFlag>, pass_version: SemVer }` — `Serialize + Deserialize`
- `ComponentDigestSet` — `BTreeMap<ComponentId, Hash256>` so canonical order is structural
- `StageId(string_id)` (defined here, not in foundation)
- `ComponentId(string_id)` (defined here)
- `FeatureFlag(string_id)` (defined here)
- `StageCacheKey` newtype — distinct from `Hash256` so callers cannot confuse a cache key with a content hash
- `StageCacheEntry { key, payload_hash }`
- `compose_key(&StageKey) -> StageCacheKey` — deterministic across construction order; canonical length-prefixed encoding (length-prefixed UTF-8 for `StageId`/`ComponentId`/`FeatureFlag`, `BTreeMap`/`BTreeSet` iteration for sets, fixed little-endian for integers, raw bytes for `Hash256`)
- `StageCacheError`

### Two-level decomposition (engineering rule 20, planv0 line 2940)

One physical cache, **one** canonical key with two components:
- `shard_local: ComponentDigestSet` — per-component digests where legality is local
- `global: Hash256` — whole-build hash where legality is global

A flat single-input key over-invalidates; a purely shard-local key produces false hits when a global invariant changes. The `compose_key` over both components is what keeps the cache correct.

### Determinism contract

Two `StageKey`s with structurally equivalent contents produce identical hashes regardless of construction order (`stage_cache::deterministic_keys` test). Length prefixes prevent string-boundary collisions (`compose_key_length_prefix_safe`).

### Cache miss / stale index policy

`StageCache::get` returns `Ok(None)` on miss and on stale index (index points at a GC'd blob). Stage-cache payload blobs are weak entries, **not** GC roots — see RFC §17 item 36.

### Resolved questions impacting this task

RFC §17 items 4, 5, 6 govern this task. Notably:
- 4: `StageId`, `ComponentId`, `FeatureFlag` live in `gbf-store::stage_cache` (NOT in `gbf-foundation`).
- 5: one physical cache, two-component canonical key — not two physical caches.
- 6: length-prefixed encoding is mandatory for `compose_key` collision-freedom.

title: F8: Shadow Export/Compile During Training with Pareto Selection
description: On selected checkpoints during training, export EMA weights, compile under one or more CompileRequests, run a small conformance suite, and track a Pareto frontier of quality/fit/cost. Prevents end-of-training surprises. Defines ShadowCompilePolicy and CheckpointFrontierPoint.
acceptance_criteria: All T8.x tasks closed; Shadow compile pipeline tested E2E; Pareto selection tested

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F8: Shadow Export/Compile During Training with Pareto Selection

### Background

A common failure mode in ML systems: you train a model for days/weeks, export it, try to compile/deploy, and discover it doesn't fit the target constraints. For this project, the failure modes include:
- Expert weights that are technically ternary but whose scales push the byte count over the bank budget
- Activation ranges that break the compiler's integer accumulator plan
- A routing pattern that causes pathological bank-switch counts
- A model that passes all quality metrics but whose scheduled execution exceeds the cycle budget

### The solution: compile-in-the-loop

During training (specifically during phases C-E), periodically:
1. Export the current EMA weights as a ModelArtifact
2. Compile under one or more CompileRequests (at least Default profile)
3. Run a small artifact/schedule conformance suite (a few workloads, not the full suite)
4. Record the result as a CheckpointFrontierPoint: quality + conformance + projected fit + schedule cost
5. Track a Pareto frontier of the best checkpoints across the quality/fit/cost dimensions

### ShadowCompilePolicy

Controls the shadow compile frequency and scope:
- every_n_steps: how often to run shadow compile (default: every 1000 steps during Phase E)
- requests: which CompileRequests to test (default: just Default profile)
- workloads: which WorkloadManifests to run conformance against (default: fixtures/workloads/smoke.toml)
- keep_frontier: how many frontier points to keep (default: 10)

### CheckpointFrontierPoint

Records the result of each shadow compile:
- checkpoint: which training checkpoint was exported
- quality: validation loss, perplexity, or other quality metrics
- conformance: denotational-vs-artifact conformance envelope summary
- projected_fit: does it fit the RuntimeChromeBudget? any hard failures?
- schedule_cost: estimated cycles/token, bank-switches/token from ScheduleCostAnalysis

### The training_selection.json artifact

At the end of training (or when manually triggered), the frontier selection algorithm picks the best checkpoint by:
1. Filter: remove any point with hard fit failures
2. Pareto: find points that aren't dominated on (quality, conformance, cost)
3. Select: among Pareto-optimal points, pick the one with the best quality

This checkpoint is then fully exported as the final ModelArtifact + ReferenceModelBundle.

### Practical considerations

Shadow compile is expensive. For a small model it might take seconds; for a larger one, minutes. Running it every training step is infeasible. The every_n_steps parameter should be set so that shadow compile adds < 5% to total training time. During Phase E (HardenAndSelect), this is tightened because that phase is specifically about selection.

The shadow compile can also feed compiler_feedback.json back into the training loop's hint bundle, closing the bidirectional feedback loop described in the plan.

### Confidence: HIGH

This is the single best practical reliability improvement. Finding deployment failures at training time instead of after training saves enormous amounts of time. The types (ShadowCompilePolicy, CheckpointFrontierPoint) are clean and general.

title: T-B2.19: policy_resolution.v1 schema + semantic validator + tests
description:
## What
Define `PolicyResolutionReportBody` + sections + semantic validator +
serde derives. Wire `gbf-report::report_schemas::policy_resolution_v1`.
See RFC §7.5.

## Why
This is Stage 0.5's canonical sidecar. Every later stage references
its self-hash. Without the schema, `ResolvedPolicyProduct.report` has
no concrete shape.

## Inputs
- T-B2.2 ReportEnvelope + canonical JSON.
- T-B2.13 ResolvedCompilePolicy (the resolved object).
- T-B2.14 path-level provenance.
- T-B2.17 HintConsumptionSection.
- T-B2.16 (forbidden RepairProposal_).

## Outputs
- `pub struct PolicyResolutionReportBody { artifact_identity, compile_request, result: Option<PolicyResolutionSuccessSection>, hint_consumption, diagnostics }`.
- `pub struct PolicyResolutionSuccessSection { resolved, compile_knobs, provenance }`.
- Section types: `ArtifactIdentitySection`, `CompileRequestSection`,
  `ResolvedSection`, `CompileKnobsSection`, `PolicyProvenanceSection`.
- Semantic validator implementing §7.5 invariants:
  - `outcome == Passed` iff `result.is_some()` and no Hard diagnostic.
  - `outcome == Failed` iff `result.is_none()` and ≥1 Hard.
  - Provenance sorted by `CompileKnobPath`, every chain non-empty.
  - `compile_knobs.bounds` monotone-tighter than target's defaults.
  - Every `HintBundle::preferences` entry appears in `hint_consumption`.

## Pitfalls
- A failed report has `result = None`, NEVER a partial `ResolvedCompilePolicy`.
- `compile_knobs.provenance` is `Vec<CompileKnobProvenanceEntry>`, sorted.
- Forbid `PolicySource::RepairProposal(_)` in this chunk.
- Schema-version field is in the envelope, not the body.

## Acceptance
- gbf-report::f_b2_policy_resolution_v1_schema_accepts_canonical_fixture
- gbf-report::f_b2_policy_resolution_v1_rejects_missing_required_fields
- gbf-report::f_b2_policy_resolution_v1_rejects_repair_proposal_provenance
- gbf-report::f_b2_policy_resolution_v1_self_hash_round_trip
- gbf-report::f_b2_policy_resolution_v1_failure_report_self_hash_round_trip
- gbf-report::f_b2_policy_resolution_v1_hint_consumption_invariant

## Refs
- RFC §7.5 schema, §9.1 JSON example.
- Depends on: T-B2.2, T-B2.14, T-B2.15, T-B2.16, T-B2.17.
- Blocks: T-B2.20, T-B4.11 (Stage 2 references this self-hash).

title: T8.2: Implement EMA weight export on selected checkpoints
description: Maintain an EMA (exponential moving average) copy of the model weights during training. At shadow compile checkpoints, export the EMA weights through ExportVisitor -> ModelArtifact. The EMA weights are generally smoother and more representative of the converged model than the raw weights at any given step. EMA decay rate is a training hyperparameter (default 0.999). The export is to a temporary directory that gets cleaned up after the shadow compile.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- ema passes; Decay=1.0 preserves initial; Decay=0.0 tracks current; EMA export produces valid ArtifactCore

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/shadow.rs` (extend) + `gbf-train/src/ema.rs` (new)

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "EMA weight export", "exponential moving average", "shadow compile checkpoints".

### Implementation Notes
- Maintain EMA (exponential moving average) copy of model weights throughout training.
- `pub struct EmaWeights { decay: f32, shadow: ModelWeights }`
- Default decay: 0.999
- Update: `shadow = decay * shadow + (1 - decay) * current` at each step.
- At shadow compile checkpoints (per ShadowCompilePolicy), export EMA weights through ExportVisitor.
- EMA export, not current weights, because EMA smooths over recent training noise.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- ema                  # Tests pass
# Verify: EMA with decay=1.0 preserves initial weights
# Verify: EMA with decay=0.0 tracks current weights exactly
# Verify: EMA export produces valid ArtifactCore via ExportVisitor
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.2: EMA update is pure math; scheduling at the boundary
- §V.2: EMA state is reconstructable from checkpoint + decay + training history
