title: T-B2.9: Stage 0 hint-bundle provenance + workload/golden refs
description:
## What
Implement Stage 0 validation classes 8 and 9: HintBundle provenance
self-consistency and workload/golden vector reference resolution.
See RFC §7.3.

## Why
HintBundle is load-bearing per planv0 line ~738: facts/preferences/
constraints have explicit applicability scope, and unscoped or
inconsistent facts mislead downstream stages. Workload and golden vector
refs underpin testing and conformance gates; an unresolved ref is a silent
build hole.

## Inputs
- T-B2.4 ValidatedInputs.
- `gbf-artifact::HintBundle` (`ExportFacts`, `CompilePreferences`,
  `BuildConstraints`).
- Workload/golden ref types from `gbf-workload`.

## Outputs
- Class 8 code: `HintProvenanceInconsistent { fact: TraceProbeId }` —
  fact references an `EvidenceScope` outside the active artifact/workload/
  target/lowering scope.
- Class 9 codes: `WorkloadRefUnresolved { workload }`,
  `GoldenVectorMissing { vector }`,
  `GoldenVectorDigestMismatch { vector, expected, observed }`.

## Pitfalls
- A fact is allowed to reference a *broader* scope than the build (e.g. all
  DMG targets), but never a *narrower* one (e.g. a different model lineage).
- Golden vector digests must be re-verified against actual sidecar bytes.
- `WorkloadManifestRef` resolution is via id, but the resolved manifest's
  hash must match the recorded value.

## Acceptance
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_validate_rejects_hint_provenance_inconsistent
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_validate_rejects_workload_ref_unresolved
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_validate_rejects_golden_vector_missing
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_validate_rejects_golden_vector_digest_mismatch

## Refs
- RFC §7.3 classes 8, 9.
- planv0 §"HintBundle is load-bearing" (line ~738).
- Blocks: T-B2.11.

title: T-B1.6.2: gbf-bench MatmulI8Bringup workload runner
description: Sweeps N∈{32,64,96,128}. For each N: builds ComputeBringupRequest, lowers to ROM, runs deterministic emu with full L4 cooperative scheduler, drives tile-dump pause/resume, byte-exact compare per N. Uses DeterministicPolicy (fixed RTC, RNG seed, audio off). Two consecutive runs produce identical traces and identical reports.
close_reason: Implemented L5 review-packet infrastructure that is independent of full L4 closure. Evidence: gbf-report realism_report.v1 schema/self-hash validation; gbf-bench MatmulI8Bringup streaming-ROM runner and structural-count gates; companion artifacts cycle_attribution_n128.json, matmul_n128.map.json, tile_dump_index_n128.json, frame_trace_n128.json, and streaming ROM sidecar; docs/review/f-b1 packet docs/checklist; scripts/review/f-b1/{smoke,regen,verify-packet}.sh; CI wiring and ignored-test discipline meta-check. Current report honestly marks L4 open with frame_service_misses>0 and absent widget/scheduler timing samples.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.6.2: `gbf-bench::MatmulI8Bringup` workload runner

**Target crate**: `gbf-bench`
**Target file**: `gbf-bench/src/workloads/matmul_bringup.rs`

### Background

The L5 driver. Sweeps `N ∈ {32, 64, 96, 128}`. For each N, builds the appropriate `ComputeBringupRequest`, lowers to ROM, runs under the deterministic emulator with the cooperative scheduler enabled, drives the tile-dump pause/resume protocol, and compares each N's output byte-for-byte against the reference.

### Sweep shape

| N    | Banking                    | UI / scheduler |
|------|----------------------------|----------------|
| 32   | DistinctRomBanks (small)   | full L4 cooperative |
| 64   | DistinctRomBanks           | full L4 cooperative |
| 96   | DistinctRomBanks           | full L4 cooperative |
| 128  | DistinctRomBanks (headline)| full L4 cooperative |

Even smaller-N runs use the full cooperative scheduler so the soft-margin metrics are comparable across the sweep.

### Outputs the runner produces (per N)

- Streamed-output bytes (host-reassembled, byte-exact compared to reference).
- Frame trace (input to T-B1.5.5 metric computation).
- Runtime trace (lease balance, max active leases, MBC writes, yield-while-lease counter).
- Tile-completion timestamps (for cycle-per-output-tile metric).
- Bank0 used/free bytes.
- WRAM/HRAM/SRAM peaks.

These are aggregated by T-B1.6.3 into the final report.

### Determinism

The runner is single-threaded, uses `DeterministicPolicy` from gbf-emu (fixed RTC, RNG seed, audio off). Two consecutive runs on the same host produce identical traces and identical reports.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-bench -- matmul_bringup_runner_accepts_valid_sweep
cargo test -p gbf-bench -- matmul_bringup_runner_byte_exact_per_n
cargo test -p gbf-bench -- matmul_bringup_runner_is_deterministic
cargo test -p gbf-bench -- --ignored matmul_bringup_full_n128_run
```

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §10, §14.

### Dependencies

- T-B1.4 cluster (full streamed-output stack).
- T-B1.5 cluster (cooperative scheduler events).
- T-B1.6.1 (report schema).

comments.author: bkase

title: T-B1.3.2: BankLease/BankGuard integration in matmul lowering
description: Per-panel-copy lease/release in the codegen output. Structure: lease A → copy A panel → release A → lease B → copy B panel → release B → assert no lease → compute/yield. F-A4 is the only legal MBC-writer; generated code never writes MBC directly. Short critical lease window keeps the inner-product loop free to yield.
close_reason: Implemented L2 cross-bank BankLease path per F-B1 RFC. Evidence: BringupRomWindowPlan records Bank0 resources plus A/B switchable banks; generated L2/L3 streaming ROMs acquire/release BankLease via gbf-runtime banking lowering instead of raw MBC writes; validator rejects raw MBC writes, leaked/unknown/duplicate leases, and yield while a lease is active; emulator gates f_b1_l2_cross_bank_matmul_matches_reference and f_b1_l3_streaming_rom_nonzero_offsets_match_reference_n32 pass; N=64 byte-exact cross-bank run is in docs/review/f-b1/artifacts/realism_report.v1.json from gbf-test f_b1_regen. ASM checklist: symbolic pre-layout BankLease ops lower through audited runtime banking; raw MBC writes are excluded by typed effect validation and tests.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.3.2: BankLease/BankGuard integration in matmul lowering

**Target crate**: `gbf-codegen`
**Target file**: `gbf-codegen/src/matmul.rs` (extends T-B1.2.4)

### Background

L2 is F-A4's first real consumer. Every panel copy in the kernel must lease the operand bank, copy the panel into WRAM, then release the lease. The kernel never writes MBC registers directly; it always goes through F-A4's `BankLease`/`BankGuard` ABI.

### Lowering structure (L2, DistinctRomBanks)

```text
section L2Kernel @ Bank0
  for mt in output_tile_rows:                         ; 0..N/16
    for nt in output_tile_cols:                       ; 0..N/16
      zero accumulator[16, 16]

      for kt in k_tiles:                              ; 0..N/16
        // ----- A panel copy under lease -----
        let guard_a = banking::acquire_rom_bank(a_bank);
        copy A[mt:mt+16, kt:kt+16] -> a_panel
        drop(guard_a);                                ; emits BankRelease

        // ----- B panel copy under lease -----
        let guard_b = banking::acquire_rom_bank(b_bank);
        copy B[kt:kt+16, nt:nt+16] -> b_panel
        drop(guard_b);

        // ----- Compute / yield region -----
        ; assert: no BankLease is active here (T-B1.3.4 invariant)
        for kk in 0..16:
          for mm in 0..16:
            for nn in 0..16:
              acc[mm, nn] += quarter_square_mul(a_panel[mm, kk], b_panel[kk, nn])

      ; tile-complete trap (T-B1.4.3 owns full pause/resume protocol)
```

### Why lease, copy, release (not lease-and-hold-during-compute)

If the lease were held during the inner-product loop, yielding under it would be unsafe (the scheduler/ISR would observe ambiguous ROMX state). Copying the panel into WRAM under a short lease, then releasing, gives us:

- A short critical window for the lease.
- All subsequent compute reads from WRAM (`a_panel`, `b_panel`).
- A clean boundary for `T-B1.5.1` to insert yields.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_l2_lowering_uses_banklease_ops
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_l2_lowering_releases_lease_before_compute
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_l2_lowering_panel_copy_inside_lease
```

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §7.8 (lowering contract).

### Dependencies

- T-B1.2.4 (codegen lowering core).
- T-B1.3.1 (`BringupRomWindowPlan`).
- **F-A4** (BankLease/BankGuard ABI) — hard external dep.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## REVISION PASS — Cross-cutting testing & logging discipline applied

This bead now blocks on one or more of the new discipline beads added in the audit pass. Specifically:

- **T-B1.0.8** — test fixture infrastructure (synthetic AsmIR, frame traces, expected outputs).
- **T-B1.0.9** — typed tracing event vocabulary + subscriber-level test capture (per AGENTS.md "Logging helper closure must cite subscriber-level capture for event shape").

### Required additions for this bead

1. **Logging discipline**: every event this bead emits or consumes uses T-B1.0.9's typed `FB1TraceEvent` enum, never raw stringly fields. Tests use `CapturedTrace::capture(...)` to assert event shape, not just count.

2. **Test fixtures**: this bead consumes synthetic fixtures from T-B1.0.8 instead of reinventing them. If this bead introduces a new synthetic fixture, it should be added to T-B1.0.8's catalogue with a one-line description in the README.

4. **`run_latest.log.summary.json` cross-reference**: if this bead's behavior is observable in the heavy-gate run (i.e., it emits typed events that the run-log captures), the summary's `tracing_event_counts` field gains an entry for those event types. The diff in that file across runs is the regression signal.

### What did not change

The bead's original specification, types, validation rules, and acceptance gates remain authoritative. This comment adds the discipline overlay; it does not replace anything.

title: Epic B: Compiler Pipeline — 14 Stages + Refinement Loop
description: The 14-stage transform pipeline from planv0.md plus the FeasibilityRefinementLoop. Validation envelope (stages 0, 0.5), QuantGraph, StaticBudgetReport, GbInferIR, ObservationPlan, RangePlan, StoragePlan, SramPagePlan, RomWindowPlan, OverlayPlan, ArenaPlan, GbSchedIR + ResourceStateValidation, ScheduleCostAnalysis, Backend (AsmIR -> ReachabilityValidation -> PlacedRom -> EncodedRom). FeasibilityRefinementLoop wraps stages 5-11 with RepairPolicy/RepairProposal/CompileKnobs (BLOCKED on oracle question).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Epic B: Compiler Pipeline — 14 Stages + Refinement Loop

### What this is

The transform pipeline from `planv0.md` §"The compiler pipeline" (line 1031). It takes a frozen `ArtifactCore` plus a `CompileRequest` and produces a `CompiledBuild` (ROM + reports + certificates). The 14 stages plus the refinement loop are the actual compiler — what most other epics produce inputs for or consume outputs from.

### Why this matters

This is the heart of GBLLM. Training (epic `bd-1rb`) produces artifacts; oracles (Epic C) verify them; M0 (Epic A) provides the target/abi/asm contracts; bench (Epic E) calibrates the cost model — but Epic B is what *compiles a model into a ROM that runs on a Game Boy*. The architectural rigor of planv0.md (validation envelope, refinement loop, three-stratum oracle correspondence) all hangs on these stages working as specified.

### Scope (sixteen features)

Headline pipeline ordering:

```
Policy / feasibility envelope:
  F-B0  ArtifactValidationAndUpgrade + ResolvedCompilePolicy        (stages 0, 0.5)
  F-B1  QuantGraph                                                   (stage 1)
  F-B2  StaticBudgetReport                                           (stage 2)

Transformative stages (wrapped by FeasibilityRefinementLoop):
  F-B3  GbInferIR (value/effect IR)                                  (stage 3)
  F-B4  ObservationPlan                                              (stage 4)
  F-B5  RangePlan                                                    (stage 5)
  F-B6  StoragePlan                                                  (stage 6)
  F-B7  SramPagePlan                                                 (stage 7)
  F-B8  RomWindowPlan                                                (stage 8)
  F-B9  OverlayPlan                                                  (stage 8.5; replaces existing T11.4 stub bd-gmw)
  F-B10 ArenaPlan                                                    (stage 9)
  F-B11 GbSchedIR + ResourceStateValidation                          (stages 10, 10.5)
  F-B12 ScheduleCostAnalysis                                         (stage 11)
  F-B13 Backend (AsmIR codegen + ReachabilityValidation + PlacedRom + EncodedRom)  (stage 12)

Cross-cutting:
  F-B14 FeasibilityRefinementLoop + RepairPolicy + CompileKnobs       (BLOCKED on oracle question)
  F-B15 StageCache integration across all stages
```

### Out of scope (deferred)

- Producing build reports + certificates → Epic F.
- Running oracles against compiler output → Epic C.
- Running bench against compiled ROMs to refine calibration → Epic E.

### Critical path

```
F-B0 → F-B1 → F-B2 → F-B3 → F-B4 → F-B5 → F-B6 → F-B7 → F-B8 → F-B9 → F-B10 → F-B11 → F-B12 → F-B13
                                  └─────── F-B14 wraps stages 5-11 ──────────┘
F-B15 cross-cuts every stage.
```

`F-B14` is **blocked** until the oracle answers the `CompileKnobs` question we drafted (typed surface for `RepairProposal::tighten`, plus per-profile `RepairPolicy` defaults). All other features can proceed.

### Existing beads to absorb / reshape

- **`bd-w80` (T3.4)** — Update `StaticBudgetReport` for `TernaryWeightPlan` byte math. Belongs to F-B2; can be re-parented or its work absorbed.
- **`bd-gmw` (T11.4)** — `OverlayPlan` IR stub. Replaced by F-B9 with the plan's `{regions, installs, share_classes}` shape. T11.4 should be closed-as-superseded once F-B9 lands.
- **`bd-1f7` (T8.3)** — Shadow compile pipeline (under F8 / `bd-2am`). Orchestrates Epic B stages but doesn't implement them.

### Constitution checkpoints

- **§I.1**: Each stage is a typed transform from one IR to the next; no opaque blobs cross stage boundaries.
- **§III.1**: Most regressions get caught by per-stage golden snapshot tests rather than running ROMs.
- **§I.4**: The pipeline's verifiable reward is the certificate set + a passing `conformance.json` against `ArtifactOracle`.
- **§VI.1**: Each pass type lives in `gbf-codegen` (or `gbf-ir` for IR types); no parallel implementations elsewhere.

### Confidence: HIGH on staging, BLOCKED on F-B14

The 13 transform stages are well-specified in planv0.md. F-B14 cannot land without first resolving (a) `CompileKnobs` enumeration and (b) per-profile `RepairPolicy` defaults — both questions are out for oracle review.

### When this epic is done

The compiler can take an artifact + CompileRequest and produce a passing tiny-model ROM end-to-end, with all certificates emitted, with at least one shadow-compile run from training-time succeeding.

title: Burn adapter for ExpertBlockQat execution
description: Implement the gbf-train Burn adapter for ExpertBlockQat, including the two-matrix up -> clipped activation -> ActFakeQuant -> down execution path, differentiable gradients through the supported clipped activations, and explicit rejection for unsupported GatedLinearUnit expert configs until a real three-matrix executable/export path exists. Tests must prove the Burn adapter matches the gbf-model scalar ExpertBlockQat behavior and preserves activation phase options.

title: F-D1: Persistence — versioned SRAM record protocol
description: PersistHeader + PersistGroupCommit + PersistKind + PageState. Double-buffered pages; explicit commit boundaries; SRAM-authoritative recovery. Per-record-kind compatibility (semantic_state_hash for SequenceState, resume_abi_hash for Continuation, build_identity_hash for harness/trace).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-D1: Persistence — versioned SRAM record protocol

### What this is

Per `planv0.md` lines 1718–1788. `PersistHeader` + `PersistGroupCommit` + `PersistKind` + `PageState` (`Writing`/`Committed`/`Retired`). Double-buffered pages with explicit commit boundaries (`NeedInput`, `TokenReady`, `Finished`, or compiler-approved checkpoint). Records sharing mutual consistency get a `CommitGroupId` and atomic group-commit manifest. SRAM-authoritative recovery (HRAM caches but SRAM is truth).

### Why this matters

A power cut mid-write must not corrupt the next session. Without this protocol, "save state" becomes a trust nightmare and every schema bump becomes user-hostile.

### Design sketch

Implement `gbf-runtime/src/persistence.rs` (currently a stub). Header layout per planv0.md; CRC verification; per-record-kind compatibility validation (semantic_state_hash for SequenceState, resume_abi_hash for Continuation, build_identity_hash for harness/trace). Boot validates newest committed group.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A3.4 FaultCode (PersistChecksum, PersistTornWrite, PersistSchemaUnknown)
- F-A2.2 SRAM constants
- F-A5.6 video_commit (persistence runs concurrently with UI)
- F-A6.5 gbf-migrate (StateMigrator integration)

### Confidence: HIGH. Protocol shape is well-specified.

title: F-F4: FailureCapsule + minimization + reduction_log
description: Capture failed runs with structured state (workload, observations, fault snapshot, build identity, run manifest) + minimized_workload + reduction_log. Cross-build replayable. Makes failures bisectable instead of ephemeral.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-F4: FailureCapsule + minimization

### What this is

`FailureCapsule` captures failed runs with structured state (workload manifest, observed observations, fault snapshot, build identity, run manifest) plus a `minimized_workload` and `reduction_log`. Cross-build replayable.

### Why this matters

A failure that can't be reproduced is a failure that can't be fixed. Minimization (delta-debug-style reduction of the workload that triggers the failure) makes investigations bisectable.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct FailureCapsule {
    pub original_workload: WorkloadId,
    pub minimized_workload: WorkloadManifest,
    pub reduction_log: Vec<ReductionStep>,
    pub fault: Option<FaultSnapshot>,
    pub run_manifest: RunManifest,
    pub conformance_snapshot: ConformanceEnvelope,
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A3.4 FaultSnapshot
- F-F1 RunManifest
- F-A6.1 gbf-store (capsule archival)

### Confidence: HIGH on capture, MEDIUM on minimization (needs experimentation)

title: T-B4.8a: Static-fit interpretation and necessary-condition decision semantics
description:
## What
Define `StaticFitInterpretation` and pin the contract that `fits = true`
means "passes Stage-2 necessary static checks", NOT final deployability.
See RFC §7.6 / §7.7.

## Why
Without explicit interpretation, `fits` becomes overclaimed. Reviewers
might conclude "the build is final" when in fact `RomWindowPlan` (F-B10)
or `ResourceStateValidation` (F-B13) could still fail. This task is small
but load-bearing: it pins the meaning of the boolean.

## Inputs
- T-B4.3..T-B4.7 (failure modes).

## Outputs
- `pub enum StaticFitInterpretation { PassesNecessaryStaticChecks, FailsNecessaryStaticChecks }`.
- `BudgetDecisionSection.interpretation` field.
- Schema invariant: `fits == true` ⇒ `interpretation = PassesNecessaryStaticChecks`;
  `fits == false` ⇒ `interpretation = FailsNecessaryStaticChecks`.
- Documentation pinning the contract: later passes (F-B10/F-B12/F-B13)
  remain authoritative for final deployability.

## Pitfalls
- Implementation is small (an enum + invariant). The HARD part is the
  documentation: this task's deliverable is mostly the contract pin.
- Don't extend the enum with a "Tentative" or "Pending" variant in
  this chunk — semantics here are binary.

## Acceptance
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_fits_means_necessary_checks_only
- gbf-report::f_b4_static_budget_v1_decision_interpretation_invariant

## Refs
- RFC §7.6, §7.7 BudgetDecisionSection, §16.1 forward-compat for F-B10/F-B12/F-B13.
- Depends on: T-B4.8.
- Blocks: T-B4.11.

This closes the bidirectional feedback loop. Config-gated because the feedback format may evolve.

title: T-B1.6.3: Report aggregation logic + cycle attribution
description: Aggregator turns raw traces into realism_report.v1 numeric fields. Applies canonical cycle-attribution rules: per-product-accumulate excludes dump pause + ISR + scheduler; per-output-tile excludes dump; per-full-matmul includes everything. Computes wall-clock seconds from DMG clock, effective bandwidths, distribution stats.
close_reason: Implemented L5 review-packet infrastructure that is independent of full L4 closure. Evidence: gbf-report realism_report.v1 schema/self-hash validation; gbf-bench MatmulI8Bringup streaming-ROM runner and structural-count gates; companion artifacts cycle_attribution_n128.json, matmul_n128.map.json, tile_dump_index_n128.json, frame_trace_n128.json, and streaming ROM sidecar; docs/review/f-b1 packet docs/checklist; scripts/review/f-b1/{smoke,regen,verify-packet}.sh; CI wiring and ignored-test discipline meta-check. Current report honestly marks L4 open with frame_service_misses>0 and absent widget/scheduler timing samples.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.6.3: Report aggregation logic + cycle attribution

**Target crate**: `gbf-report`
**Target file**: `gbf-report/src/aggregation.rs`

### Background

Takes the raw trace + runtime metrics from `gbf-bench::MatmulI8Bringup` and produces a `realism_report.v1` document. Cycle-attribution rules are applied here; this is the bead that turns "the emulator told us X cycles passed" into "X cycles per product-accumulate."

### Cycle-attribution rules (canonical)

- `cycles_per_product_accumulate` is computed from emulator cycle deltas over known product-count regions (kernel inner-loop body). Tile-dump pause is excluded.
- `cycles_per_output_tile` is the cycle delta from "first product of tile" to "tile-complete safe point" — does not include the dump pause.
- `cycles_per_full_matmul` is end-to-end from "kernel entry" to "final tile dump complete." Includes ISR, dump-pauses, scheduler service. This is the wall-clock-equivalent number.
- `wall_clock_seconds_at_gb_clock_f64` is derived: `cycles_per_full_matmul / (4_194_304.0 * 4.0)` for M-cycles, since `1 M-cycle = 4 T-cycles` and `1 T-cycle ≈ 1 / 4.194 MHz`.
- `effective_*_bandwidth_*_f64` is `bytes_read / wall_clock_seconds_at_gb_clock_f64`.

### Aggregation outputs

For each N, the aggregator produces:

- `compute_costs` — distribution stats (min/mean/max/p99) over per-product-accumulate cycles, per-tile cycles, and the scalar `cycles_per_full_matmul`.
- `memory_bandwidth` — Bank0 table-read bytes, ROMX operand-read bytes, effective bandwidths, WRAM/HRAM/SRAM peaks.
- `rom_layout` — Bank0 used/free bytes, ROMX operand bank size.
- `banking` — logical-switch counts, MBC-write counts, lease acquire/release/balance/max-active, yield-while-lease counter.
- `scheduling` — frames-to-completion, gated-frame count, vblank count, widget-update count, scheduler-service count, frame-service-misses, max-no-progress, worst-case interrupt latency, cycles-remaining-at-widget-tick distribution, cycles-remaining-at-scheduler-service distribution.
- `conformance` — byte-exact-match flag, reference-source hash, fixture hash, expected-output hash.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-report -- aggregation::cycle_attribution_excludes_dump_pause
cargo test -p gbf-report -- aggregation::wall_clock_seconds_derived_from_dmg_clock
cargo test -p gbf-report -- aggregation::distributions_have_min_mean_max_p99
cargo test -p gbf-report -- aggregation::frame_service_misses_zero_in_passing_run
cargo test -p gbf-bench -- f_b1_report_aggregation_known_fixture
```

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §9.1 (cycle attribution rule), §10.

### Dependencies

- T-B1.6.1 (schema).
- T-B1.6.2 (runner provides traces).
- T-B1.5.5 (frame trace ordering metrics).
- T-B1.3.5 (lease metrics).

comments.author: bkase

title: T1.4: Implement NormApproxQat module
description: Deployable norm approximation that can be trained end-to-end. Supports NormPlan::AffineClipLut (simplest) and NormPlan::TileRmsThenAffineClip (more faithful). Must match compiler's NormPlan lowering exactly. The LUT contents, affine parameters, and clip bounds are all exported as first-class tensors.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::norm passes; Both NormPlan variants implemented; Export params match compiler NormPlan contract
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

- Artifact contract: `gbf-artifact::norm_plan::{NormPlan, NormExportParams}` is the canonical contract. `NormApproxQat::export_norm_params()` and `NormApproxBurnQat::export_norm_params()` emit `AffineClipLut { plan, lut_values }` or `TileRmsThenAffineClip { plan }` through that artifact type.
- Differentiable Burn path: `gbf-train/src/qat/norm.rs::NormApproxBurnQat::forward`, behind `burn-adapter`, owns affine and clip values as Burn parameters and runs AffineClipLut or tiled RMS with Burn tensor ops.
- Tests that prove it: `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::norm`, `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::norm`, `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`, and `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`.
- Acceptance movement: the original gradient-flow proof is intentionally located in `gbf-train`, not `gbf-model`, because `bd-1mv` establishes the Burn adapter boundary and `gbf-model` must not depend on Burn. This is semantically equivalent to the bead's gradient criterion because the adapter wraps the `gbf-model` core and exports through the same artifact contract.

## Path Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `NormApproxPlan::AffineClipLut` forward | supported | supported | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::norm`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::norm` |
| `NormApproxPlan::TileRmsThenAffineClip` forward | supported | supported | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::norm`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::norm` |
| Affine parameters and clip bounds as training tensors | not applicable | supported | supported | `burn_norm_affine_clip_lut_gradients_reach_input_affine_and_clip_bounds`; `burn_norm_export_matches_artifact_contract_from_owned_tensors` |
| LUT values for `AffineClipLut` export | supported | supported | supported | `qat_norm_affine_clip_lut_forward_and_export_match`; `burn_norm_export_matches_artifact_contract_from_owned_tensors` |
| Ragged tile input | rejected | rejected | not applicable | `qat_norm_rejects_ragged_tile_input`; `burn_norm_rejects_ragged_tile_input_before_reshape` |
| Future/unsupported norm variants | rejected | rejected | rejected | Exhaustive `NormApproxPlan` matches guarded by `cargo check -p gbf-model` and `cargo check -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter` |

## Claim-To-Gate Matrix

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `gbf-model` norm scalar core compiles and exposes both supported variants | `cargo check -p gbf-model` | none | Covers the backend-independent module required by the bead. |
| Scalar forward and export match the canonical artifact norm contract | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::norm` | none | Tests both `AffineClipLut` and `TileRmsThenAffineClip`, export shapes, invalid contracts, non-finite input, and ragged tiles. |
| `gbf-model` has no clippy warnings for the norm implementation | `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings` | none | Acceptance criterion. |
| Burn training path compiles through the adapter boundary | `cargo check -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter` | `burn-adapter` | Proves the differentiable path lives outside `gbf-model`. |
| Burn norm gradients flow through input, affine params, and clip bounds | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::norm` | `burn-adapter` | Includes focused autodiff tests for AffineClipLut and TileRmsThenAffineClip. |
| Burn adapter exports the same `NormExportParams` contract as the scalar core | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::norm` | `burn-adapter` | `burn_norm_export_matches_artifact_contract_from_owned_tensors`. |
| Burn imports remain confined to `gbf-train` | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Architecture sensor protects `bd-1mv` boundary. |
| QAT closure constraints are satisfied | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py` | none | Harness passed before closure. |

## No Future Variant Acceptance

`NormApproxPlan` accepts only `AffineClipLut` and `TileRmsThenAffineClip`; no future norm plans, dynamic LUT training modes, or implicit LayerNorm lowering variants are accepted. Invalid scalar contracts are rejected by constructors, Burn ragged tile inputs are rejected before reshape, and Burn export revalidates trained affine and clip tensors before producing artifact parameters. `NormPlan::None` remains an artifact-level "no norm module" value, not an accepted `NormApproxQat` training variant.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-model/src/qat/norm.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "NormApproxQat", "NormPlan", "AffineClipLut", "TileRmsThenAffineClip".

### Implementation Notes
- `NormApproxQat` is a deployable normalization approximation trainable end-to-end.
- Supports two `NormPlan` variants:
  - `NormPlan::AffineClipLut` — learnable affine transform + clipping, deployable via a small LUT
  - `NormPlan::TileRmsThenAffineClip` — tiled RMS norm followed by affine + clip
- Key API:
  - `forward(&self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor` — runs the approximate norm
  - `export_norm_params(&self) -> NormExportData` — parameters for compiler's NormPlan lowering
- Must produce outputs that EXACTLY match what the compiler's NormPlan lowering expects
- The norm approximation error is part of the training loss (the model learns to compensate)

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::norm            # Unit tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings        # No warnings
# Verify: both NormPlan variants implemented
# Verify: export produces parameters matching NormPlan contract
# Verify: gradient flows through the approximation
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: NormPlan enum is the canonical source of truth — match its variants exactly
- §III.1: Compiler/types catch NormPlan mismatch at compile time, not runtime

title: F-B2: ArtifactValidationAndUpgrade + ResolvedCompilePolicy (Stages 0, 0.5)
description: Validation envelope at the head of the pipeline. Verify schema, semantic core hash, manifest invariants, lowering round-trip, calibration freshness. Resolve CompileRequest into ResolvedCompilePolicy with PolicyProvenance. Emit policy_resolution.json.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B0: ArtifactValidationAndUpgrade + ResolvedCompilePolicy (Stages 0, 0.5)

### What this is

The validation envelope at the head of the compiler pipeline (`planv0.md` line 1152, 1037). Stage 0 verifies the artifact's schema, semantic core hash, manifest invariants, required-feature set, self-consistency of canonical tensors / LUTs / quant spec / sequence semantics / decode capabilities / lexical spec / interaction bundle / hint bundle / workload references / golden vectors. Validates `TargetDataLoweringArtifact` packer round-trips. Validates `CompileRequest` against the resolved `TargetProfile`, required-feature set, and any referenced calibration bundles in the `CalibrationSetRef`.

Stage 0.5 (`ResolvedCompilePolicy`) records the fully resolved policy with `PolicyProvenance` (target_defaults, profile_defaults, hint_bundle_hash, compile_request_hash, calibration_hash). Every build emits `policy_resolution.json`.

### Why this matters

Most "the build worked but produced garbage" failures trace back to compiling against an artifact whose schema doesn't match the compiler's expectations or against stale calibration. Catching them here is cheap; catching them later means redoing scheduling, layout, encoding.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub fn validate_artifact(core: &ArtifactCore, manifest: &ArtifactManifest, aux: &ArtifactAux)
    -> Result<(), ValidationError>;
pub fn validate_lowerings(core: &ArtifactCore, lowerings: &[TargetDataLoweringArtifact])
    -> Result<(), ValidationError>;
pub fn resolve_policy(req: &CompileRequest, target: &TargetProfile)
    -> Result<ResolvedCompilePolicy, PolicyResolutionError>;
```

Output: `policy_resolution.json` with the fully resolved policy and provenance.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A2.1 (TargetProfile)
- F-A6.2 (StageCache: validation results are cached)
- gbf-artifact (existing) for ArtifactCore types
- gbf-policy (existing) for CompileRequest, ResolvedCompilePolicy, RepairPolicy

### Confidence: HIGH

Mostly schema validation + serde + provenance hashing.

comments.author: bkase
bkase
comments.text:
## RFC published: F-B2 + F-B4 paired RFC

The detailed design for this feature plus its sibling F-B4 lives at:

`history/rfcs/F-B2-F-B4-pipeline-entry-validation.md`

The RFC supersedes the design sketch in this bead's earlier comments. Highlights:

- Three reports, not two: `artifact_validation.json` (Stage 0),
  `policy_resolution.json` (Stage 0.5), `static_budget.json` (Stage 2 / F-B4).
- Stage 0 owns 10 validation classes with a closed `ValidationCode` enum.
- Stage 0.5 uses a constraint-frame merge resolver with path-level
  `ConstraintProvenance` and forbids `RepairProposal(_)` provenance until F-B16
  unblocks.
- `ResolvedPolicyProduct` wraps `ResolvedCompilePolicy` with input-hash
  identity and report self-hash so Stage 2 has a typed handle.
- Schema compatibility limited to lossless in-memory adapters per §7.0.
- StageCache key shape pinned in §7.8 (success + failure memo).
- No profile-time relaxations: Bringup uses an explicit
  `BootstrapCalibrationBundle` and `bringup-*.chrome_budget.json` fixture.
- Implementation order in RFC §8.5; worked examples in §7.9; review-packet
  markdown contents in §13.1; forward-compatibility contract in §16.1.

Task graph: 21 tasks (T-B2.1 .. T-B2.21). See bead labels `f-b2-f-b4,chunk1`
for the full set, or RFC §12 for the dependency-ordered list.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## RFC amendments (2026-05-07): bead-graph review pass

Three text-level corrections to `history/rfcs/F-B2-F-B4-pipeline-entry-validation.md`
discovered during the bead-graph review. Logged here so the RFC can be
amended in a separate doc PR without losing context:

### Amendment §8.5 ordering (was Issue #6)

The Wave-4 implementation order in §8.5 lists step 18 "T-B4.3..T-B4.7 in
parallel" *before* step 19 "T-B4.8 deterministic static placement models".
But T-B4.3 (per-bank occupancy + slot assignment) consumes the placement
models defined in T-B4.8, so T-B4.8 must come first. The bead graph
correctly enforces `bd-1bax → bd-3cfz`. Recommended §8.5 patch:

> 17.5. T-B4.8 deterministic static placement models.
> 18.   In parallel: T-B4.3 (depends on T-B4.8), T-B4.4, T-B4.5, T-B4.6, T-B4.7.

Also: the bead-graph review added a new ordering constraint that
T-B4.9 (BudgetFailure taxonomy) must precede T-B4.3..T-B4.7 because those
tasks construct `BudgetFailure` values. Recommended §8.5 patch:

> 17.6. T-B4.9 BudgetFailure taxonomy + ValidationCode wiring.
> 18.   In parallel: T-B4.3 (depends on T-B4.8 + T-B4.9), T-B4.4, T-B4.5,
>       T-B4.6 (also depends on T-B4.10), T-B4.7 (also depends on T-B4.10).
> 19.   T-B4.10 RuntimeChromeBudget binding.

### Amendment §7.6 QuantGraphBudgetView ordering (was Issue #8)

§7.6's `QuantGraphBudgetView` semantic-validator bullets pin sort order
for `experts`, `shared_kernels`, `reduction_sites` but only mention
`shared_luts sorted by LUT id` in passing. Recommend explicit bullet:

> * `shared_luts` sorted by `SharedLutId` ascending;

### Amendment §7.8 cache-key registry source (was Issue #9)

§7.8 lists `compatibility_adapter_registry_hash` in the Stage 0 cache
key without naming where the registry lives. T-B2.5 (bd-18ut) introduces
the registry as a side-effect (it has to, to apply lossless adapters).
Recommend a sentence in §7.8 or §7.0:

> The compatibility adapter registry is owned by `gbf-codegen::stages::validate`
> (introduced in T-B2.5). Its canonical hash flows into the Stage 0 cache key.

### Status

These are doc-level corrections only; the bead graph has been updated to
reflect the correct ordering and explicit edges. RFC body should be patched
when convenient.

title: T1.8: Burn version pinning and drift CI check
description: Pin burn = '=X.Y.Z' in Cargo.toml. Add CI check that: (a) verifies the pin hasn't been loosened, (b) runs a small QAT round-trip test on Burn version bumps, (c) checks that gbf-model does not import burn directly. This prevents silent API drift from breaking the training/artifact agreement.
acceptance_criteria: CI check script exits 0 on valid state; Exits 1 if burn pin missing or gbf-model imports burn directly; QAT round-trip test on version bump
close_reason:
Implemented Burn version pinning and drift CI guard.

Changes:
- Added `scripts/check_burn_pin.sh` as the CI entry point. Default mode runs the structural static boundary check, the existing architecture Burn-import boundary test, and a one-step Burn QAT train/export round-trip test. `--static-only` honors `GBF_BURN_PIN_REPO_ROOT` for fixture roots. `--self-test` runs permanent negative fixtures.
- Updated `.github/workflows/ci.yml` to run `scripts/check_burn_pin.sh --self-test` and `scripts/check_burn_pin.sh` before the normal QAT harness/fmt/clippy/test gates.
- Replaced line-oriented manifest assertions in `gbf-train::adapter` tests with structural TOML validation. The check requires the workspace Burn dependency to be exact-pinned, requires `gbf-train` to consume it via `workspace = true, optional = true`, rejects direct/aliased Burn dependencies in `gbf-model`, and scans `gbf-model/src` for direct `burn::` references.
- Added negative fixture coverage for loosened/missing pins, `gbf-train` direct Burn versions, direct `gbf-model` Burn deps, package aliases, workspace aliases, and direct source imports.
- Added adapter aliases for Burn optimizer APIs needed by training tests.
- Added `burn_ternary_one_step_train_export_round_trip_survives_burn_api`, which performs a forward loss, backward pass, one Adam optimizer step, conversion back to model-owned QAT state, ExportVisitor artifact generation, and JSON deserialize equality.

Review resolution:
- Intent: accepted non-blocking note. Closure records that the exact pin is owned in the workspace while `gbf-train` consumes it through the workspace dependency.
- Design: accepted. Replaced grep-based manifest validation with structural TOML checks and made the script delegate the broad Burn import boundary to the existing architecture test.
- Semantic: accepted. Fixed package-alias/workspace-alias bypasses and replaced the QAT smoke test gate with a literal one-step train/export round trip.
- Evidence: accepted. Added permanent negative fixtures and also manually verified script nonzero behavior for loosened pin, aliased model Burn dependency, and direct model Burn import.

Feedback Harness Reflection:
| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| QAT drift gate was narrower than the bead text | A forward/backward/export smoke test was named as a round trip | Only claim QAT round-trip coverage when optimizer step and artifact serialization/deserialization are exercised, or explicitly narrow the claim | `qat::ternary::tests::burn_ternary_one_step_train_export_round_trip_survives_burn_api` | `scripts/check_burn_pin.sh` and CI Burn pin step |
| Manifest grep missed TOML shapes and Burn aliases | The manifest boundary was treated as line text instead of structured Cargo data | Manifest ownership checks should parse TOML and resolve `package = \"burn\"` plus workspace aliases | `adapter::tests::burn_static_boundary_from_env_root_passes` | `scripts/check_burn_pin.sh --static-only` |
| Exit-1 acceptance had no permanent negative proof | Failure modes were tested manually only | Acceptance failure modes need fixture sensors when the script is the product surface | `adapter::tests::burn_static_boundary_rejects_invalid_fixtures` | `scripts/check_burn_pin.sh --self-test` and CI |
| Burn import boundary was duplicated with weaker scope | The new script reimplemented part of the existing architecture rule | Prefer delegating to the named architecture boundary gate when one already exists | `gbf-test --test architecture burn_imports_are_confined_to_train_adapter` | `scripts/check_burn_pin.sh` default mode |

Proof:
- `cargo check -p gbf-train`
- `scripts/check_burn_pin.sh --self-test`
- `scripts/check_burn_pin.sh --static-only`
- `scripts/check_burn_pin.sh`
- Manual fixture command verified script nonzero behavior for loosened pin, aliased `gbf-model` Burn dependency, and direct `gbf-model` Burn import.
- `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture burn_imports_are_confined_to_train_adapter -- --exact`
- `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-features -- -D warnings`
- `cargo test --workspace --all-features`

comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`, CI config
**Target file(s)**:
- `gbf-train/Cargo.toml` (verify exact pin)
- `gbf-model/Cargo.toml` (verify burn is NOT a dependency)
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` or equivalent CI config

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "Burn version pinning", "drift CI check".

### Implementation Notes
- CI check that:
  1. Verifies `burn = "=X.Y.Z"` exact pin in gbf-train/Cargo.toml
  2. Verifies gbf-model does NOT import burn directly (grep for `use burn::` in gbf-model/src/)
  3. On Burn version bumps: runs QAT round-trip test to detect API drift
- Can be a simple shell script or a `#[test]` in gbf-train that checks the manifest
- The "QAT round-trip test": create a small model, train 1 step, export, verify export matches expected format

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                       # Compiles
# CI check script exits 0 on valid state:
./scripts/check_burn_pin.sh                     # Or equivalent
# CI check script exits 1 if burn pin is missing or gbf-model imports burn directly
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §II.1 Latency: This check should run in <5 seconds
- §III.2 Unit/Property Tests: The version pin check is a unit-level verification
- §IV.1: Exact version pins are a hard requirement per the constitution

title: T8.4: Implement Pareto frontier tracking and selection
description: Maintain a bounded Pareto frontier (max keep_frontier points). A point dominates another if it's better on ALL of: quality, conformance, projected_fit, schedule_cost. Non-dominated points form the frontier. When the frontier exceeds keep_frontier, evict the oldest non-frontier point. Selection: filter hard failures, then pick the frontier point with best quality. Emit training_selection.json with the full frontier, the selected checkpoint, and the domination reasoning.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- selection passes; Dominated points evicted; Frontier bounded; Selection filters hard failures

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/selection.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "Pareto frontier", "frontier tracking", "selection", "training_selection.json".

### Implementation Notes
- Bounded Pareto frontier over CheckpointFrontierPoints.
- Dominance: point A dominates point B if A is better on ALL metrics (quality, conformance, fit, cost).
- `pub struct ParetoFrontier { points: Vec<CheckpointFrontierPoint>, max_size: usize }`
- `pub fn add_point(&mut self, point: CheckpointFrontierPoint)` — adds if non-dominated, evicts dominated
- Selection: filter hard failures (fits=false, conformance.passes=false), pick frontier point with best quality.
- Output: `training_selection.json` with the selected checkpoint and rationale.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- selection            # Tests pass
# Verify: dominated points are evicted
# Verify: frontier size stays bounded (max_size)
# Verify: selection filters hard failures
# Verify: training_selection.json is valid JSON
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.2: Dominance check is a pure function
- §V.1: training_selection.json is the structured record of the decision

title: T4.3: Implement dense teacher checkpoint freezing
description: At the end of Phase A (or a manually specified step), freeze a copy of the model weights as the dense teacher. The frozen teacher: (a) serves as the distillation target for phases C-D (T4.4 logit distillation), (b) has no gradient (detached from optimizer), (c) is kept in memory alongside the student. The teacher weights are frozen via .detach() / requires_grad=false and cloned. Validation that the frozen copy matches the original is a log assertion. The EXPORT of the teacher as ReferenceModelBundle is a separate task (T4.3b) so that distillation can begin before the full export machinery is ready.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- teacher passes; Teacher weights independent of student; No gradient through teacher; Frozen by construction
close_reason:
Implemented the pre-export dense teacher freeze boundary in `gbf-train`.

Scope completed:
- Added `gbf_train::teacher` with `freeze_teacher`, `freeze_teacher_with_logging`, `FrozenTeacher`, `TeacherFreezeMetadata`, and the `DenseTeacherModel` boundary.
- `FrozenTeacher` exposes only immutable `forward_no_grad` and fingerprint accessors; it has no `&mut self` API.
- Freeze clones the source snapshot, detaches it, validates weight fingerprints match, rejects shared storage, and rejects snapshots that still require gradients.
- Teacher freeze logging is now freeze-local: `teacher_freeze` records source/frozen weight fingerprints and `weights_match`, not a future `ReferenceModelBundle` hash.
- Added subscriber-level producer coverage for `freeze_teacher_with_logging`.

Non-goals and moved ownership:
- Exporting the frozen teacher as `ReferenceModelBundle` remains bd-7lu.
- Phase A scheduler/training-loop adoption remains bd-14k/bd-1wd because no production training loop exists yet.
- Real full-model Burn integration remains with the future training model/scaffold owner; this bead proves the freeze boundary and toy executable contract, not an end-to-end Burn training loop.

## Support Matrix

| Behavior | Status | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- |
| In-memory frozen teacher snapshot | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- teacher` |
| Frozen teacher has no mutable API | supported | Type surface: `FrozenTeacher` exposes no `&mut self` methods |
| Clone independence after student mutation | supported for boundary test model | `freeze_teacher_clones_weights_independent_of_student_mutation` |
| Shared-storage clone rejection | supported | `freeze_teacher_rejects_snapshot_that_shares_student_storage` |
| Snapshot weight mutation during detach rejection | supported | `freeze_teacher_rejects_snapshot_that_changes_weights_during_detach` |
| No-grad snapshot guard | supported at boundary | `freeze_teacher_rejects_snapshot_that_still_requires_grad`; real Burn model adoption moved |
| Canonical `teacher_freeze` producer event | supported | `teacher_freeze_producer_is_captured_by_tracing_subscriber` |
| `ReferenceModelBundle` export/hash | moved | bd-7lu |
| Phase-boundary freeze integration | moved | bd-14k and bd-1wd |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Target crate compiles | `cargo check -p gbf-train` | Passed |
| Teacher freeze acceptance tests pass | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- teacher` | Passed; includes unit tests and the subscriber producer test selected by the filter |
| Clippy remains clean | `cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings` | Passed |
| Full crate tests remain clean | `cargo test -p gbf-train` | Passed |
| Subscriber logging catches producer fields | `cargo test -p gbf-train --test structured_logging` | Passed |

## Review Feedback Addressed

| Feedback item | Resolution | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Freeze required `reference_bundle_hash`, coupling bd-2hp to bd-7lu | Replaced with freeze-local source/frozen weight fingerprints and `weights_match`; bundle export hash remains bd-7lu | `teacher_freeze_producer_is_captured_by_tracing_subscriber` |
| Deep clone independence was not enforced | Added storage fingerprint validation and shared-storage rejection | `freeze_teacher_rejects_snapshot_that_shares_student_storage` |
| No-grad was only a convention | Added `teacher_requires_grad` boundary check and rejection | `freeze_teacher_rejects_snapshot_that_still_requires_grad` |
| Weight equality was checked but not logged | `teacher_freeze` event now records source/frozen fingerprints and `weights_match=true` | `teacher_freeze_producer_is_captured_by_tracing_subscriber` |
| Producer adoption was only helper-level | Added subscriber test that drives `freeze_teacher_with_logging` | `cargo test -p gbf-train --test structured_logging` |

## Feedback Harness Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Freeze event required a future `ReferenceModelBundle` hash | Event schema crossed into bd-7lu before export exists | Updated `logging-bead-closure`: producer events must not require downstream artifact identifiers before those artifacts exist | `teacher_freeze_producer_is_captured_by_tracing_subscriber` asserts freeze-local fields | `.agents/skills/logging-bead-closure/SKILL.md`; bd-7lu owns bundle hash/export |
| Deep-clone claim was self-attested by `Clone` | Boundary had no independent storage guard | Teacher freezer now requires storage fingerprints and rejects shared source/frozen storage | `freeze_teacher_rejects_snapshot_that_shares_student_storage` | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- teacher` |
| No-grad claim was only a toy output flag | Boundary did not check frozen snapshot grad state | Teacher freezer now rejects snapshots that still require gradients | `freeze_teacher_rejects_snapshot_that_still_requires_grad` | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- teacher` |
| Logging adoption could be overclaimed from helper tests | Existing subscriber test drove `TrainingLogEmitter` directly | Added a subscriber test that drives the real freeze producer entrypoint | `teacher_freeze_producer_is_captured_by_tracing_subscriber` | `cargo test -p gbf-train --test structured_logging` |

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/teacher.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "dense teacher", "checkpoint freezing", "ReferenceModelBundle", "Phase A".

### Implementation Notes
- At end of Phase A (DenseTeacherWarmup), freeze a copy of model weights as the dense teacher.
- Teacher requirements:
  - Deep clone of all model weights at the freeze point
  - No gradient tracking (detached from computation graph)
  - Immutable after freeze — never updated during remaining training
  - Used for logit distillation in Phases C and D
- Implementation: `pub fn freeze_teacher(model: &Model) -> FrozenTeacher`
- `FrozenTeacher` wraps the cloned model and provides `forward_no_grad()`.
- Export as ReferenceModelBundle is a SEPARATE task (T4.3b / bd-7lu).

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                         # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- teacher                # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings          # No warnings
# Verify: teacher weights are independent of student (modifying student doesn't affect teacher)
# Verify: teacher forward produces no gradients
# Verify: teacher weights are frozen — no update method exists
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: FrozenTeacher type has no `&mut self` methods — frozen by construction
- §I.2: Freeze is a pure operation (clone weights); the training loop boundary decides when

Source of truth: RFC §§3.1.4, 3.6. init hydrates .sym text through gbf_asm::symbols, stores a canonical SessionSymbolTable in .gbsess, and exec/inspect/scripts use the embedded table only. gb.symbol/gb.symbol_in_bank/gb.symbol_at/gb.symbol_at_in_bank are implemented with ambiguity refusal.
