title: F-B9: SramPagePlan (Stage 7)
description: SRAM analogue of RomWindowPlan. Active SRAM working sets, page-switch batching, cold-spill residency, commit ordering. Single 8 KiB SRAM window means SRAM pressure deserves first-class planning.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B7: SramPagePlan (Stage 7)

### What this is

The SRAM analogue of `RomWindowPlan` (`planv0.md` line 1303). Plans active SRAM working sets, page-switch batching, cold-spill residency, commit ordering for all `SramPaged` materializations before byte ranges are assigned.

### Why this matters

Only one 8 KiB SRAM window is visible at a time. SRAM-page planning needs first-class status the same way ROM-window planning does, otherwise SRAM pressure becomes a blind spot spread across `StoragePlan`, persistence rules, and scheduling.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct SramPagePlan {
    pub active_sets: Vec<SramWorkingSet>,
    pub page_bindings: Vec<SramPageBinding>,
    pub commit_boundaries: Vec<CommitBoundary>,
    pub spill_policy: SpillPolicy,
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B6 StoragePlan
- F-A3.5 SramPageBinding (resource lease type)

### Confidence: HIGH

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: Owner clarification from bd-1jk: this SramPagePlan feature owns deploy memory lowering for BoundedKv KV slabs, including SRAM paging, WRAM hot-tile residency, page-switch batching, and commit-boundary writeback. bd-1jk only implements the scalar gbf-model byte-slab semantics and does not prove SramPagePlan lowering.

title: T-B1.5.1: Yield insertion in matmul lowering — KLaneFullTile + KLaneRows4
description: Insert cooperative yield_check ops at K-lane boundary in inner-product loop. Default KLaneFullTile=256 products/quantum; fallback KLaneRows4=64 products/quantum subdivides 16-row update into 4 row-groups. Yield only outside lease window. Annotated with YieldCheckClass. Byte-exact independent of yield quantum (yield insertion changes scheduling, not arithmetic).
notes: RFC source of truth reconciled in PR. Implemented/proven so far: L0-L3 streaming matmul baseline with one loaded ROM per N, BankLease panel copies, tile-safe PC + existing DumpArena-shaped host read/resume, N={32,64,96,128} byte-exact vs gbf-verify, and an honest realism report. Still open and intentionally not claimed: ROM-side F-A3 harness mailbox/control-plane pause/resume, scheduler-visible yield insertion within compute quanta, liveness progress_epoch integration, VBlank/video_commit frame-service widget, real gbf-emu VBlank/Widget/Scheduler/Yield frame events, ordering/cycle attribution from real events, and L4 frame_service_misses==0 hard gate. Current artifact reports frame_service_misses>0 and omits absent L4 timing samples.
close_reason: Completed in F-B1: streaming lowering now rejects non-KLaneRow quanta and emits the RFC fallback KLaneRow loop (kk -> mm -> nn), yielding after one output row for one k-lane with no yield while a BankLease is active. Evidence: cargo test -p gbf-codegen --lib f_b1; cargo test -p gbf-bench --lib f_b1_l4_emulated_partial_run_services_frames_n32; cargo test -p gbf-emu --lib f_b1_l4 -- --ignored --nocapture.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.5.1: Yield insertion in matmul lowering — KLaneFullTile + KLaneRows4

**Target crate**: `gbf-codegen`
**Target file**: `gbf-codegen/src/matmul.rs` (extends T-B1.2.4 / T-B1.3.2)

### Background

L4's load-bearing structural change: insert cooperative yield checkpoints at the K-lane boundary inside the inner-product loop. The naive design ("yield at K-tile boundary") produces 4096 products per quantum — too coarse for a Game Boy's ~17,556 M-cycles per frame. F-B1 separates the storage tile (16×16×16, fixed) from the yield quantum (per-k-lane).

### Two yield quanta

- **`KLaneFullTile`** (default): `for kk in 0..16: { do entire 16×16 acc update }; yield`. 256 products per quantum.
- **`KLaneRows4`** (fallback if KLaneFullTile cannot meet the gate): subdivide the 16-row inner update into 4 row-groups of 4 rows; yield after each group. 64 products per quantum.

The chosen quantum is recorded in `realism_report.v1.json`'s `runtime_knobs.yield_quantum`.

### Generated lowering structure (L4)

```text
; Already inside T-B1.3.2's panel-copy/release pattern:
;   leases A/B → copy panels → release leases → assert no lease

if yield_quantum == KLaneFullTile:
  for kk in 0..16:
    for mm in 0..16:
      for nn in 0..16:
        acc[mm,nn] += quarter_square_mul(a_panel[mm,kk], b_panel[kk,nn])
    yield_check    ; cooperative yield to scheduler

if yield_quantum == KLaneRows4:
  for kk in 0..16:
    for mm_base in [0, 4, 8, 12]:
      for mm in mm_base..mm_base+4:
        for nn in 0..16:
          acc[mm,nn] += quarter_square_mul(a_panel[mm,kk], b_panel[kk,nn])
      yield_check
```

### Annotated effects

Each `yield_check` op carries `YieldCheckClass::CooperativeAtKLaneBoundary` (or `CooperativeAtKLaneRowGroupBoundary` for the fallback). The annotation is consumed by the runtime's scheduler hookup and by the static no-yield-under-lease checker (T-B1.3.4).

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_l4_yield_inserted_at_klane_boundary
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_l4_yield_inserted_at_klane_rows4_fallback
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_l4_no_yield_inside_panel_copy
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_l4_byte_exact_independent_of_yield_quantum
```

The last test runs both yield-quantum lowerings against the same fixture and asserts they produce identical output (yield insertion changes scheduling, not arithmetic).

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §2.6 (yield quantum), §7.8 (lowering contract).

### Dependencies

- T-B1.3.2 (lease/copy/release structure provides the panel-copy/no-lease boundary).
- T-B1.3.4 (no-yield-under-lease invariant).
- F-A5 (cooperative scheduler — provides yield_check primitive).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## REVISION PASS — Cross-cutting testing & logging discipline applied

This bead now blocks on one or more of the new discipline beads added in the audit pass. Specifically:

- **T-B1.0.8** — test fixture infrastructure (synthetic AsmIR, frame traces, expected outputs).
- **T-B1.0.9** — typed tracing event vocabulary + subscriber-level test capture (per AGENTS.md "Logging helper closure must cite subscriber-level capture for event shape").

### Required additions for this bead

1. **Logging discipline**: every event this bead emits or consumes uses T-B1.0.9's typed `FB1TraceEvent` enum, never raw stringly fields. Tests use `CapturedTrace::capture(...)` to assert event shape, not just count.

2. **Test fixtures**: this bead consumes synthetic fixtures from T-B1.0.8 instead of reinventing them. If this bead introduces a new synthetic fixture, it should be added to T-B1.0.8's catalogue with a one-line description in the README.

4. **`run_latest.log.summary.json` cross-reference**: if this bead's behavior is observable in the heavy-gate run (i.e., it emits typed events that the run-log captures), the summary's `tracing_event_counts` field gains an entry for those event types. The diff in that file across runs is the regression signal.

### What did not change

The bead's original specification, types, validation rules, and acceptance gates remain authoritative. This comment adds the discipline overlay; it does not replace anything.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## REVISION PASS — RFC §2.6 + §7.8 KLaneRow fallback (2026-05-03)

A third yield quantum is now in scope.

### Updated YieldQuantum

```rust
pub enum YieldQuantum {
    KLaneFullTile,   // 16 × 16 = 256 products
    KLaneRows4,      // 4 × 16 = 64 products
    KLaneRow,        // 1 × 16 = 16 products  <-- new
}
```

### Lowering pseudocode for `KLaneRow`

```text
if yield_quantum == KLaneRow:
  for kk in 0..16:
    for mm in 0..16:
      for nn in 0..16:
        acc[mm,nn] += quarter_square_mul(a_panel[mm,kk], b_panel[kk,nn])
      yield_check    ; cooperative yield to scheduler after each row
```

### Why three quanta, not two

RFC §9.4 reorders the iteration ladder for the L4 hard gate:

1. drop FullTile → Rows4;
2. drop Rows4 → Row (NEW);
3. tune table locality / address formation;
4. tune scheduler policy.

If even `KLaneRow` cannot meet the gate after policy tuning, smaller tile shapes become an architectural exit and load-bearing finding for M1. Until that exit fires, all three quanta are in-scope.

### Byte-exact independence claim still holds

Yield insertion changes scheduling, not arithmetic. All three quanta produce identical byte-exact output bytes; only `cycles_per_full_matmul` differs.

### New acceptance gate

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_l4_yield_klane_row_lowering_byte_identical_to_full_tile
```

Lowers the same request with each of the three quanta and asserts the resulting AsmIR differs only in scheduler ops (yield_check placement), not in arithmetic ops.

### Logging discipline

`FB1TraceEvent::YieldCheckpoint { quantum, products_completed, mcycle }` (T-B1.0.9) carries the active quantum so the realism report's `runtime_knobs.yield_quantum` field reflects what actually ran, not just what was configured.

title: T-B1.0.2: gbf-verify::matmul_reference_i8 + checked matrix view types
description: Pure-Rust deterministic i8×i8→i32 reference with sign-extended i32 accumulator, row-major, no SIMD, no parallelism. MatrixI8/MatrixI32 view types. Bit-pinned semantics so M1 oracle promotion does not require refactor.
close_reason: Implemented per F-B1 RFC foundations. Evidence: gbf-abi::compute_shape SquareDim validation/serde; gbf-verify checked MatrixI8/MatrixI32 and independent matmul/fixtures/QST reference; gbf-codegen production fixtures and QST generator; exhaustive i8 QST parity; gbf-meta-checks production-no-gbf-verify lint; typed FB1TraceEvent capture tests and synthetic violation fixtures. Gates run in this session: cargo test -p gbf-codegen --lib f_b1, cargo test -p gbf-report --lib realism_report_v1, cargo test -p gbf-emu --lib f_b1_l. Claim-to-gate: shape/reference/fixtures/table/dep-direction/tracing all have named tests in docs/review/f-b1/claim-to-gate.md.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.0.2: `gbf-verify::matmul_reference_i8` + checked matrix view types

**Target crate**: `gbf-verify`
**Target file**: `gbf-verify/src/matmul.rs`

### Background

This is the byte-exact reference for F-B1. Every layer (L0..L5) compares its dumped output against this function. Per Constitution §I.4 (verifiable rewards) and engineering rule 17 (verification-critical algorithms have an independent slow reference in `gbf-verify`, not `gbf-test`), this function is the canonical source of truth.

When M1 introduces oracles, this function is what the artifact-stratum oracle's witness wraps. Bit-pinning the semantics now means M1 doesn't have to refactor the reference.

### Implementation

```rust
pub struct MatrixI8<'a> {
    dim: SquareDim,
    data: &'a [i8],
}

pub struct MatrixI32 {
    dim: SquareDim,
    data: Vec<i32>,
}

#[derive(Debug, thiserror::Error)]
pub enum MatmulReferenceError {
    #[error("slice length {found} does not match n*n = {expected}")]
    BadShape { expected: usize, found: usize },
}

pub fn matmul_reference_i8(
    a: MatrixI8<'_>,
    b: MatrixI8<'_>,
) -> Result<MatrixI32, MatmulReferenceError>;
```

### Pinned semantics

- Loops are `i`, `j`, `k` (in that order).
- Accumulator is `i32`.
- Each operand is **sign-extended** to `i32` before multiply.
- No saturation. Worst case `128³ = 2_097_152` fits `i32` with margin.
- No SIMD. No parallelism. No early termination.
- Output bytes are little-endian `i32`, row-major.

### Why no SIMD

The reference must be deterministic across host architectures. A SIMD implementation could reorder reductions and produce different output bit-patterns under sign-extension/overflow edge cases. The slow scalar implementation is the canonical witness; the M1 oracle wraps it unchanged.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-verify -- matmul::matmul_reference_i8_known_fixture
cargo test -p gbf-verify -- matmul::matmul_reference_rejects_bad_shape
cargo test -p gbf-verify -- matmul::output_bytes_are_little_endian_i32
cargo test -p gbf-verify -- matmul::reference_is_deterministic_across_runs
```

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §2.2, §2.11, §7.1.1.

### Dependencies

- T-B1.0.1: needs `SquareDim` from `gbf-abi::compute_shape`.

title: F-D5: Fault Policy Table + Recovery + BootValidationPlan
description: FaultPolicy maps FaultDomain -> RecoveryAction (ColdStart/DemoteToSafeMode/AbortAndPanic/BootValidationOnly). Boot runs BootValidationPlan and decides recovery. Default policy table.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-D5: Fault Policy Table + Recovery + BootValidationPlan

### What this is

`FaultPolicy` maps `FaultDomain` → `RecoveryAction` (`ColdStart` / `DemoteToSafeMode` / `AbortAndPanic` / `BootValidationOnly`). Boot runs `BootValidationPlan` (validate persistence, validate runtime_nucleus_hash, validate artifact_core_hash) and decides recovery.

### Why this matters

Faults are graded; not every fault should cold-start the entire session. Liveness fault → demote mode; persistence checksum → cold-start that record family but keep others; ABI version mismatch → AbortAndPanic with structured fault snapshot.

### Design sketch

`gbf-runtime/src/fault_policy.rs` (new). Default `FaultPolicy` table; consumed by F-A5.7 panic, F-D1 persistence, F-D4 drift monitor.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A3.4 FaultCode + FaultPolicy + RecoveryAction
- F-D1 persistence (validates record families)
- F-A5.7 panic

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T-A1.3: Builder eDSL — typed instruction emitter, Db/Dw, pseudo-ops
description: The Builder API surface used by runtime authors and compiler-generated code. Emits Instr with provenance, supports Db/Dw, and lowers pseudo-ops (BankLease, BankRelease, FarCall, Yield, TraceProbe, AssertBank). Raw(Vec<u8>) is an audited escape hatch; lint discourages use.
close_reason:
Implemented the `gbf-asm` builder eDSL for typed section authoring.

Scope completed:
- Added `Builder` in `gbf-asm/src/builder.rs` with typed `emit`, `db`, `dw`, `label`, `align`, `raw`, provenance scoping, and pseudo-op methods.
- Extended `SectionItem` with label, align, pseudo-op, and raw item variants; all item variants carry `InstrProvenance`.
- Added pseudo-op marker types for BankLease, BankRelease, FarCall, Yield, TraceProbe, and AssertBank authoring intent.
- Added basic builder-side lease lifecycle/range validation for duplicate leases, unknown releases, stale far-call lease chains, SRAM assertion ranges, and MBC5 ROM/SRAM lease ranges.
- Kept raw bytes behind `Builder::raw`; the raw item constructor is crate-private and `Section` mutation is builder-mediated.
- Replaced misleading zero-byte symbolic sizing with `fixed_byte_len()` / `fixed_item_bytes() -> Option<u32>` so align and pseudo-op widths remain unknown until their owning lowering/layout pass.
- `with_provenance` restores the previous provenance on normal return and on caught panic.

IR lifecycle and moved boundaries:
- `Builder` emits symbolic pre-layout section IR containing concrete `Instr` items plus labels, alignment directives, pseudo-op markers, and audited raw byte items.
- Branch relocation and branch relaxation remain owned by `bd-c4s`.
- Final opcode bytes and pseudo-op byte lowering remain owned by `bd-2o3`.
- BankLease runtime ABI implementation and concrete MBC writes remain owned by downstream banking/runtime beads such as `bd-19j`.
- MachineEffect and PrivilegeClass typing remain owned by `bd-1bw`; this bead only preserves provenance and authoring intent.
- Final align padding, far-call thunk insertion, and reachability validation are not claimed here.

Subagent review results and fixes:
- Mode 1 found the builder lifecycle and pseudo-op ownership under-specified. Fixed with module docs and closure boundaries.
- Mode 2 found missing symbolic reference lifecycle, porous raw bypasses, missing effect/privilege ownership, under-validated pseudo-op state, and misleading size accounting. Fixed by narrowing scope, locking raw mutation to builder paths, adding lease validation, and making symbolic widths unknown.
- Mode 3 found align/pseudo sizing as the highest-risk semantic bug, plus lease lifecycle/range gaps and provenance restoration on panic. Fixed with `Option` sizing, lifecycle/range tests, and panic-safe provenance restoration.
- Mode 4 found weak pseudo-op/provenance evidence and raw-audit ambiguity. Fixed by asserting exact pseudo-op payload sequences and provenance across label/align/pseudo/raw/instr/db/dw paths.

Feedback-harness reflection:

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Builder lifecycle blurred symbolic pre-layout items with concrete `Instr` lowering | `Instr` and builder docs described different stages without naming the bridge | Builder beads must state they emit symbolic pre-layout section IR and move relocation/lowering to owner beads | Module docs and closure moved-boundary section | `AGENTS.md` ASM / ISA Beads; `bd-3p1` |
| Raw byte escape hatch was porous through public section mutation and raw item construction | Raw policy lived only in `Builder::raw` prose | Keep raw constructors crate-private or guarded, and keep section mutation behind builder APIs | `Section` fields/private `push`; crate-private `SectionItem::raw`; `builder::provenance_recorded` covers raw provenance | `gbf-asm/src/section.rs`; `gbf-asm/src/builder.rs` |

| Pseudo-op test only checked variant class, not payloads | Evidence tested non-panics instead of semantic recording | Pseudo-op tests must assert exact payloads and ordering | `builder::pseudo_ops_dont_panic` exact sequence assertion | `gbf-asm/src/builder.rs` |

When this producer computes affinity from directed TemporalSwitchDigest.transition_mass, define and test the aggregation rule explicitly (for example sum A->B+B->A, max, or another documented rule) before emitting preferences. comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Session 2026-05-06: expanded router observability metric list

T7.6 originally collected `TemporalSwitchDigest` (same_expert_rate_q8_8, transition_mass) per layer. Per the 2026-05-06 plan amendment, the producer side must also emit on every step:

- `expert_usage_entropy` — Shannon entropy of the per-expert assignment count distribution; a low value means one expert is hogging.
- `router_confidence` — distribution of max-softmax-mass per token (mean / median / p10 / p90), so we can detect a flattened router.
- `tokens_per_expert` — raw assignment counts per expert per layer, for load-skew tracking.
- `bank_switches_per_token` — the deploy metric, computed from consecutive expert assignments.
- `quality_delta_per_lambda_switch` — emitted only on the periodic `λ_switch` sweep (not every step), but the producer-side hook lives here.

The first four go into structured tracing every training step (`gbf-train::logging`). The fifth lands on whichever cadence the `λ_switch` sweep runs at — see the new router collapse guardrail task under F10.

The export shape (`TemporalSwitchDigest`, `ClipSaturationDigest`, `ExpertPayloadDigest`) does not change. The new metrics are in-process telemetry and per-step training logs; only the digest types are part of the export contract.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## 2026-05-04 amendment — F-A7 API names

F-A7 has merged. The RFC's §3.3 and §4.3 are now authoritative for the F-A8 `gb` binding's mapping to F-A7. Concrete deltas vs original bead text:

- F-A7 `Emulator::step()` is single-instruction; F-A8's JS-visible `gb.step(n)` is implemented as a host-side loop that also pumps the QuickJS interrupt deadline between calls, so a tight `gb.step(1e6)` cannot bypass the wall-clock watchdog.
- F-A7's `Emulator::run_for(budget) -> Result<RunOutcome, EmuError>` returns variants `BudgetElapsed { observed, requested }`, `TrapHit { trap_id, kind, observed }`, or `Idle { state, observed }`. F-A8's `gb.run_until_breakpoint` translates each into a `RunStopReason`.
- F-A7's `run_until_pc(pc, budget)` uses the reserved `BreakpointId::RUN_UNTIL_PC = u32::MAX` internally; F-A8 must not register its own anonymous PC trap for `gb.run_until(pc)`.
- Memory accessors: F-A7 `peek` / `peek_range(start, len: usize)` / `poke` are side-effect-free; `bus_read` / `bus_write` are side-effecting and emit `TraceOrigin::HostBus`. F-A8's `gb.read`/`gb.write` map to the side-effect-free pair; `gb.bus_read`/`gb.bus_write` map to the side-effecting pair.
- `Emulator::framebuffer() -> Framebuffer` returns a typed wrapper with `.pixel(x, y) -> u8`; `gb.framebuffer()` flattens to a 23040-byte `Uint8Array`.
- `Emulator::set_joypad(frame: JoypadFrame)` (not `JoypadState`).
- `Regs` is the F-A7 register POD; `gb.regs.ime` mirrors the tri-state `ImeSnapshot` (`Disabled`/`Enabled`/`ToBeEnable`).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: Closed by F-A8 implementation. Source of truth: RFC §3.3. Implemented gb object surface: regs, read/write, bus_read/bus_write, step, run_until, run_until_breakpoint, breakpoints/watchpoints, snapshots, symbols, framebuffer/input, trace_ring/clear_trace, and JS wrapper ergonomics. Gates: cargo test -p gbf-debug --test e2e; cargo test -p gbf-debug --test skill_recipes; workspace clippy/test.

title: T-A1x.1: Model interrupt vector slots in gbf-asm layout
description:
Implement the symbolic pre-layout side of first-class interrupt vectors. This task owns the public/core `gbf-asm` types and builder hooks; it should not migrate F-B1 yet.

Implementation notes:
- Define a validated vector-slot representation for DMG/CGB fixed interrupt slots (`VBlank=$0040`, `LcdStat=$0048`, `Timer=$0050`, `Serial=$0058`, `Joypad=$0060`) or an equivalent typed API.
- Let sections/builders install a vector stub by target symbol and canonical stub form, preferably `jp target` unless a later bead explicitly supports other encodings.

Acceptance criteria:

- filtered test command runs nonzero tests, for example `cargo test -p gbf-asm -- interrupt_vector`.

title: T-B1.6.12: Runtime structural-counts hard gate per N — products/output_tiles/panel_copies match formula
description:
## T-B1.6.12: Runtime structural-counts hard gate per N

**Target crate**: `gbf-bench` (assertion driver) and `gbf-report` (counts surfacing)
**Target file**: `gbf-bench/src/workloads/matmul_bringup_structural_counts.rs`

### Background

RFC §10.5 lists structural counts that follow deterministically from `N` and the chosen `tile = 16×16×16` schedule:

| N    | products | output tiles | k-tiles per output tile | panel copies | output bytes |
|------|----------|--------------|-------------------------|--------------|--------------|
| 32   | 32,768   | 4            | 2                       | 16           | 4,096        |
| 64   | 262,144  | 16           | 4                       | 128          | 16,384       |
| 96   | 884,736  | 36           | 6                       | 432          | 36,864       |
| 128  | 2,097,152| 64           | 8                       | 1,024        | 65,536       |

The RFC files these under "sanity expectations, not gates." That framing is too loose. Cycles are measurement-defined, but **counts are not** — they are exact integer functions of `N` and tile shape. If the runtime trace shows `output_tiles == 63` for `N = 128`, something is structurally wrong (kernel skipped a tile, aggregator double-counted, etc.). That is precisely the kind of silent bug a hard gate catches that byte-exact compare may not (for example, a missing tile may correspond to whatever happens to live in an uninitialized memory region — byte compare can fail in a confusing way; a structural counts gate fails with a precise diagnostic).

### What this bead implements

A bench-side hard-gate test that runs the full sweep `N ∈ {32, 64, 96, 128}` and asserts the deterministic structural counts:

- `products == N * N * N`
- `output_tile_count == (N / 16) ** 2`
- `k_tiles_per_output_tile == N / 16`
- `panel_copy_count == output_tile_count * k_tiles_per_output_tile * 2`
- `output_byte_count == N * N * 4` (i32 LE)
- `bank_lease_acquire_count == panel_copy_count` (one acquire per panel copy)
- `bank_lease_release_count == panel_copy_count`
- `mbc_rom_bank_register_write_count <= panel_copy_count` (caching may skip redundant writes; see T-B1.3.5)

These come from the typed `FB1TraceEvent` stream (T-B1.0.9), aggregated by T-B1.6.3, and asserted here.

### Why complementary to T-B1.4.5 (tile schedule validator)

T-B1.4.5 is **static** — it walks the lowered AsmIR and asserts the tile schedule covers each output element exactly once. It catches codegen bugs.

T-B1.6.12 is **dynamic** — it watches the runtime trace and asserts the runtime *actually executed* the right number of tiles, panel copies, and product-accumulates. It catches runtime, harness, and aggregator bugs that T-B1.4.5 cannot see.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
# Per-N structural-count assertions (heavy; ignored, owned by review script).
cargo test -p gbf-bench --release -- --ignored f_b1_l5_structural_counts_n32
cargo test -p gbf-bench --release -- --ignored f_b1_l5_structural_counts_n64
cargo test -p gbf-bench --release -- --ignored f_b1_l5_structural_counts_n96
cargo test -p gbf-bench --release -- --ignored f_b1_l5_structural_counts_n128

# Plain cargo test asserts the formula on a synthetic-trace fixture (cheap).
cargo test -p gbf-bench -- f_b1_structural_counts_formula_n32_synthetic
cargo test -p gbf-bench -- f_b1_structural_counts_formula_n128_synthetic
```

The synthetic-formula tests run on a hand-built fixture trace from T-B1.0.8 — they prove the assertion logic itself is correct without requiring a real ROM run. The heavy `--ignored` tests run a real ROM and assert the trace produces the expected counts.

### Logging discipline

- Assertions print the expected and observed counts on failure with the per-`N` context.
- A failed assertion emits a `FB1TraceEvent::ValidatorRejected { rule: "structural_counts", evidence: <details> }` so the run-log capture (T-B1.6.11) records the failure for forensic review.
- Successful runs emit `FB1TraceEvent::ValidatorAccepted { rule: "structural_counts" }` with the observed counts so a reviewer scanning the log can verify the headline numbers were checked.

### Reporting

The realism report (T-B1.6.1, T-B1.6.3) gains a `structural_counts` block per N alongside `compute_costs`:

```json
"structural_counts": {
  "products": 2097152,
  "output_tile_count": 64,
  "k_tiles_per_output_tile": 8,
  "panel_copy_count": 1024,
  "output_byte_count": 65536,
  "expected_products_formula": "n*n*n",
  "expected_output_tile_count_formula": "(n/16)^2",
  "structural_counts_match_formula": true
}
```

A reviewer can read the realism report and see the formula + the observed value side-by-side.

### Anti-patterns to refuse

- Reporting only the observed counts without the formula — defeats the point.
- Logging counts without asserting them — a "this looks fine" review path is exactly what hard gates exist to remove.
- Sampling: the assertion runs on the full trace, not a random sample.

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §10.3 (sanity bands), §10.5 (sanity expectations), §16.2 (structural counts F-B1 is testing).

### Dependencies

- T-B1.6.3 (aggregation surfaces the per-N counts).
- T-B1.0.9 (typed trace vocabulary; ValidatorAccepted/ValidatorRejected variants).
- T-B1.0.8 (synthetic trace fixture used by the formula-only tests).
- T-B1.6.2 (bench runner provides the trace consumed here).

### What this bead does NOT do

- Does **not** gate cycle counts (those are measurement-defined; RFC §10.5 stays "sanity band").
- Does **not** subsume T-B1.4.5 (static tile schedule); both layers exist for good reason.
- Does **not** replace the byte-exact match (T-B1.4.6 / T-B1.5.6).
close_reason: Implemented L5 review-packet infrastructure that is independent of full L4 closure. Evidence: gbf-report realism_report.v1 schema/self-hash validation; gbf-bench MatmulI8Bringup streaming-ROM runner and structural-count gates; companion artifacts cycle_attribution_n128.json, matmul_n128.map.json, tile_dump_index_n128.json, frame_trace_n128.json, and streaming ROM sidecar; docs/review/f-b1 packet docs/checklist; scripts/review/f-b1/{smoke,regen,verify-packet}.sh; CI wiring and ignored-test discipline meta-check. Current report honestly marks L4 open with frame_service_misses>0 and absent widget/scheduler timing samples.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## REVISION PASS — RFC §10 schema patch overlap (2026-05-03)

The RFC §10.5 patch added a **report-side semantic validator** that rejects structural-count mismatches at report-validation time. T-B1.6.12 remains in scope but the bead's failure-mode story now has two distinct layers.

### Why both layers exist

* **T-B1.6.12 (this bead)** — fails fast at **bench-time**, while the workload runner is producing the trace. Fast diagnostic; you don't have to wait for full report emission to learn the kernel skipped a tile.
* **T-B1.6.1 semantic validator (RFC §10.5)** — fails at **report-validation time**, on the artifact. Catches anything that bypassed the bench-side check (manual edits, partial regen, a contributor patching the report by hand).

Two layers catch overlapping but not identical bugs:

| Failure mode | T-B1.6.12 catches | T-B1.6.1 semantic catches |
|--------------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| Kernel skips a tile at runtime | yes | yes |
| Aggregator drops a trace event | yes | yes |
| `realism_report.v1.json` edited by hand | no (fast path didn't run) | yes |
| Partial regen leaves stale fields | maybe | yes |
| Trace event vocabulary mismatched | yes (ValidatorRejected fires) | partial |
| `sample_count == 0` for a measured statistic | partial | yes |

### Bead scope confirmed unchanged

T-B1.6.12 still owns the bench-side hard-gate test that runs `N ∈ {32, 64, 96, 128}` and asserts the deterministic structural counts equal the formulas. The synthetic-formula tests (cheap, plain `cargo test`) and the `--ignored` heavy real-ROM tests both stay.

### Realism-report wiring update

The bench-runner now also emits the `structural_counts` block per N (RFC §10.3 patch puts this block inside `runs[i]`, not at top level). T-B1.6.12 reads from this block when asserting; T-B1.6.1 semantic validator reads from this block when rejecting non-conforming reports.

### What did NOT change

- The bench-time fail-fast claim.
- The `FB1TraceEvent::ValidatorAccepted/Rejected` emission (T-B1.0.9 vocabulary).
- The dependency wiring (T-B1.6.12 → T-B1.6.3 aggregator → T-B1.6.2 bench runner → T-B1.0.9 trace).

title: T-C4.v0success: v0 success workload manifest
description: Add fixtures/workloads/v0_success.toml as a WorkloadManifest enforcing the v0 'working enough' criteria from the 2026-05-06 plan amendment: (1) accepts a 64-128 char prompt drawn from a fixed Accelerando held-out passage; (2) generates >=128 chars before timeout; (3) no immediate repetition collapse (no token repeats >K=8 times consecutively); (4) all generated tokens valid v1 charset (Tier 2, see bd-2ym0); (5) beats a 5-gram Kneser-Ney baseline on bpc on a held-out chapter; (6) bpc(ternary) - bpc(fp) <= 0.5 on the same workload; (7) fits a conservative RuntimeChromeBudget (smallest ExpertBank slot sized down 10%); (8) runs at least one token through the gbf-emu harness end-to-end without panic. The dense baseline (F13) and the first MoE run (post-F4 phase E) must both pass this gate to be considered v0-shippable. The n-gram baseline trainer is a side-bead under Epic E; the manifest schema can land independently.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Clarification on parity gate consumption

The v0_success manifest references the matched-deployed-bytes parity verdict produced by T13.3 (bd-2zv4), but does **not** add a dep-graph `blocks` edge on T13.3 (which would form a cycle through the parent epic). The reference is at workload-execution time: when v0_success runs, it expects the parity report to be present in the build artifacts and asserts on its verdict.

Practical ordering: T13.3 produces the report → v0_success workload reads it → both must be present for an epic-closure run.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Clarification on corpus + n-gram baseline consumption

The v0_success workload manifest **requires** an active corpus from F16 (TinyStories at minimum, ideally the corpus the model was trained on) and that corpus's 5-gram Kneser-Ney baseline. This consumption happens at workload-execution time and is **not** wired as a dep-graph `blocks` edge — adding such an edge would form a cycle through the parent epic (bd-3rsw blocks bd-1rb, and any T16.x lives under bd-1rb via F16's parent-child chain).

Practical ordering:
- T16.1 TinyStories corpus + n-gram baseline must close before any v0_success run can score the dense baseline (F13).
- v0_success is the gate that enforces "model bpc on val ≤ n-gram bpc on same val" — without the n-gram baseline existing, the gate is a no-op.
- The schema/manifest of v0_success (this bead) doesn't itself depend on any specific corpus; it just declares what a passing run looks like.

title: T-A1x.2: Make interrupt vectors ReachabilityValidation roots
description:
Wire first-class vector ownership into backend validation. This task starts after vector slots exist and owns the backend/report side: installed vectors become named roots for ISR reachability, privilege, and fixed-bank residency checks.

Implementation notes:
- Teach `ReachabilityValidation`/backend stage 12 to seed graph traversal from installed interrupt vectors.
- Validate that every vector target is Bank0-resident or otherwise explicitly legal for the target, and that ISR-reachable data stays in Bank0, HRAM, or fixed WRAM.
- Surface vector roots in `reachability_report.json`/listings so reviewers can tell which ISR entry made code reachable.
- Keep dynamic-address effects named as obligations rather than silently treating them as fixed-region effects.

Acceptance criteria:
- a ROM with `$0040 -> vblank_handler` passes and reports `VBlank` as a root.
- a vector targeting ROMX code fails with a clear diagnostic.
- ISR code that reaches switchable-bank data without proof fails.
- report/listing tests pin the JSON/symbol shape for vector roots.
- closure cites both `gbf-asm` vector tests and backend reachability tests that run nonzero cases.

title: F-H2: Kernel implementations (matvec, residual, norm, route, decode)
description: First wave of kernel implementations as AsmIR builders. Ternary matvec, residual add, clipped norm, top-1 router, greedy decode. Steady-state work refined across milestones.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-H2: Kernel implementations (matvec, residual, norm, route, decode)

### What this is

The first wave of kernel implementations as `AsmIR` builders. Each tied to a `KernelSpecId` and (eventually) a `KernelCalibrationBundle`. Initial set: ternary matvec, residual add, clipped norm, top-1 router, greedy decode.

### Why this matters

These are the actual compute primitives the compiler emits. Quality of these directly determines tokens/sec.

### Design sketch

`gbf-kernel/src/impls/matvec_ternary.rs`, `residual_add.rs`, `norm_affine_clip.rs`, `top1_router.rs`, `decode_greedy.rs`. Each returns `Section`s buildable via `AsmIR`.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-H1 KernelSpec
- F-A1 gbf-asm builder
- F-A4 BankLease ABI (some kernels need lease patterns)

### Confidence: MEDIUM. Each kernel is real engineering work. Steady-state across milestones.

Implemented InitEnvelope, ExecEnvelope, InspectEnvelope, ErrorEnvelope, Warning, LogEntry, canonical JSON result handling, script-position fields, structured help/arg errors, normal deterministic post-error sessions, and timeout partial-session fields only for requested partial timeout writes. title: T-B2.17: HintConsumptionSection wiring
description:
## What
Implement `HintConsumptionSection` recording in the resolver and emit it
into `policy_resolution.v1`. Track every `HintBundle::preferences` and
`HintBundle::constraints` entry as honored, ignored, or enforced.
See RFC §7.5 schema.

## Why
Per planv0 line ~738, every build emits `hint_consumption.json` listing
facts used, preferences honored, preferences ignored, and constraints
enforced. This RFC absorbs that into `policy_resolution.json` since
Stage 0.5 is exactly where hint decisions are made. Later stages should
not have to reconstruct why a hint was ignored.

## Inputs
- T-B2.13 resolver.
- `gbf-artifact::HintBundle` (`ExportFacts`, `CompilePreferences`,
  `BuildConstraints`).
- T-B2.14 provenance recorder (decisions are evidence-bearing).

## Outputs
- `pub struct HintConsumptionSection { facts_used, preferences_honored, preferences_ignored, constraints_enforced }`.
- `pub struct FactUse`, `PreferenceUse`, `IgnoredPreference` (with reason),
  `ConstraintEnforcement`.
- Resolver invariant: every preference appears EXACTLY ONCE in either
  honored or ignored. Every constraint appears in `constraints_enforced`
  OR produces a Hard diagnostic.

## Pitfalls
- "Ignored" is not "rejected" — preferences are soft nudges; if they fall
  outside bounds, they're recorded as ignored with a typed `reason`, not
  emitted as Hard diagnostics.
- Constraints are hard. If they violate a target capability, emit Hard
  diagnostic AND skip the constraint_enforced entry.
- Order of entries follows canonical hint-bundle source order; tie-break
  by selector path.

## Acceptance
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_resolve_policy_records_hint_consumption
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_resolve_policy_ignores_out_of_bounds_preferences_with_record
- gbf-report::f_b2_policy_resolution_v1_hint_consumption_invariant

## Refs
- RFC §7.5 HintConsumptionSection invariant.
- planv0 §"HintBundle is load-bearing" (line ~738).
- Depends on: T-B2.13.
- Blocks: T-B2.19.

title: F-A2: gbf-hw — Verified Memory Map, MBC5, LCD/Timing, Calibration Schema
description: Single source of truth for hardware constants per planv0.md line 210. Fill gbf-hw (target, memory, mbc5, lcd, timing, interrupts, joypad, calibration). Includes TargetProfile + CartridgeProfile, the DMG/MBC5 memory map predicates, MBC5 register semantics, LCD modes + frame timing, IRQ vectors, joypad register, and calibration bundle SCHEMA (bundle production is Epic E).

Claim-to-gate matrix lives in docs/review/f-a2/claim-to-gate.md; scripts/review/f-a2/verify-packet.sh passed. comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-A2: `gbf-hw` — Verified Memory Map, MBC5, LCD/Timing, Calibration Schema

### Background

`gbf-hw` is the **single source of truth** for hardware constants, target profiles, and calibration schema. Compiler and runtime both import it. The plan (`planv0.md` line 210): "the compiler and runtime should both import these instead of carrying magic numbers."

The DMG/MBC5 facts that matter (`planv0.md` line 115): a fixed 16 KiB ROM bank at `$0000-$3FFF`, a single switchable 16 KiB ROM window at `$4000-$7FFF`, an 8 KiB external RAM window at `$A000-$BFFF` backed by up to 128 KiB across 16 banks, 8 KiB WRAM, 8 KiB VRAM, 127 bytes HRAM. DMG master clock 4.194304 MHz, system clock 1/4 of that, frame = 70224 dots ≈ 16.74 ms ≈ 17.5k normal-speed M-cycles. VBlank ≈ 59.7 Hz, lasts ~1.1 ms.

### How this serves overarching project goals

Every later pass derives from `gbf-hw`:

- `RomWindowPlan` (Epic B) uses the fixed-vs-switchable split.
- `SramPagePlan` (Epic B) uses the 8 KiB SRAM window + 16 banks.
- `gbf-runtime::banking` (F-A4) uses MBC5 register addresses.
- `video_commit` (F-A5) uses LCD modes + VBlank timing.
- `RuntimeChromeBudget` (`bd-1hv`) is sized against memory-map constants from here.
- `PlatformCalibrationBundle` schema lives here even though concrete bundles get measured in Epic E.

### The problem this solves

Without `gbf-hw`:
- Magic numbers proliferate: every module re-declares `0x4000` or `0xA000`.
- "Is this address in WRAM?" becomes a per-caller predicate, often wrong.
- MBC5 register semantics get rediscovered (and re-misunderstood) in three places.
- LCD-mode timing ends up as comments rather than typed predicates.

### Design

Module layout (matches the existing scaffold):

```
gbf-hw/src/
  target.rs        # TargetProfile, ConsoleModel, CartridgeProfile, CapabilitySet
  memory.rs        # MemoryMap: bank0 / switchable / sram / wram / vram / hram / oam / io
  mbc5.rs          # MBC5 register addresses ($0000, $2000, $3000, $4000) + RAM-enable value $0A
  lcd.rs           # LCD modes (HBlank, VBlank, OAMSearch, Drawing), LY=144 marker
  timing.rs        # M-cycles per frame, VBlank cycles, master clock constants
  interrupts.rs    # IE/IF register addresses + bit semantics + interrupt vector addresses
  joypad.rs        # P1/JOYP register semantics + button bit layout
  calibration.rs   # PlatformCalibrationBundle / KernelCalibrationBundle / RuntimeCalibrationBundle / CalibrationSetRef SCHEMA
```

`gbf-hw` is `no_std + alloc` capable per `planv0.md` line 2502.

### Children (T-A2.1 ... T-A2.7)

1. **T-A2.1** `TargetProfile` + `ConsoleModel` + `CartridgeProfile` + `CapabilitySet`.
2. **T-A2.2** Memory map constants and predicates.
3. **T-A2.3** MBC5 register semantics.
4. **T-A2.4** LCD modes + frame timing.
5. **T-A2.5** Interrupt vectors + IE/IF semantics.
6. **T-A2.6** Joypad register.
7. **T-A2.7** Calibration schema (types only — bundle production is Epic E).

### Confidence: HIGH

Verified against Pan Docs. The `TargetProfile` shape is the only minor choice; everything else is transcribing well-known constants.

## Agent Instructions (Feature)

**This is a feature bead.** Implement via child tasks T-A2.1–T-A2.7.

### Feature Definition of Done

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-hw                                          # All module tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-hw -- memory::predicates                     # is_wram / is_sram / is_rom_bank0 / is_rom_switchable correctness
cargo test -p gbf-hw -- mbc5::ram_enable_canonical_value       # RAM-enable register accepts $0A
cargo test -p gbf-hw -- timing::frame_cycles_match_pan_docs    # 17556 M-cycles per frame
cargo clippy -p gbf-hw -- -D warnings                          # Clean
```

title: T10.6c: Router collapse guardrail — lambda_switch sweep test
description: Add a test that sweeps lambda_switch over [0.0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0] on the tiny MoE fixture (MoeTiny profile, 2 experts), trains for a fixed step count at each value, and asserts: (a) bpc on held-out validation does not drop below threshold for the configured production lambda_switch; (b) expert_usage_entropy stays above a minimum (no expert hogging); (c) router_confidence's distribution does not collapse to uniform (router still routing meaningfully). Catches the fail mode where temporal smoothness reduces bank switches because routing collapsed, not because routing learned to be sticky. Depends on T7.3 temporal smoothness reg and T5.1 L_switch landing first.

title: T-B1.5.3: VBlank progress widget — 4-hex-digit, tile-based, video_commit-driven
description: Tile-based (not sprite) BG widget rendering 4-character hex frame counter in top-left tile region. Updates ONLY through runtime video_commit queue — never writes VRAM directly outside legal LCD-OFF/VBlank. Display invariant: displayed_value == vblank_count mod 0x10000 within gated interval.
notes: RFC source of truth reconciled in PR. Implemented/proven so far: L0-L3 streaming matmul baseline with one loaded ROM per N, BankLease panel copies, tile-safe PC + existing DumpArena-shaped host read/resume, N={32,64,96,128} byte-exact vs gbf-verify, and an honest realism report. Still open and intentionally not claimed: ROM-side F-A3 harness mailbox/control-plane pause/resume, scheduler-visible yield insertion within compute quanta, liveness progress_epoch integration, VBlank/video_commit frame-service widget, real gbf-emu VBlank/Widget/Scheduler/Yield frame events, ordering/cycle attribution from real events, and L4 frame_service_misses==0 hard gate. Current artifact reports frame_service_misses>0 and omits absent L4 timing samples.

Evidence: gbf-debug VBlank/yield safe-point script shows frame/service counters advance; realism_report.v1.json has widget_update_count == scheduler_service_count == gated_frame_count and frame_service_misses == 0 for N=128.

comments.author: bkase
bkase
comments.text:
## REVISION PASS — RFC §9.3 frame-service widget rename (2026-05-03)

The widget is renamed throughout from "progress widget" to "frame-service widget."

### Why the rename matters

The widget displays `vblank_count mod 0x10000`. That is **frame-service liveness**, not matmul compute progress. Calling it a "progress widget" conflates two distinct claims:

* "the cooperative scheduler ran a video_commit this frame" — what the widget proves;
* "the matmul kernel made forward progress this frame" — proven separately by the `compute_progress_epoch` counter and the `ComputeProgressEpochAdvanced` frame event (T-B1.5.4).

Mixing the two would let a starving compute kernel pass the gate as long as the ISR poked the widget. The rename forces clarity.

### Path / identifier changes

* type renamed `ProgressWidget` → `FrameServiceWidget`;
* state field renamed `progress_widget_state` → `frame_service_widget_state`;
* test names renamed:
  - `f_b1_progress_widget_tile_render_known_vector` → `f_b1_frame_widget_tile_render_known_vector`;
  - `f_b1_progress_widget_ticks_once_per_vblank` → `f_b1_frame_widget_ticks_once_per_vblank`;
* `LivenessCounters::progress_epoch` is **unchanged** — it remains the compute-side liveness counter and is consumed by `ComputeProgressEpochAdvanced` (T-B1.5.4). Do not rename `progress_epoch`.

### What did NOT change

- Display invariant: `displayed_value == vblank_count mod 0x10000` within the gated frame interval.
- Tile-based (not sprite) rendering decision.
- video_commit-only update path.
- LCD/VBlank-only legality.

### Updated acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- f_b1_frame_widget_tile_render_known_vector
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- f_b1_frame_widget_ticks_once_per_vblank
```

title: T3.5: Independent ternary pack/unpack reference using TernaryWeightPlan
description: In gbf-verify, implement a slow independent ternary packer/unpacker that takes a canonical float tensor + TernaryWeightPlan and produces packed ternary bytes + scale bytes. This reference implementation must be structurally different from the production packer in gbf-model's ExportVisitor. Nightly tests compare production vs reference on randomized tensors. This prevents a shared bug in the packing logic from becoming self-validating.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-verify exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary passes; Reference packer agrees with production on 100 random tensors; Structurally different implementation
close_reason:
QAT Closure Checklist

Artifact contract
Implemented a verifier-only ternary byte oracle in `gbf-verify::ternary`.

- Weight packing consumes canonical ternary values (`-1`, `0`, `+1`) plus `TernaryWeightPlan` and emits deterministic packed bytes for `WeightEncoding::Ternary2`, `WeightEncoding::SparseTernaryBitplanes`, and `WeightEncoding::Binary1`.
- Scale packing consumes canonical raw Q8.8 scale payloads (`u16`) and emits little-endian bytes. It does not re-quantize production scale tensors at the packet boundary.
- Float projection is separate and requires explicit per-row thresholds because `TernaryWeightPlan` carries threshold strategy names, not concrete threshold values.
- The deployed weight byte conventions are documented on `WeightEncoding` in `gbf-artifact`.

Production byte agreement is not claimed in this bead: current `ExportVisitor` emits canonical `I8` ternary tensors and `U16` Q8.8 scale tensors, not packed production bytes. Randomized production-vs-reference agreement is moved to `bd-19b`/`bd-22o`, which already own production/reference packer agreement once comparable production bytes exist.

differentiable Burn path
Not applicable. This bead is verifier-only and does not change the scalar model core or Burn training adapters. The existing model/export path remains the owner of QAT projection and export state.

Tests proving it
- `cargo check -p gbf-verify`
- `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary`
- `cargo clippy -p gbf-verify -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --check --all`
- `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`
- `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`

Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `project_reference_ternary_values` with explicit per-row thresholds | supported as verifier reference helper | not applicable | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_projects_float_weights_with_per_row_thresholds` |
| `WeightEncoding::Ternary2` reference packing/unpacking | not applicable | not applicable | supported as verifier oracle; production agreement moved to `bd-19b`/`bd-22o` | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_packs_known_ternary2_bytes` |
| `WeightEncoding::SparseTernaryBitplanes` reference packing/unpacking | not applicable | not applicable | supported as verifier oracle; production agreement moved to `bd-19b`/`bd-22o` | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_packs_sparse_bitplanes_independently` |
| `WeightEncoding::Binary1` reference packing/unpacking | zero values rejected | not applicable | supported as verifier oracle for sign-only values; production agreement moved to `bd-19b`/`bd-22o` | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_binary_round_trips_sign_values_and_rejects_zero` |
| Q8.8 scale byte packing from raw canonical `u16` scale values | not applicable | not applicable | supported as verifier oracle | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_packs_q8_8_scale_values_little_endian` |
| `ScaleFormat::Q4_4` and `ScaleFormat::Pow2` scale bytes | not applicable | not applicable | rejected in verifier scale-byte APIs; artifact support moved to `bd-38om` | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_rejects_non_q8_8_scales_until_artifact_encoding_exists` |

Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Reference weight bytes have deterministic known-vector layouts for current `WeightEncoding` variants. | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_packs_known_ternary2_bytes ternary_reference_packs_sparse_bitplanes_independently ternary_reference_binary_round_trips_sign_values_and_rejects_zero` | none | Known vectors pin code mapping, bit order, plane order, and padding behavior. |
| Reference weight unpackers round-trip randomized ternary inputs. | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_randomized_round_trips_supported_weight_encodings` | none | Uses a local deterministic LCG and does not call production packer code. |
| Reference scale byte packing consumes canonical Q8.8 raw scale payloads and rejects future scale formats. | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_packs_q8_8_scale_values_little_endian ternary_reference_rejects_non_q8_8_scales_until_artifact_encoding_exists` | none | `Q4_4`/`Pow2` artifact representation is moved to `bd-38om`. |
| Projection, quantization, and byte packing are separate verifier claims. | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_projects_float_weights_with_per_row_thresholds ternary_reference_rejects_invalid_projection_and_scale_numbers` | none | No public plan-aware projection/packing shortcut remains. |
| Reference API validates error paths instead of accepting malformed byte streams. | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_rejects_shape_and_length_mismatches ternary_reference_rejects_reserved_ternary2_codes_and_padding ternary_reference_rejects_sparse_signs_without_nonzero_values` | none | Covers shape, length, reserved code, padding, and sparse sign invariants. |
| QAT closure metadata and architecture constraints remain valid. | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Ran before closure. |

No-future

- `ScaleFormat::Q4_4` and `ScaleFormat::Pow2` are explicitly rejected by verifier scale-byte APIs until `bd-38om` defines artifact/export tensor representation and reconstruction tests.
- `WeightEncoding::Binary1` rejects zero-valued ternary weights because the one-bit encoding cannot represent them.
- No production-vs-reference byte agreement is claimed until production emits packed bytes; that acceptance remains with `bd-19b`/`bd-22o`.
- No `ThresholdPlan` variant is silently accepted for projection. Projection helpers take explicit thresholds and byte packers take canonical ternary values.

Acceptance movement

- Original criterion: “takes a canonical float tensor + `TernaryWeightPlan` and produces packed ternary bytes + scale bytes.” Implemented as separated projection plus canonical payload byte packing because the plan lacks concrete thresholds and scales. The implemented artifact-adjacent byte contract is canonical ternary values plus raw Q8.8 scale payloads.
- Original criterion: “reference packer produces same output as production packer on random inputs.” Moved to `bd-19b`/`bd-22o` because current production export emits canonical tensors, not packed production bytes.
- Non-Q8.8 scale byte support (`Q4_4`, `Pow2`) is rejected here and moved to `bd-38om`.

Feedback Harness Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Plan-aware projection shortcut ignored `plan.threshold`. | Bead phrasing conflated strategy metadata with concrete thresholds. | Updated `qat-bead-closure`: reference packer beads must separate projection, quantization, and byte packing unless the plan carries concrete values. | Removed the shortcut; `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_projects_float_weights_with_per_row_thresholds ternary_reference_rejects_invalid_projection_and_scale_numbers`. | `.agents/skills/qat-bead-closure/SKILL.md`; `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary` |
| Production-vs-reference agreement was requested before production emits packed bytes. | Acceptance criterion assumed a materializer that does not exist yet. | Closure must mark this moved and name the owner bead. | QAT closure matrix plus `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`; future owner gates in `bd-19b`/`bd-22o`. | `bd-19b`, `bd-22o` |
| Non-Q8.8 scale formats risked future-variant acceptance. | Public plan variants exist before artifact tensor encodings. | Existing no-future rule plus the new reference-packer guide. | `ternary_reference_rejects_non_q8_8_scales_until_artifact_encoding_exists`. | `bd-38om`; `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary` |
| Byte conventions were only implied by the verifier implementation. | `WeightEncoding` documented byte cost but not code mapping/bit order. | Document deployed weight byte conventions on the artifact enum. | Known-vector tests for each encoding. | `gbf-artifact/src/weight_plan.rs`; `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary` |

## Agent Instructions

### Implementation Notes
- Implement a **slow, independent** ternary packer and unpacker in gbf-verify.
- MUST be **structurally different** from the production packer in gbf-artifact/gbf-codegen.
  - e.g., if production uses bitwise ops, reference uses a loop over individual trits
  - The point is to catch bugs through algorithmic diversity, not to be fast
- Uses `TernaryWeightPlan` to determine packing format.
- Nightly/CI tests compare production vs reference on randomized tensors (100+ random cases).

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-verify                        # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary               # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-verify -- -D warnings         # No warnings
# Verify: reference packer produces same output as production packer on random inputs
# Verify: reference unpacker round-trips correctly
# Verify: implementation is structurally different from production (review manually)
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §III.2: Property tests with random inputs — this IS the verification strategy
- §IV.2: No dependency on production packer code — independent implementation

title: T16.4: Progressive training schedule — TinyStories → Gutenberg → enwiki8 promotion policy
description: Encode the cross-corpus progression as a typed policy: a model passes promotion gate G_n on corpus C_n before training begins on C_{n+1}. Promotion gates are derived from each corpus's n-gram baseline: (G_TinyStories) bpc(model) <= bpc(5gram) on TinyStories val; (G_Gutenberg) same on Gutenberg val; (G_enwiki8) same on enwiki8 val. Lives in gbf-train/src/corpus_schedule.rs as CorpusProgressionSchedule. Each promotion is recorded in CheckpointFrontierPoint so F8 shadow_compile knows which corpus a checkpoint was trained on. Failure to promote pauses training rather than continuing on harder data.

comments.text:
## T-B1.6.4: Companion artifacts — `cycle_attribution_n128.json` + `matmul_n128.map.json`

**Target crate**: `gbf-report` (emitter logic)
**Target paths**: `docs/review/f-b1/artifacts/cycle_attribution_n128.json`, `docs/review/f-b1/artifacts/matmul_n128.map.json`

### Background

Two machine-readable companion artifacts that travel with the realism report. They explain how to interpret the report's numeric fields, and they expose the ROM layout in a form a reviewer can validate without parsing `.lst`.

### `cycle_attribution_n128.json`

Documents per-metric attribution rules:

```json
{
  "schema": "cycle_attribution.v1",
  "metrics": {
    "cycles_per_product_accumulate": {
      "computed_from": "emulator cycle delta over kernel inner-loop body",
      "excludes": ["dump pause", "vblank ISR", "scheduler service overhead"]
    },
    "cycles_per_output_tile": {
      "computed_from": "first-product to tile-complete-safe-point",
      "excludes": ["dump pause"]
    },
    "cycles_per_full_matmul": {
      "computed_from": "kernel entry to final tile dump complete",
      "includes": ["dump pauses", "vblank ISR", "scheduler service"]
    },
    "...": "..."
  }
}
```

### `matmul_n128.map.json`

Machine-readable Bank0 layout, table placement, symbol sizes:

```json
{
  "schema": "rom_map.v1",
  "build_hash": "sha256:...",
  "bank0": {
    "size_bytes": 16384,
    "used_bytes": 0,
    "free_bytes": 0,
    "regions": [
      { "name": "vectors",          "offset": "0x0000", "length": 256 },
      { "name": "header",           "offset": "0x0100", "length": 80 },
      { "name": "runtime_nucleus",  "offset": "...", "length": "..." },
      { "name": "matmul_kernel",    "offset": "...", "length": "..." },
      { "name": "quarter_square_table", "offset": "...", "length": 1024 }
    ]
  },
  "operand_banks": {
    "a_bank": 1,
    "b_bank": 2,
    "operand_size_bytes": 16384
  },
  "symbols": [
    { "name": "f_b1_kernel_entry",   "bank": 0, "offset": "..." },
    { "name": "f_b1_qs_table_base",  "bank": 0, "offset": "..." }
  ]
}
```

### Why companion artifacts

A realism report is consumed numerically. The cycle attribution and ROM map are consumed by humans (reviewers) and by tooling (sanity checkers). Putting them in `.json` next to the report keeps them discoverable, diff-able, and tooling-friendly. `.lst` is for manual reading; `.map.json` is for scripts.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-report -- cycle_attribution_v1_schema
cargo test -p gbf-report -- rom_map_v1_schema
cargo test -p gbf-report -- rom_map_bank0_used_free_round_trip
cargo test -p gbf-report -- rom_map_includes_required_regions
```

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §14 (review packet artifacts).

### Dependencies

- T-B1.6.3 (aggregation provides the data).

title: F4: Phased Training with Dense Teacher and Explicit QAT Schedule
description: Training proceeds in 5 explicit phases (DenseTeacherWarmup -> RouterWarmup -> ExpertTernaryQat -> FullNumericQat -> HardenAndSelect) with per-phase QAT hardness control. Frozen dense teacher serves as distillation target and default ReferenceModelBundle.
acceptance_criteria: All T4.x tasks closed; Phase types, scheduler, teacher, distillation all tested

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F4: Phased Training with Dense Teacher and Explicit QAT Schedule

### Background

Ternary QAT training does not go from random initialization to fully hard-quantized model in a single training run. The literature (and practical experience with BitNet, 1-bit LLMs, etc.) shows that stability requires a careful phase schedule:

1. First train a decent model at higher precision
2. Then gradually introduce quantization constraints
3. Use the higher-precision model as a distillation target

Without this, ternary training tends to collapse: the STE gradients are noisy, the router can't find stable specialization when expert weights are simultaneously being quantized, and activation ranges swing wildly.

### The five phases

**Phase A (DenseTeacherWarmup)**: Train with no hard ternary, no activation fake quant. Router can be soft top-1. Goal: get a model with good quality and stable expert specialization. This phase produces the dense teacher checkpoint.

**Phase B (RouterWarmup)**: Freeze the basic model geometry. Turn on hard top-1 routing (with Gumbel or straight-through), load-balance loss, z-loss, expert dropout, and temporal switch regularization. Goal: make the router commit to stable, deployment-friendly patterns before quantization noise hits.

**Phase C (ExpertTernaryQat)**: Turn on hard/soft ternary projection for expert up/down projections only. Learn or anneal per-row thresholds. Fake-quantize the exported scales. Distill from the frozen dense teacher (logit-level KL divergence). Goal: ternarize expert weights while the teacher provides a stable gradient signal.

**Phase D (FullNumericQat)**: Turn on activation fake quant at expert input/output, residual boundaries, and deployable norm modules. Keep range penalties on. Goal: make the entire forward pass match the deployed numeric behavior.

**Phase E (HardenAndSelect)**: Run EMA export, shadow compile, small conformance suite, and frontier selection. Goal: pick the best checkpoint that balances quality, conformance, and deployment fit.

### Why the dense teacher matters for the architecture

The dense teacher checkpoint is not just a training aid — it becomes the default ReferenceModelBundle. This gives you a natural, high-quality denotational reference without needing a separate "reference model" training run. The approximation relation between DenotationalOracle (running the dense teacher) and ArtifactOracle (running the ternary artifact) is exactly the quantization gap, which is what conformance.json should measure.

### Confidence: HIGH with reservations

The phases themselves are textbook. The exact phase boundaries (which step to switch at) will need tuning once we have actual training data and loss curves. The types (TrainPhaseSpec, QuantHardness, RouterTrainMode) are clearly right even if the default phase schedule changes.

### The 5 Phases

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Session 2026-05-06: dense-baseline-first scaffolding

The 5-phase F4 schedule is correct but assumed-MoE. Per the 2026-05-06 amendment in planv0.md, the **first** training run is a dense-only `ModelTopologyConfig` (all blocks `FfnPathConfig::Dense`) using the **same** F4 phase scheduler, teacher freeze, and shadow_compile pipeline.

Phase mapping for the dense run:
- Phase A `DenseTeacherWarmup` — identical (no QAT, dense already)
- Phase B `RouterWarmup` — degenerates to no-op (no router to warm); `λ_balance`, `λ_zrouter`, `λ_switch` weights become 0
- Phase C `ExpertTernaryQat` — ternarizes the dense FFN's two linears (the scheduler's `expert_qat: QuantHardness::{Soft, Hard}` flips dense FFN modules, since they implement the same `QatHardnessControl` trait)
- Phase D `FullNumericQat` — identical
- Phase E `HardenAndSelect` — identical; produces a dense `CheckpointFrontierPoint` consumed by F8 shadow compile

The scheduler does not need new phases. The QAT-hardness flip works on any module implementing `QatHardnessControl`, and dense FFN linears already do.

Owner of the dense run is the new feature **F13 Dense Baseline Track** (sibling of F4 under bd-1rb), not F4 itself. F4 stays as written.

title: T-A5.4: Text renderer + font
description: Text renderer and M0 font per RFC section 3D. The M0 font is FONT_TILE_COUNT = 128 glyphs times 16 bytes = 2048 bytes; full 256-glyph or LexicalSpec.charset-sized fonts are a CommonBank-backed follow-up. Boot installs tile bitmaps once via video_commit::emit_bootstrap_vram_init while LCD is off. Per-frame text printing stages UiCommitOp::PutGlyphCell through video_commit and emits no direct StoreToVram; TextLayout is 20x18 visible cells with a 32-entry BG-map stride.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A5.4: Text renderer + font

**Target crate**: `gbf-runtime`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-runtime/src/text.rs`, plus a font asset (`gbf-runtime/assets/font_8x8.bin` or similar)
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 1626, line 1651 (font/assets in Bank 0).

### Background

The text renderer is what gets the model's tokens onto the screen. It writes glyph tiles into VRAM and updates the BG map. Crucially, all VRAM writes go through `video_commit` (T-A5.6) — text is a *producer* into the commit queue, not a direct VRAM writer.

### Background risk: Bank 0 budget

A typical 8×8 mono font has 256 glyphs × 16 bytes = 4096 bytes. That's a quarter of Bank 0. The minimal+UI shell budget assumes this, but if the budget is tight, font goes to a CommonBank instead. Default for v1: keep font in Bank 0 (simplest), accept the cost.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub fn build_text_section() -> Section;

pub fn font_bytes() -> &'static [u8];                            // 4096 bytes for 256 glyphs

pub struct TextLayout {
    pub bg_map_origin: u16,                                       // VRAM address
    pub columns: u8,                                              // 20 for DMG (160 / 8)
    pub rows: u8,                                                 // 18 for DMG (144 / 8)
}

/// Emit AsmIR: stage a glyph commit through video_commit::queue_glyph(x, y, glyph_id).
pub fn emit_text_print_glyph<B: Builder>(b: &mut B, x: u8, y: u8, glyph: u8);
```

Text never writes VRAM directly; it stages a `UiCommitOp::PutGlyph { ... }` into the commit queue. T-A5.6 owns the actual write at the next legal LCD-mode window.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-runtime
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- text::font_size                    # 256 glyphs * 16 bytes = 4096 bytes
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- text::layout_dmg                    # 20x18 DMG dimensions
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- text::print_glyph_stages            # Generates a UiCommitOp, never a direct VRAM write
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: Text never writes VRAM directly — invariant enforced at the API boundary.
- §VI.1: Single text renderer.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## RFC F-A5 alignment - 2026-05-03

Source of truth: history/rfcs/F-A5-bank0-runtime.md section 3D. This comment supersedes earlier bead text where inconsistent.

Corrections:
- The M0 font is 128 glyphs, 2048 bytes, not 256 glyphs or 4096 bytes.
- A full 256-glyph or LexicalSpec.charset-sized font is deferred to a CommonBank-backed follow-up in gbf-codegen or Epic B.
- Text stages UiCommitOp::PutGlyphCell, not PutGlyph, and never emits MachineEffect::StoreToVram.
- TextLayout includes visible_columns = 20, visible_rows = 18, and bg_map_stride = 32. Cell addressing uses stride 32, not visible_columns.
- Boot installs the font tiles through video_commit::emit_bootstrap_vram_init while LCDC.7 is off.

title: T-B2.0: gbf-policy core schema (compile + objective + repair + budget types)
description:
## What
Introduce the foundational `gbf-policy` schema types that F-B2/F-B4 list as
inputs but no current task actually owns. Per RFC §2.12, F-B2/F-B4 must
introduce these before Wave 2 can begin. The current code has
`gbf-policy/src/{budget,envelope,objective,repair,risk}.rs` as module stubs
and `gbf-policy/src/compile.rs` as a tiny `SequenceSemanticsRef` placeholder.

Schema-only deliverables (no logic; logic lives in T-B2.12, T-B2.13, T-B4.10):

- `gbf-policy::compile`:
  - `CompileRequest`
  - `ResolvedCompilePolicy`
  - `CompileKnobs` { global, bounds, locks, overrides, provenance }
  - `CompileKnobValues` (eight sub-knobs per planv0: placement, observation,
    range, storage, sram, rom_window, overlay, schedule)
  - `CompileKnobBounds`, `CompileKnobOverrides`, `KnobLockSet`
  - `CompileKnobId` (closed enum), `CompileKnobPath { knob, selector, field }`
  - `PolicySource` (closed enum: TargetDefault | ProfileDefault |
    CompileRequestOverride | HintBundle | Calibration | RepairProposal(_))
    -- `RepairProposal(_)` variant exists in the enum but its USE is forbidden
    by T-B2.16 in this chunk.
  - `PolicyProvenance`, `EffectiveConstraints`
  - `MonotoneKnob` trait (so admissibility is type-driven, per bd-3aqf intent)
- `gbf-policy::objective`: `CompileObjective`, `RiskPolicy` (with
  `require_confidence_at_least: CalibrationConfidenceClass`).
- `gbf-policy::repair`: `RepairPolicy` (defaults table per planv0; loop logic
  owned by F-B16).
- `gbf-policy::budget`: `RuntimeChromeBudget`, `RomBudgetSlot`, `BudgetSlotClass`
  ({ Bank0Free, CommonBank, ExpertBank }). Schema only -- runtime-shell
  emission is owned by T2.2 (F2 epic), training preflight wiring by T2.3.
- `gbf-policy::risk`: `CalibrationConfidenceClass` (None, Transferred,
  WithinFamily, Onsite, ...).

## Why
RFC §2.12 explicitly lists these as F-B2/F-B4 deliverables. Today,
`CompileRequest`, `ResolvedCompilePolicy`, `CompileObjective`, `RiskPolicy`,
`RepairPolicy` have NO owning task; `CompileKnobs` is owned by T-B16.1
(bd-3aqf, milestone M3, blocked on oracle); `RuntimeChromeBudget` is owned
by T2.1 (bd-yfv, milestone M2, F2). All of T-B2.12, T-B2.13, T-B2.14,
T-B2.19, T-B4.10 list these as inputs. Without a Wave-1 task that produces
them, the chunk cannot start.

## Inputs
- `gbf-foundation::{Hash256, BlobRef, SemVer, ByteCost, ids}` (existing).
- planv0 §"What each crate is responsible for" (line 142-206).
- planv0 §"Profiles and objectives" (RepairPolicy defaults table).

## Outputs
- All types listed above, with `serde::{Serialize, Deserialize}`,
  `deny_unknown_fields`, explicitly-tagged enums.
- `MonotoneKnob` trait + impls for each sub-knob.
- Round-trip serde tests for every type.
- A canonical-default-bounds fixture (used by T-B2.12 to seed profile specs).

## Pitfalls
- DO NOT include refinement-loop logic (`RepairProposal(_)` value paths,
  `KnobDelta`, `ConstraintDelta`, `EstimatedCostDelta`). Those land in F-B16.
  This task only defines the enum variants; T-B2.16 forbids their USE.
- DO NOT add `BringupRelaxationRecord`, `CompileProfileRelaxation`,
  `ConstraintFrame.authorized_relaxations`, or any `bringup_*` relaxation
  field (RFC §2.13).
- `ConstraintOperation::AuthorizedRelaxation` exists in the enum for
  forward-compat with later chunks; F-B2/F-B4 never construct it.
- `PolicySource::RepairProposal(RepairProposalId)` variant must exist in the
  enum but T-B2.16 forbids it as a value in this chunk.

## Acceptance
- `gbf-policy::compile_types_round_trip`
- `gbf-policy::compile_knobs_monotone_order_per_subknob`
- `gbf-policy::budget_types_round_trip`
- `gbf-policy::objective_types_round_trip`
- `gbf-policy::repair_policy_defaults_match_planv0_table`
- Module exports compile cleanly with `-D warnings`.

## Refs
- RFC §2.12 (current-code note + must-introduce list).
- planv0 §"Profiles and objectives" (RepairPolicy defaults).
- bd-3aqf (T-B16.1) -- schema-only sibling, will become an "extend with
  refinement-only fields" follow-up after this lands.
- bd-yfv (T2.1) -- RuntimeChromeBudget definition was scoped to this; this
  task absorbs the schema half so M1 work can proceed without waiting on F2.
- Wave 1 in RFC §8.5.
- Blocks: T-B2.1, T-B2.2, T-B2.12, T-B2.13, T-B4.9, T-B4.10.

title: T16.3: enwiki8 corpus — canonical 100MB Wikipedia char-level benchmark, splits, n-gram baseline
description: Integrate enwiki8 (Hutter Prize, the canonical 100MB char-level Wikipedia LM benchmark) as the production-comparable training corpus. Acceptance: (1) deterministic downloader (enwiki8 URL + sha256 pin); (2) license metadata (CC BY-SA via Wikipedia derivative); (3) standard splits — first 90M bytes train, next 5M val, last 5M test (the canonical literature split); (4) normalization through F-G2 charset_v1 with unmappable-rate analysis (Wikipedia has more unicode breadth than Accelerando); (5) 5-gram Kneser-Ney baseline trained on train, bpc reported on val/test (target: roughly competitive with literature, e.g. ~1.6 bpc range for n-gram); (6) contamination check vs Gutenberg + TinyStories.

title: T-B2.8: Stage 0 calibration binding (resolution + freshness + confidence gate, all Hard)
description:
## What
Implement Stage 0 validation class 7: calibration binding. Two gates,
both Hard: (1) resolution — every referenced layer resolves to a present
bundle; (2) freshness + confidence — bundle hashes match active inputs
and declared `CalibrationConfidenceClass` ≥ `RiskPolicy::require_confidence_at_least`.
See RFC §7.3 class 7, §2.8.

## Why
Calibration drift is the biggest source of "the build worked but produced
garbage" failures. Catching mismatch here is cheap; catching it later is
expensive. Per planv0 line ~1531, stale or mismatched calibration must be
rejected at any layer.

## Inputs
- T-B2.4 ValidatedInputs.
- T-B2.18 BootstrapCalibrationBundle fixture (for tests).
- `gbf-policy::risk::RiskPolicy::require_confidence_at_least`
  (from `compile_profile.repair_policy`).
- Layered calibration bundles (`PlatformCalibrationBundle`,
  `KernelCalibrationBundle`, `RuntimeCalibrationBundle`).

## Outputs
- Class 7 codes: `CalibrationMissing { class }`,
  `CalibrationStale { class, declared, observed }`,
  `CalibrationConfidenceTooLow { required, observed }`.
- Stale = `target_profile_hash`, `kernel_set_hash`, `packer_version`,
  or `calibration_schema_hash` mismatch with active build.
- Bringup with `BootstrapCalibrationBundle (confidence=None)` succeeds
  because Bringup's `RiskPolicy::require_confidence_at_least == None`.
- Default/Trace/Recovery reject the same bundle via
  `CalibrationConfidenceTooLow`.

## Pitfalls
- DO NOT special-case `Bringup`. The same `CalibrationConfidenceTooLow`
  gate handles every profile uniformly. Bringup just sets the threshold to
  `None`.
- Stale calibration is NEVER soft. There is no "older but still acceptable"
  case — a mismatch is a mismatch.
- Runtime-nucleus mismatch is checked elsewhere (Stage 2 missing
  RuntimeChromeBudget); don't fold runtime checks into calibration.
- The Bringup latitude was deliberately removed in revision; do not
  reintroduce it.

## Acceptance
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_validate_rejects_calibration_stale
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_validate_accepts_bootstrap_calibration_when_profile_requires_none
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_validate_rejects_bootstrap_calibration_under_default_profile
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_validate_rejects_explicit_calibration_unresolved

## Refs
- RFC §7.3 class 7, §2.8 calibration semantics, §2.13 Bringup-as-profile.
- planv0 calibration freshness rule line ~1531.
- Depends on: T-B2.18 (bootstrap fixture).
- Blocks: T-B2.11.

title: T-A8.5: Stateless CLI — init/exec/inspect commands
description: gbf-debug init --rom rom.gb --sym rom.sym --out session.gbsess (forges fresh session at PC $0100). gbf-debug exec --in session --script script.js --out next.gbsess (runs and writes new session). gbf-debug inspect session.gbsess (no-run state dump). All commands deterministic; all session files content-addressable by SHA-256.
close_reason: Implemented in F-A8 gbf-debug PR; gates recorded in closure comments. Parent feature closes in this same PR.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## 2026-05-04 amendment — F-A7 Emulator construction

F-A7 has merged. The `init` / `exec` / `inspect` sequences in RFC §3.4 use these F-A7 names exactly (see RFC §4.3):

- Construction: `Emulator::load_rom(bytes: &[u8], config: EmulatorConfig) -> Result<Self, EmuError>` with `EmulatorConfig { boot_mode: BootMode::PostBootDmg, policy: DeterminismPolicy::default(), .. }` (other fields default).
- Restore: `emu.restore(&snapshot) -> Result<(), EmuError>` mutates in place; F-A7 lineage failures surface as typed `CliError::EmulatorRestore`.
- Snapshot: `emu.snapshot() -> Result<Snapshot, EmuError>`; `Snapshot` derives serde directly (no `to_bytes`/`from_bytes`).
- Trap registration on persisted breakpoints: `emu.traps().add_pc(addr, predicate, action)` returning `BreakpointId`; for watchpoints, `add_mem_read`/`add_mem_write`/`add_mem_rw` taking `AddressRange::new(addr, addr)?`. `TrapAction::HaltAndReport` is the only action F-A8 uses.
- Errors: `EmuError` (not `EmulatorError`).

The post-load PC=`$0100` invariant in step 4 of `init` (`CliError::PostLoadPcUnexpected`) is preserved; F-A7 `BootMode::PostBootDmg` is the default and the regs() snapshot lands at $0100 immediately after `load_rom`.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: Closed by F-A8 implementation. Source of truth: RFC §§3.4, 3.5. Added gbf-debug binary with init/exec/inspect, stateless input/output sessions, parent_sha256 lineage, replace-existing flags, timeout/run-budget options, JSON-only stdout/stderr, and .cargo xtask alias support. Gates: cargo test -p gbf-debug --test e2e init_exec_inspect_tiny_rom; cargo fmt --check --all; cargo clippy --workspace --all-features -- -D warnings; cargo test --workspace --all-features.

title: T4.3b: Export frozen teacher as ReferenceModelBundle
description: After teacher weights are frozen (T4.3), export them as the default ReferenceModelBundle via ExportVisitor. Steps: (1) Run ExportVisitor on frozen teacher weights — this produces a ReferenceModelBundle with ReferenceProgram, ReferenceTensors, and ReferenceNumericProfile. (2) Validate the ReferenceProgram by evaluating it on named fixture prompts and comparing against the live frozen teacher's forward pass — outputs must be exact. (3) Store as sibling artifact alongside the eventual student ModelArtifact. This is the denotational truth baseline for conformance.json. Separating this from the teacher freeze (T4.3) allows distillation to begin immediately while the export machinery catches up.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- teacher::export passes; ReferenceModelBundle matches teacher forward on fixtures; Deterministic export
