comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.2.2: `OperandLayout` enum + `RomBankId` + smoke restriction validation

**Target crate**: `gbf-codegen`
**Target file**: `gbf-codegen/src/operand_layout.rs`

### Background

`OperandLayout` resolves the L0/L1 vs L2+ contradiction. The naive draft of the RFC said L1 had "no banking" and that `ComputeBringupRequest` required "distinct nonzero ROM banks." Both cannot be true. The `WramSmoke` variant is the explicit, narrowly-scoped escape valve that lets the L1 codegen path run without F-A4 BankLease pressure.

### Variants

```rust
pub enum OperandLayout {
    /// L0/L1 smoke profile only. Operands live in WRAM, no ROM banking.
    /// Valid only when `matrix_dim = 16`. NOT valid for closure artifacts
    /// or the L5 review-packet report.
    WramSmoke,

    DistinctRomBanks {
        a_bank: RomBankId,
        b_bank: RomBankId,
    },
}
```

### `RomBankId`

Checked newtype over `u16`.

### Validation rules

For `WramSmoke`:
- `matrix_dim.n() == 16`.

For `DistinctRomBanks`:
- bank id is valid for the selected target profile;
- for MBC5, bank id fits the supported ROM-bank range;
- A and B banks are nonzero;
- A and B banks are distinct;
- `offset + length <= rom_bank_size`;
- For `N = 128`, operand length is exactly one 16 KiB bank, so operand offset must be zero unless the target profile explicitly provides a larger switchable bank;
- generated ROM layout must not overlap Bank0 interrupt vectors, header, runtime nucleus, kernel, or quarter-square table.

### Why `WramSmoke` is restricted

If `WramSmoke` were accepted at large N, a sloppy reviewer could let a closure artifact slip through with no banking pressure — silently invalidating the entire premise of F-B1. The restriction is a typed contract: `WramSmoke` paired with N != 16 is rejected at request-validation time.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_request_validation_accepts_l1_smoke_layout
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_request_validation_rejects_smoke_layout_for_n_gt_16
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- compute_bringup_request_rejects_same_bank
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- compute_bringup_request_rejects_bank0_operand
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- compute_bringup_request_rejects_bank_overflow
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- compute_bringup_request_n128_operand_offset_must_be_zero
```

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §7.3 (operand layout, RomBankId validation).

### Dependencies

- T-B1.0.1 (`SquareDim`).
- T-B1.2.1 (`ComputeBringupRequest` consumes this).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## REVISION PASS — RFC §7.3 OperandLayout patch (2026-05-03)

The `DistinctRomBanks` variant now carries explicit per-operand placement, not bare bank IDs.

### Type change

Before:
```rust
DistinctRomBanks {
    a_bank: RomBankId,
    b_bank: RomBankId,
}
```

After:
```rust
DistinctRomBanks {
    a: BankedOperandPlacement,
    b: BankedOperandPlacement,
}

pub struct BankedOperandPlacement {
    pub bank: RomBankId,
    pub offset: RomBankOffset,
}

pub struct RomBankOffset(u16);
```

### Why

The previous RFC validated `offset + length <= rom_bank_size` but the request type had no `offset` field. The validator was structurally unable to detect a mis-aligned operand placement because the field did not exist.

### Validation

* `bank` and `offset` are validated together against the target profile.
* `offset + length <= rom_bank_size` is computed in checked arithmetic widened to at least `u32` (otherwise `u16` overflow can hide the bug we are trying to catch).
* For `N = 128`, length is exactly one 16 KiB bank, so offset must be zero unless the target profile explicitly provides a larger switchable bank.

### Realism report wiring

The per-run `workload.operand_layout` in `realism_report.v1.json` now serializes as:

```json
"operand_layout": {
  "a": { "bank": 1, "offset": 0 },
  "b": { "bank": 2, "offset": 0 },
  "multiply_table_bank": 0
}
```

This matches the new request shape. Schema test in T-B1.6.1 has been extended to assert the new layout shape (see T-B1.6.1 revision).

### New negative test

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- compute_bringup_request_rejects_bad_operand_offset
```

Constructs a `BankedOperandPlacement` whose offset+length exceeds the bank size and asserts the validator rejects it.

title: T1.6: Implement ExportVisitor
description: Walks a trained gbf-model, extracts all canonical tensors (ternary weights, scales, embeddings, router weights, classifier, norm params, LUT contents), assembles them into ArtifactCore + TernaryWeightPlan + QuantSpec. Must produce byte-identical results for the same model weights regardless of training backend. This is the single most critical path for artifact/training agreement.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export passes; Deterministic export (same weights = same ArtifactCore hash); Scale tensors exported as first-class CanonicalTensors
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

**Artifact contract:** Implemented `ArtifactCore`, `CanonicalTensor`, `QuantSpec`, and `ExportFacts.activation_ranges` in `gbf-artifact`, plus `ExportVisitor` in `gbf-model`. The visitor emits deterministic `CanonicalTensor` entries for ternary weights, first-class ternary scale tensors, bias tensors, router weights/biases, dense/shared branch weights/biases, embeddings, classifier tensors, and norm LUT contents. `QuantSpec` references the ternary weight plans and tensor ids. `RangeDigest` records visited `ActFakeQuant` ranges in deterministic order.

**differentiable Burn path:** Not applicable to this bead. This is the backend-independent artifact/export boundary and has no Burn dependency. It consumes already-extracted model/QAT scalar state. Router/expert Burn adapters remain outside this export bead and are tracked separately by bd-1ptv.

**Tests proving it:** See the claim-to-gate matrix below.

**Acceptance movement:** None. This bead now owns and implements the artifact-side export contract it names. It does not claim compiler/runtime lowering agreement or ArtifactOracle agreement; those remain downstream validation work.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `ArtifactCore` assembly from QAT modules | supported | not applicable | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` |
| `CanonicalTensor` schema and deterministic content hashes | supported | not applicable | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact` |
| Ternary projection export as separate weight and scale tensors | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` |
| Router weight/bias export | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_extracts_router_shared_dense_and_norm_lut_tensors` |
| Expert block traversal, including optional shared dense branch | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_records_all_qat_module_kinds_and_activation_ranges` |
| Activation range facts through `RangeDigest` / `ExportFacts.activation_ranges` | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_records_all_qat_module_kinds_and_activation_ranges` |
| Norm LUT contents | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_extracts_router_shared_dense_and_norm_lut_tensors` |
| Embedding and classifier raw tensor export helpers | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_builds_deterministic_artifact_for_same_modules` |
| Exhaustive QAT visitor enum dispatch through `QatModuleRef` | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_builds_deterministic_artifact_for_same_modules` |
| Direct Burn module walking | not applicable | moved | not applicable | bd-1ptv |
| Target-specific packed tensor/lut lowering | not applicable | not applicable | not applicable | No `PackedTensor` or target lowering is emitted by this API |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `gbf-artifact` owns `ArtifactCore`, `CanonicalTensor`, `QuantSpec`, and `ExportFacts.activation_ranges` | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact` | none | Covers schema construction, tensor validation, duplicate ids, and deterministic core hashing |
| `ExportVisitor` compiles without Burn or backend dependencies | `cargo check -p gbf-model` | none | `gbf-model` depends only on artifact/foundation/hw plus serde |
| Export is deterministic for same weights independent of visit order | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_builds_deterministic_artifact_for_same_modules` | none | Compares `ArtifactCore` hash and serialized export bytes after different visitation order |
| All current QAT module types are visited | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_records_all_qat_module_kinds_and_activation_ranges` | none | Covers ternary, activation, norm, router, expert, expert block, and shared branch traversal |
| Scale tensors are first-class `CanonicalTensor` records, not embedded in packed repr | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` | none | Asserts separate `.weight`, `.scale`, and `.bias` tensor ids and `QuantSpec` references |
| Router/shared dense/norm LUT tensors are exported | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_extracts_router_shared_dense_and_norm_lut_tensors` | none | Covers router matrices, shared dense alpha, and norm LUT payloads |
| Non-finite float export payloads are rejected | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_rejects_non_finite_raw_float_tensors` | none | Prevents NaN/Inf tensors from entering artifact state |
| Duplicate export paths are rejected | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_rejects_duplicate_paths` | none | Prevents ambiguous tensor/fact identity |
| No direct Burn dependency leaks into model/artifact export | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Architecture test checks Burn import confinement and artifact/model boundary |
| QAT closure/sensor invariants still pass | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py` | none | Harness passed after the implementation |
| Clippy is clean for the target crate | `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings` | none | Also checks the `gbf-artifact` dependency |

## No-future Acceptance

No future export encodings are accepted. `CanonicalTensorPayload` supports only implemented `F32`, ternary `I8` values constrained to -1/0/1, and `Q8_8` `U16` scales. The visitor emits target-independent canonical tensors only; it does not accept or pretend to lower packed target layouts, compiler schedules, or runtime-specific tensor encodings. `QatModuleRef` is exhaustive for the QAT module types present today and has no dynamic downcast fallback.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-model/src/qat/export.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "ExportVisitor", "ArtifactCore", "CanonicalTensor", "export visitation", "TernaryWeightPlan", "QuantSpec".

### Implementation Notes
- `ExportVisitor` walks a trained gbf-model and extracts ALL canonical tensors for artifact generation.
- This is the **single most critical path** for training/artifact agreement.
- Output: `ArtifactCore` containing:
  - One ternary weight `CanonicalTensor` + one scale `CanonicalTensor` per projection layer
  - Router weight tensors (at higher precision)
  - Embedding tensors
  - Norm parameters
  - `TernaryWeightPlan` per weight group
  - `QuantSpec` referencing the plans
- **MUST produce byte-identical results** for the same model weights regardless of training backend.
- Visitor pattern: walks each module type, calls `export_canonical()` on each QAT module.
- No Burn dependency in the export path — works on extracted tensors.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                          # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export             # Unit tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings           # No warnings
# Verify: determinism — same weights produce identical ArtifactCore hash
# Verify: all QAT module types visited (ternary, activation, norm, router, expert)
# Verify: scale tensors are first-class CanonicalTensors, not embedded in packed repr
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: Visitor must exhaustively match all module types — use enum dispatch, not dynamic downcasts
- §IV.3: Reproducible builds — byte-identical export is a hard requirement
- §III.4: Golden snapshot tests for export output

## Activation range export ownership clarification

`bd-g90` explicitly owns the artifact side of the `bd-pk3` activation range contract: define/populate `RangeDigest` and `ExportFacts.activation_ranges` from visited `ActFakeQuant` modules, including deterministic ordering and a test under `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export`. `bd-pk3` only guarantees the pre-export module contract (`export_range` / `forward_spec`) and does not claim ArtifactCore or ExportFacts materialization.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Corrective scope note from bd-28f6

Follow-up review found that the bd-g90 closure overstated two pieces of scope:

- Cross-backend byte agreement is not proven by bd-g90 alone. bd-g90 provides the deterministic scalar/artifact export machinery. The end-to-end cross-backend agreement proof remains with bd-22o.
- Direct walking of trained Burn router/expert modules is not implemented by bd-g90. The Burn adapter/export handoff for Top1RouterQat and ExpertBlockQat remains with bd-1ptv.

bd-28f6 tightens the implemented artifact boundary: activation range and eval passthrough semantics are part of QuantSpec and ArtifactCore identity; ArtifactCore validates QuantSpec tensor references, tensor kinds, element types, and shapes; semantic_hash uses a domain-separated canonical byte encoder over tensor content hashes and quant metadata rather than serde JSON.

title: T-B1.4.2: BringupArenaPlan + non-overlap validation + WRAM pressure reporting
description: Static WRAM slot allocation for accumulator (4-byte aligned, 1024B), a_panel (256B), b_panel (256B). No bump allocator (M1 owns that). Reports wram_peak_bytes into realism report memory_bandwidth block. Validates non-overlap, alignment, WRAM fit.
close_reason: Implemented L3 streamed-output building blocks now proven by a single streaming ROM per size. Evidence: BringupStoragePlan/OutputBinding/HarnessTileStream and BringupArenaPlan non-overlap validation; tile_schedule covers every output tile exactly once; gbf-bench reassembles TileDump bytes into full matrices; build_l3_streaming_rom loops mt/nt/kt inside one ROM, reaches a repeated tile-safe PC, and host performs the existing DumpArena-shaped accumulator read/resume; N=128 byte-exact output is regenerated by cargo run -p gbf-test --bin f_b1_regen. ASM checklist: streaming ROM uses typed gbf-asm sections, BankLease lowering for panel copies, no raw MBC writes, and no harness/tile safe point while a lease is active. Open sibling bd-h5aj remains for ROM-side harness mailbox/control-plane pause-resume.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.4.2: `BringupArenaPlan` + non-overlap validation + WRAM pressure reporting

**Target crate**: `gbf-codegen`
**Target file**: `gbf-codegen/src/arena.rs`

### Background

The arena plan records the WRAM offsets for the accumulator tile and the two operand panels. F-B1 has no allocator — the slots are statically computed. M1 will introduce a real bump allocator and lifetime-class partitioning; F-B1 lays the type-shape with explicit deferred-to-M1 docs.

### Types

```rust
pub struct BringupArenaPlan {
    pub accumulator_tile: WramArenaSlot,
    pub a_panel:          WramArenaSlot,
    pub b_panel:          WramArenaSlot,
}

pub struct WramArenaSlot {
    pub offset: u16,
    pub length: u16,
    pub align:  u16,
}
```

### Validation rules

- Slots do not overlap.
- All slots fit in WRAM.
- `accumulator_tile` is 4-byte aligned (i32).
- Total WRAM scratch pressure is reported (sum of slot lengths + alignment padding).

### WRAM pressure reporting

The arena plan emits a `wram_peak_bytes` value into the realism report's `memory_bandwidth` block. For F-B1's single-tile reuse pattern this is just `accumulator + a_panel + b_panel + alignment_padding`, but exposing it as a metric lets M1 see the headroom available for larger temporaries.

### Deferred to M1

- A real bump allocator.
- Per-`LifetimeClass` arena partitions.
- Peak-pressure analysis across multiple tiles.
- `ResourceVector` integration.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_l3_arena_slots_do_not_overlap
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_arena_accumulator_4byte_aligned
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_arena_fits_wram
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_arena_reports_wram_pressure
```

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §7.7.

### Dependencies

- T-B1.4.1 (StoragePlan defines the slot consumers).

title: T11.4: Stub OverlayPlan IR placeholder in gbf-codegen
description: Add OverlayPlan placeholder pass output in gbf-codegen between RomWindowPlan and ArenaPlan (per planv0.md compiler-pipeline section 8.5). Types only for now: OverlayPlan { region_bytes, residents: Vec<OverlayResident>, reload_policy: OverlayReloadPolicy }. No allocator logic; compiler will fill in once expert kernel sets exist. Documents the IR slot so later passes can reason about overlay residency.
close_reason: Superseded by F-B9 (bd-140); see comment

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-codegen` (or `gbf-ir` if that owns the IR types).
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-codegen/src/overlay_plan.rs` (new) or equivalent IR module.

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — section 8.5 `OverlayPlan`. Also F11 design notes on bd-1i8.

### Implementation Notes

This task is intentionally a **stub**. The full overlay allocator depends on expert kernel sets that don't exist yet (F1/F6 land first). What we need *now* is the IR slot so later passes can refer to it and so T11.3's preflight has a place to read overlay demand from.

Define:

```rust
pub struct OverlayPlan {
    pub region_bytes: u16,                  // = CompileProfile.wram_layout.overlay_bytes
    pub residents: Vec<OverlayResident>,    // empty in v1
    pub reload_policy: OverlayReloadPolicy, // = CompileProfile.overlay_reload
}

pub struct OverlayResident {
    pub kind: OverlayResidentKind,          // ExpertKernel, MicroKernel, LutFragment
    pub size_bytes: u16,
    pub source_bank: SourceBankRef,
    pub estimated_reload_cost_cycles: Option<u32>,
}

pub enum OverlayResidentKind {
    ExpertKernel,
    MicroKernel,
    LutFragment,
}
```

Place `OverlayPlan` in the compiler-pipeline ordering after `RomWindowPlan` (planv0.md section 8) and before `ArenaPlan` (section 9), as planv0.md section 8.5 prescribes.

### What this task does NOT do

- It does not implement an allocator.
- It does not select residents.
- It does not estimate reload cost.

These all wait until expert kernels exist (F1/F6) and `gbf-bench` produces calibration data.

### What this task DOES do

- Declares the IR types.
- Adds a no-op pass that constructs an empty `OverlayPlan { region_bytes: profile.wram_layout.overlay_bytes, residents: vec![], reload_policy: profile.overlay_reload }`.
- Wires the pass into the pipeline so downstream consumers can reference it.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-codegen                              # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- overlay_plan                # Empty OverlayPlan constructible from CompileProfile
cargo clippy -p gbf-codegen -- -D warnings               # No warnings
# Verify: OverlayPlan is reachable from the pipeline ordering
# Verify: region_bytes equals the active CompileProfile.wram_layout.overlay_bytes
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `OverlayResidentKind` is exhaustive; future kinds force compile errors at consumers.
- §VI.1: IR slot lives in the codegen pipeline, not invented ad hoc by later passes.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Superseded by F-B9 (bd-140) — 2026-04-26

This stub used a session-driven shape `{ region_bytes, residents, reload_policy }` that diverged from the canonical planv0.md `OverlayPlan`. F-B9 replaces it with the canonical shape `{ regions: Vec<OverlayRegion>, installs: Vec<OverlayInstall>, share_classes: Vec<OverlayShareClass> }`. For BringUp v1, F-B9 keeps `regions.len() == 1` and `share_classes.is_empty()` so the small-scope semantics of the original stub are preserved.

Closing as superseded. Implementation work is tracked under F-B9 and its (future) child tasks.

title: T-B4.4: Common bank footprint (kernels, LUTs, shared dense FFN)
description:
## What
Compute aggregate common-bank footprint: kernel bytes, LUT bytes,
optional shared dense FFN bytes (per bd-33q handoff). See RFC §7.6, §7.7.

## Why
Common-bank is scarce on DMG/MBC5 and the trade between expert count and
common kernels is part of every M1 design conversation. Stage 2 must give
that conversation a concrete number.

## Inputs
- T-B4.1 QuantGraphBudgetView (`shared_kernels`, `shared_luts`, optional
  `shared_dense_ffn`).
- T-B4.2 byte-math helpers.

## Outputs
- `pub struct CommonBankFootprint { kernel_bytes, lut_bytes, shared_dense_ffn_bytes: Option<ByteBudget>, aggregate_bytes }`.
- Aggregator that respects ordering and Bank0-compatibility flags.
- Hard reject `BudgetCommonBankExceedsCap { assigned_bytes, cap_bytes }`
  when the aggregate exceeds the common-bank cap.

## Pitfalls
- `aggregate_bytes` MUST equal the sum of components. Validator checks this.
- Optional shared dense FFN per bd-33q: distinguish its bytes from
  per-expert slot bytes. Don't double-count.
- Tied embedding/classifier export aliasing belongs to bd-3bf1, not here.

## Acceptance
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_includes_shared_dense_ffn_in_common_bank_footprint
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_rejects_common_bank_exceeds_cap
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_aggregate_bytes_equals_sum_of_components

## Refs
- RFC §7.6, §7.7.
- bd-33q handoff (shared dense FFN).
- bd-3bf1 (tied embedding aliasing — separate).
- Depends on: T-B4.2.
- Blocks: T-B4.11.

title: T-B4.1: QuantGraphBudgetSource trait + QuantGraphBudgetView schema
description:
## What
Define the seam between F-B4 and F-B3: the `QuantGraphBudgetSource` trait
that F-B4 consumes and the serializable `QuantGraphBudgetView` struct it
returns. Define `ExpertProjection`, `SharedKernelProjection`,
`SharedLutProjection`, `ReductionSiteProjection`, `SequenceStateProjection`,
`RoutingProjection`. See RFC §7.6.

## Why
F-B4 must ship and be tested before F-B3 lands. The trait + serializable
view pattern (rather than a dyn iterator trait) gives F-B4 a stable,
hashable, ordered input it can validate, and gives F-B3 a clear output
contract. The view's `quant_graph_hash` is the canonical hash of the full
F-B3 QuantGraph; F-B4 only depends on it being stable.

## Inputs
- `gbf-foundation::Hash256`, ID newtypes.
- T-B2.13 ResolvedPolicyProduct (Stage 2 input shape — defines what
  Stage 2 takes from Stage 0.5).
- `gbf-artifact::TernaryWeightPlan` (consumed by ExpertProjection).

## Outputs
- `pub trait QuantGraphBudgetSource { quant_graph_hash, semantic_core_hash, to_budget_view -> Result<QuantGraphBudgetView, QuantGraphBudgetViewError> }`.
- `pub struct QuantGraphBudgetView` with sorted vectors and identity hashes.
- `ExpertProjection` with `rows`, `cols`, `metadata_bytes`, `plan` —
  payload byte math is owned by F-B4 (T-B4.2), not trusted from input.
- `ReductionSiteProjection` with `term_count`, `input_max_abs_q`,
  `weight_max_abs_q`, `bias_max_abs_q`, `accumulator_domain`.
- Semantic validator: experts sorted by `(layer, expert)`,
  shared kernels by `KernelSpecId`, reductions by `ReductionSiteId`;
  every `ExpertProjection` references a known layer and has non-zero shape.

## Pitfalls
- DO NOT use `Box<dyn Iterator<Item = ExpertProjection> + '_>` — it hides
  iteration order and complicates hashing. Use `Vec<ExpertProjection>`
  with documented sort order.
- DO NOT include `weight_bytes` / `scale_bytes` in `ExpertProjection`.
  F-B4 computes those from `rows`, `cols`, and `plan` (T-B4.2).
- The trait MUST be small and stable; F-B3 will implement it later.

## Acceptance
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_validates_quant_graph_budget_view_ordering
- gbf-codegen::f_b4_budget_uses_quant_graph_budget_source_trait_stub_until_f_b3
- gbf-codegen quant_graph_budget_view_round_trips_canonically

## Refs
- RFC §7.6 trait + view, §16.1 forward-compat for F-B3.
- Depends on: T-B2.13 (ResolvedPolicyProduct).
- Blocks: T-B4.2, T-B4.5, T-B4.6, T-B4.7, T-B4.8.

title: T11.1: Define WramLayoutPolicy, OverlayReloadPolicy, WramReserved types in gbf-policy
description: Add WramLayoutPolicy { overlay_bytes, continuation_bytes, stack_bytes, hot_arena_bytes_min }, OverlayReloadPolicy enum (PerExpertSwitch default + PerSlice + PerLayerStep variants), and WramReserved { overlay, hot_arena_floor, total } in gbf-policy. Restructure RuntimeChromeBudget.wram_reserved to use WramReserved. Schema-only — no logic.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-policy`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-policy/src/budget.rs` (extend), `gbf-policy/src/profile.rs` (new — but only the WRAM-layout types here; `CompileProfile` itself is T11.2).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search "WRAM hot arena", "WRAM overlay", "OverlayPlan", "ArenaPlan". Also see the 2026-04-26 session refinement comment on bd-yfv.

### Implementation Notes

Add to `gbf-policy`:

```rust
pub struct WramLayoutPolicy {
    /// Fixed-size region of WRAM reserved for overlay payloads (expert hot kernels copied
    /// from expert banks). Sized per CompileProfile so it is reproducible and preflightable.
    pub overlay_bytes: u16,
    /// WRAM bytes reserved for the resumable continuation record (gbf-abi).
    pub continuation_bytes: u16,
    /// WRAM bytes reserved for the call stack.
    pub stack_bytes: u16,
    /// Floor that ArenaPlan must satisfy after overlay/continuation/stack/runtime use.
    pub hot_arena_bytes_min: u16,
}

pub enum OverlayReloadPolicy {
    /// Default: reload the overlay only when the routed expert (or its kernel family)
    /// changes. Caches well under temporal smoothness regularization.
    PerExpertSwitch,
    /// Reload at every safe-point/yield. Predictable; may reload redundantly.
    PerSlice,
    /// Reload at each layer's expert dispatch. Worst per-token; trivially correct.
    PerLayerStep,
}

pub struct WramReserved {
    pub overlay: u16,
    pub hot_arena_floor: u16,
    pub total: u16,
}
```

Then **restructure `RuntimeChromeBudget`**:
- Replace `pub wram_reserved: u16` with `pub wram_reserved: WramReserved`.
- This is the field referenced in T2.1 (bd-yfv) refinement; coordinate so T2.1 lands the new shape and T11.1 owns the supporting types.

`OverlayReloadPolicy::PerExpertSwitch` is the default for the BringUp profile (T11.2). The other variants are named so future profiles can pick them; they are not implemented as alternative behaviors here, just declared.

Doc comments must explicitly note:
- `overlay_bytes` is per-CompileProfile and constant for that profile.
- `hot_arena_bytes_min` is a floor; actual `ArenaPlan` allocation may exceed it.
- `WramReserved.total <= 8192` (8 KiB WRAM is the absolute upper bound).

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-policy                                  # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- wram_layout                     # Round-trip + invariant tests
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- budget                          # Existing budget tests still pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-policy -- -D warnings                   # No warnings
# Verify: WramReserved serializes and total field equals overlay + runtime-internal use
# Verify: OverlayReloadPolicy is exhaustive over the three documented modes
# Verify: WramLayoutPolicy.overlay_bytes + continuation_bytes + stack_bytes + hot_arena_bytes_min <= 8192
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1 Correctness by Construction: `OverlayReloadPolicy` enum makes reload-cadence exhaustive; `WramReserved` field makes overlay-vs-arena explicit.
- §III.1 Compiler-first: WRAM upper bound is encoded as a debug assertion in constructors so violations are caught at construction.
- §VI.1 Single Source of Truth: WRAM split rules live in `gbf-policy` and are imported by `gbf-train` and `gbf-codegen`; no magic numbers elsewhere.

title: T-B1.4.3: Tile dump pause/resume protocol — runtime side
description: Explicit pause/resume protocol for the tile dump path. Runtime hits a safe point (harness trap, breakpoint, or F-A3 control-plane pause), stops accumulator-slot reuse until host acknowledges. Communicates tile-ready metadata (tile_index, mt, nt, source_addr, byte_len, layout) to host. Eliminates host-poll/ROM-continue race.
notes: RFC source of truth reconciled in PR. Implemented/proven so far: L0-L3 streaming matmul baseline with one loaded ROM per N, BankLease panel copies, tile-safe PC + existing DumpArena-shaped host read/resume, N={32,64,96,128} byte-exact vs gbf-verify, and an honest realism report. Still open and intentionally not claimed: ROM-side F-A3 harness mailbox/control-plane pause/resume, scheduler-visible yield insertion within compute quanta, liveness progress_epoch integration, VBlank/video_commit frame-service widget, real gbf-emu VBlank/Widget/Scheduler/Yield frame events, ordering/cycle attribution from real events, and L4 frame_service_misses==0 hard gate. Current artifact reports frame_service_misses>0 and omits absent L4 timing samples.
close_reason: Completed in F-B1: tile dump pause/resume is exercised by one loaded streaming ROM, repeated tile_safe_point PC stops, F-A3 DumpArena command decoding for accumulator-tile payload reads, one-instruction resume, and host tile reassembly. Evidence: gbf-debug safe-point script reaches first and second tile safe points with differing tile checksums; cargo test -p gbf-bench --lib f_b1_dump_arena_command_pins_existing_harness_op; scripts/review/f-b1/regen.sh.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.4.3: Tile dump pause/resume protocol — runtime side

**Target crate**: `gbf-runtime`
**Target file**: `gbf-runtime/src/harness/tile_pause.rs`

### Background

The naive draft of this RFC said "dump after tile completion." That races the next tile: by the time the host reads the WRAM accumulator, the kernel may already be reusing the slot. This bead implements the explicit pause/resume protocol that prevents the race.

### Protocol

1. Runtime completes one accumulator tile in WRAM.
2. Runtime reaches a harness-visible safe point and **stops reuse** of the accumulator slot.
3. Host issues the existing F-A3 dump command for the accumulator tile address and length.
4. Host reads exactly 1024 bytes.
5. Host appends those bytes into the output matrix in deterministic row-major tile order.
6. Host **acknowledges/resumes** the ROM via the F-A3 control plane.
7. Runtime reuses the same accumulator tile for the next output tile.

### Safe-point implementation choice

Three legal mechanisms (any one is acceptable for F-B1):

- **Harness trap**: emit a debug-trap instruction; the deterministic emulator recognizes it and pauses execution.
- **Breakpoint**: emit at a fixed PC, set by the harness on ROM load.
- **F-A3 control-plane pause**: explicit command-block exchange that pauses the ROM until the host resumes.

The chosen mechanism is recorded in the realism report's `runtime_knobs` and is part of the build identity.

### Tile-ready metadata

Communicated to the host (either by convention via the trap address or via a small in-WRAM descriptor):

```text
tile_index           u16     // 0..(N/16)^2
mt                   u16     // output tile row offset (multiple of 16)
nt                   u16     // output tile col offset (multiple of 16)
source_wram_addr     u16     // WRAM address of the accumulator tile
byte_len             u16     // 1024
tile_internal_layout enum    // row_major_i32_le
```

### Why this is load-bearing

Without explicit pause/resume, the L3/L4 streamed-output path can pass under sequential test runs and fail intermittently under cooperative scheduling once VBlank ISR latency varies. The protocol makes the failure mode unrepresentable.

### Anti-pattern

- Polling a "tile_ready" flag in WRAM from the host while the ROM continues to run. The next tile may overwrite the accumulator before the dump finishes.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- f_b1_tile_pause_safe_point_exists
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- f_b1_tile_pause_blocks_until_resume
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- f_b1_tile_pause_metadata_layout_stable
```

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §2.10.

### Dependencies

- F-A3 (existing harness arena/dump op + control-plane pause).
- F-A5 (runtime nucleus — owns the safe-point hookup).

2. **Test fixtures**: this bead consumes synthetic fixtures from T-B1.0.8 instead of reinventing them. If this bead introduces a new synthetic fixture, it should be added to T-B1.0.8's catalogue with a one-line description in the README.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## REVISION PASS — RFC §2.10 tile metadata patch (2026-05-03)

The "tile-ready metadata" block in the previous RFC threatened to become a hidden new harness ABI. The patch resolves this: tile identity is now **derived**, not communicated through a new ROM-side data structure.

### Updated protocol

1. Runtime completes one accumulator tile in WRAM.
2. Runtime reaches a harness-visible tile-complete safe point, **with no active `BankLease`** (see T-B1.3.4 revision).
3. Host issues the existing F-A3 dump command for the accumulator address and length.
4. Host reads exactly 1024 bytes.
5. Host appends in canonical order.
6. Host acknowledges/resumes via existing F-A3 control plane.
7. Runtime reuses the same accumulator slot.

### Tile identity is derived, not transmitted

Instead of an in-trap `tile_index`/`mt`/`nt` metadata block:

* the host knows the tile schedule from the lowered request (canonical mt-major, nt-minor order);
* the host counts tile-complete safe points it has observed since kernel entry;
* `(tile_index, mt, nt)` follow deterministically from the count.

### Map-side metadata only

Static layout metadata (`source_wram_addr`, `byte_len = 1024`, `tile_internal_layout = row_major_i32_le`) lives in the checked-in `matmul_n128.map.json` artifact (owned by T-B1.6.4), NOT in any per-tile in-trap payload.

### Optional debug-only `TileReadyRecord`

A small WRAM `TileReadyRecord` is allowed as a *debug assertion* (so a harness test can sanity-check the derivation), but:

* it must be dumped through the existing F-A3 memory/arena dump op;
* it must NOT introduce a new `HarnessOp` discriminant;
* it is not part of the closure path — the closure path uses derivation only.

### Anti-patterns to refuse

* Adding a new `HarnessOp::TileReady` variant. Defeats the whole reason F-B1 reuses the F-A3 dump op.
* A trap address whose value encodes `tile_index` numerically. That is a smuggled ABI; use the trap-count derivation instead.
* Polling a WRAM address for "tile ready" without an explicit pause. Reintroduces the host/ROM race the protocol exists to prevent.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-emu -- f_b1_l3_tile_identity_derives_from_trap_count
cargo test -p gbf-emu -- f_b1_l3_pause_with_no_active_banklease
```

The first asserts that the host's tile derivation matches the canonical schedule. The second asserts that every observed pause occurs with `active_lease_count == 0` in the trace.

### Logging discipline

Each tile-complete safe point emits `FB1TraceEvent::TileCompleteSafePoint { mcycle, derived_tile_index, active_lease_count }` (T-B1.0.9). The structured event lets the run-log capture (T-B1.6.11) reconstruct the entire dump cadence post-mortem.

title: Use cryptographic digest for artifact semantic hashes
description: Follow-up from bd-28f6 review: ArtifactCore::semantic_hash and CanonicalTensor::content_hash currently use the local stable_digest helper to produce deterministic 256-bit values, but Hash256 should be backed by a real cryptographic digest such as SHA-256 or BLAKE3 before these hashes become content-addressed artifact identities. Evaluate dependency policy and replace the local FNV-derived helper with a cryptographic implementation.

title: T-B2.15: Stage 0.5 lock/bound enforcement
description:
## What
Implement lock/bound enforcement in the merge resolver: locked knobs
reject overrides; out-of-bounds values are hard rejected; bounds are
monotone-tighter through resolution.

## Why
The `CompileKnobs` surface F-B16 will eventually mutate must be locked
where the profile says "this is non-negotiable for this build class."
Without enforcement, F-B16's loop could silently widen bounds the profile
intended to cap.

## Inputs
- T-B2.13 resolver scaffolding.
- T-B2.12 CompileProfileSpec fixtures (for `KnobLockSet`).
- T-B2.1 diagnostic taxonomy.

## Outputs
- `PolicyKnobLockedAndOverridden { knob: CompileKnobId }` Hard diagnostic.
- `PolicyKnobOutOfBounds { knob, requested, bounds }` Hard diagnostic.
- Bounds-monotonicity check: every `bounds` field at least as tight after
  resolution as before. Violation is an internal-error-class panic in tests
  (a resolver bug, not user input).

## Pitfalls
- Locks apply AFTER profile frames, BEFORE compile-request overrides
  (RFC §7.4 merge rules).
- Out-of-bounds is detected against the *post-merge* bounds, not the
  initial bounds.
- Test with both single-knob locks and lock sets that include nested
  paths (e.g. `ScheduleResourcePressure → wram_hot.hard`).

## Acceptance
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_resolve_policy_rejects_locked_knob_override
- gbf-codegen::f_b2_resolve_policy_rejects_out_of_bounds_value

## Refs
- RFC §7.4 lock/bound rules.
- Depends on: T-B2.13.
- Blocks: T-B2.19.

title: T-B4.12: StageCache key for Stage 2
description:
## What
Wire the Stage 2 StageCache key in `gbf-codegen::stage_cache`, including
failure-memo cell. See RFC §7.8.

## Why
Cache invalidation must be tight enough that schema/feature-flag changes
invalidate, but not so loose that legitimately-cacheable repeat runs miss.
Stage 2's reads are `policy_resolution_self_hash`, `quant_graph_hash`,
`runtime_chrome_budget_hash` (Optional), `target_profile_hash`, plus
versioning.

## Inputs
- T-B4.11 static_budget.v1 schema (for failure memos).
- T-B2.20 StageCache pattern.
- T-B2.13 ResolvedPolicyProduct (for policy_resolution_self_hash).
- T-B4.1 QuantGraphBudgetView (for quant_graph_hash).

## Outputs
- Stage 2 cache key inputs: `policy_resolution_self_hash`, `quant_graph_hash`,
  `runtime_chrome_budget_hash`, `target_profile_hash`, `pass_version_budget`,
  `crate_feature_set_hash`, `static_budget_schema_hash`.
- Success cell: `StaticBudgetReport` + report bytes.
- Failure memo: `static_budget.json` JSON bytes + diagnostics, replayed
  only on exact input-hash match.
- Cache-hit materializes cached canonical bytes (no recanonicalization).

## Pitfalls
- `runtime_chrome_budget_hash` is Optional in the *report*, but in the
  *cache key* a `None` value still hashes to a stable representation
  distinct from `Some(...)`.
- `pass_version_budget` bumps when Stage 2 logic changes; treat like an ABI version.
- `static_budget_schema_hash` changes whenever static_budget.v1's schema
  or canonicalization rules change.

## Acceptance
- gbf-codegen::stage_cache_key_budget_is_deterministic
- gbf-codegen::stage_cache_failed_pass_does_not_enter_success_cache (already from T-B2.20; reaffirmed for Stage 2)
- gbf-codegen::stage_cache_failure_memo_replays_only_on_exact_input_match (Stage 2 variant)

## Refs
- RFC §7.8 keys.
- Depends on: T-B2.20, T-B4.11.
- Blocks: T-B4.13.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## REVISION PASS — RFC §8.5 hard-gate patch (2026-05-03)

The L4 hard-gate suite gains three new gate items and a gated-frame-interval clarification.

### Updated hard-gate block

```text
frame_service_misses == 0
widget_update_count == gated_frame_count
scheduler_service_count == gated_frame_count
max_no_progress_frames <= 1
max_unyielded_compute_mcycles <= 17_556 - soft_deadline_margin_mcycles                  <-- new
max_bank_lease_hold_mcycles <= 17_556 - soft_deadline_margin_mcycles                    <-- new
byte_exact_match == true
yield_while_bank_lease_active_count == 0
harness_pause_while_bank_lease_active_count == 0                                        <-- new (T-B1.3.4 revision)
```

All count equality gates are computed over the gated frame interval only — i.e. excluding startup, shutdown, and host-paused tile-dump intervals. The interval is `[gated_frame_start, gated_frame_end_exclusive)` from the realism report's `scheduling` block (T-B1.6.1 revision).

### Why these three additions

* `max_unyielded_compute_mcycles` directly bounds how long compute can run without giving the cooperative scheduler a chance to fire. The widget+scheduler counters can pass even with a single multi-frame compute spike if the spike happens to straddle exactly one frame; this gate removes the loophole.
* `max_bank_lease_hold_mcycles` bounds scheduler starvation caused by long bank-lease holds. A panel copy under lease is non-yieldable; a runaway copy could starve service even if the post-copy code yields normally.
* `harness_pause_while_bank_lease_active_count` extends the no-yield-under-lease invariant to harness pauses (see T-B1.3.4 revision).

### New tests

```rust
#[test]
#[ignore = "Heavy"]
fn f_b1_l4_max_unyielded_compute_within_frame_budget() { /* ... */ }

#[test]
#[ignore = "Heavy"]
fn f_b1_l4_max_bank_lease_hold_within_frame_budget() { /* ... */ }

#[test]
#[ignore = "Heavy"]
fn f_b1_l4_no_harness_pause_while_banklease_active() { /* ... */ }
```

These join the existing three `#[ignore]`d tests; T-B1.6.9 (ignored discipline) requires all six to remain `#[ignore]`d.

### Logging discipline

When a gate fails, `FB1TraceEvent::ValidatorRejected { rule, evidence }` records which gate fired, with concrete evidence (the `mcycle` window, the lease ID, the frame index). T-B1.6.11's run-log capture surfaces these in the post-mortem summary.

### What did NOT change

- The hard-gate-or-RFC-amendment escalation rule (RFC §0, §9.4) is unchanged.
- `byte_exact_match` is still the headline correctness gate.
- The original three gates (`frame_service_misses`, `widget_update_count`, `scheduler_service_count`) are unchanged.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.6: L5 — Realism report + review packet + closure skill

### Background

L5 freezes the M0.5 empirical baseline. The deliverable is `docs/review/f-b1/artifacts/realism_report.v1.json`, bit-reproducible from pinned inputs, plus the review packet (scope, architecture, claim-to-gate, reviewer-checklist, known-debt, reproducibility, generated-artifacts).

### Why the report is the deliverable, not the kernel

The kernel is a means to an end. The thing M1 actually inherits is the **measured cost of bringup compute** — cycles per product-accumulate, ROM bandwidth, bank-switch frequency, frame budget consumption, WRAM/HRAM/SRAM peaks. M1's `QuantSpec` and `D_model` choices are data-driven by these numbers.

### Reproducibility — load-bearing

The report records only emulated cycle counts and emulator-deterministic outputs. **No host wall-clock fields**. `wall_clock_seconds_at_gb_clock_f64` is derived from the DMG clock, not the host. Two consecutive `regen.sh` runs must diff to zero.

`git_dirty` is evaluated before artifact writes in a fresh checkout. Regeneration is valid only if the final worktree diff is clean after the script completes.

`report_self_hash` is computed over canonical JSON with the field zeroed; the emitter writes the final hash afterward.

### Schema fields — load-bearing decisions baked in

- **No `artifact_core_hash`.** F-B1 has no `ArtifactCore`; including the field would violate the milestone's own non-goals.
- **`compute_bringup_request_hash`** (not `compile_request_hash`) — F-B1 does not have the real `CompileRequest`.
- Identity fields: `build_hash`, `runtime_nucleus_hash`, `compute_bringup_request_hash`, `asm_ir_hash`, `rom_sha256`, `operand_fixture_hash`, `quarter_square_table_hash`.
- Memory bandwidth split: `rom_bank0_table_bytes_read` vs `romx_operand_bytes_read` (different traffic classes).
- Banking split: `logical_bank_switches_*` vs `mbc_rom_bank_register_write_count` (these are different things).
- Banking includes `max_active_bank_leases` and `yield_while_bank_lease_active_count` (the safety invariant).
- `rom_layout` block reports `bank0_used_bytes`, `bank0_free_bytes`, `romx_operand_bank_size_bytes`.
- Scheduling: `frame_service_misses` (renamed from `vblank_misses`), `scheduler_service_count`, `cycles_remaining_at_widget_tick`, `cycles_remaining_at_scheduler_service` (distinguishing ISR-update from cooperative service).
- Conformance hashes split: `reference_source_hash`, `fixture_hash`, `expected_output_hash`.
- Compute costs use `cycles_per_product_accumulate` (not `cycles_per_multiply`) — the measured step includes table lookup, product reconstruction, accumulator update, and loop overhead.

### Companion artifacts

- `cycle_attribution_n128.json` — per-metric attribution rules so M1 can interpret the report.
- `matmul_n128.map.json` — machine-readable Bank0 layout, table placement, symbol sizes.

### Subtask plan

- **T-B1.6.1** `realism_report.v1` schema in `gbf-report` + self-hash convention + acceptance/rejection tests.
- **T-B1.6.2** `gbf-bench::MatmulI8Bringup` workload runner + sweep over N ∈ {32, 64, 96, 128}.
- **T-B1.6.3** Report aggregation logic + cycle attribution per `cycle_attribution rule`.
- **T-B1.6.4** Companion artifacts (`cycle_attribution_n128.json`, `matmul_n128.map.json`).
- **T-B1.6.5** Review packet scripts (`regen.sh`, `verify-packet.sh`).
- **T-B1.6.6** Review packet markdown documents.
- **T-B1.6.8** Frozen baseline check-in + reproducibility verification.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-report -- realism_report_v1_schema_accepts_fixture
cargo test -p gbf-report -- realism_report_v1_rejects_missing_required_fields
cargo test -p gbf-report -- realism_report_v1_self_hash_round_trip
cargo test -p gbf-bench -- f_b1_report_aggregation_known_fixture
./scripts/review/f-b1/regen.sh
./scripts/review/f-b1/verify-packet.sh
```

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §10, §14.

### Dependencies

- T-B1.5 (L4) — cooperative gate working at N=128 (or waiver path active).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## REVISION PASS — Two structural-counts and dev-loop beads added

Two new sub-beads close gaps that were latent in the original L5 plan:

| Bead       | Purpose                                                                                  |
|------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| T-B1.6.12  | Runtime structural-counts hard gate per N — products / output_tiles / panel_copies match formula |
| T-B1.6.13  | F-B1 dev-loop smoke entrypoint script (`scripts/review/f-b1/smoke.sh`) with verbose tracing |

### Why T-B1.6.12

RFC §10.5 lists structural counts ("products = N³, output tiles = (N/16)², panel copies = (N/16)³ × 2") as **sanity bands, not gates**. That framing is too loose for the runtime side. Cycle counts are measurement-defined, but structural counts are exact integer functions of N and tile shape. Putting them under a hard gate means a silent miscount (kernel skipped a tile, aggregator double-counted, runtime trace missed an event) fails immediately with a precise diagnostic — instead of surfacing as an opaque byte-exact mismatch in T-B1.4.6 / T-B1.5.6.

The bead is dynamic and complements T-B1.4.5 (static tile-schedule validator). T-B1.4.5 walks the lowered AsmIR; T-B1.6.12 watches the runtime trace.

### Why T-B1.6.13

The original L5 plan has two heavy-side scripts (`regen.sh`, `verify-packet.sh`) and one fast-side gate (the pre-commit hook). There is no dedicated F-B1 fast entrypoint with verbose tracing. New contributors hit the friction first; existing developers fall back to remembering individual `cargo test -p <crate> -- <pattern>` invocations. The smoke script gives F-B1 a named, layered, RUST_LOG=info-by-default dev-loop entrypoint.

Three-tier model after the addition:

| Script | When to run | Wall clock |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| `cargo test --workspace --all-features` (pre-commit hook) | every commit | seconds |
| `scripts/review/f-b1/smoke.sh` (T-B1.6.13) | every dev iteration | tens of seconds |
| `scripts/review/f-b1/regen.sh` (T-B1.6.5) | before opening PR; nightly via T-B1.6.10 | minutes |
| `scripts/review/f-b1/verify-packet.sh` (T-B1.6.5) | before merge | 2× regen.sh |

T-B1.6.9 (ignored discipline) protects the boundary: smoke.sh must not run any `#[ignore]`d test name listed there.

### Updated dependency graph

- **T-B1.6.8 (frozen baseline)** now blocks on T-B1.6.12 and T-B1.6.13 in addition to its previous deps.
- **T-B1.6.12** blocks on T-B1.6.3 (aggregation surfaces counts), T-B1.0.9 (typed trace vocabulary), T-B1.0.8 (synthetic-trace fixtures for formula tests), T-B1.6.2 (bench runner provides the trace).
- **T-B1.6.13** blocks on T-B1.6.5 (scripts directory exists), T-B1.6.2 (smoke.sh runs a subset of its tests), T-B1.0.9 (RUST_LOG-visible tracing events), T-B1.6.9 (ignored discipline guard).
- **T-B1.6.2 (bench runner)** gains a direct dep on T-B1.0.9 (it emits the structured tracing T-B1.6.11 captures; was previously transitive only).
- **T-B1.4.4 (host reassembly)** gains a direct dep on T-B1.0.9 (per-tile arrival events; was previously transitive only).

### What did NOT change

- L0 → L1 → L2 → L3 → L4 → L5 layered structure is unchanged.
- The hard 60 Hz cooperative-frame-service gate is unchanged.
- The "no silent close, escalate via RFC amendment" rule is unchanged.
- The existing 11 sub-beads under T-B1.6 are unchanged in scope.
- The existing claim-to-gate matrix in RFC §12 still holds; T-B1.6.12 adds new gate rows for structural counts.

### Total L5 sub-bead count after this pass

13 sub-beads under T-B1.6:

- T-B1.6.1 schema
- T-B1.6.2 bench runner
- T-B1.6.3 aggregation
- T-B1.6.4 companion artifacts
- T-B1.6.5 review packet scripts (regen.sh, verify-packet.sh)
- T-B1.6.6 review packet markdown
- T-B1.6.7 closure skill
- T-B1.6.8 frozen baseline check-in (gates everything)
- T-B1.6.9 ignored discipline meta-test
- T-B1.6.10 CI workflow
- T-B1.6.11 run-log capture
- **T-B1.6.12 structural-counts hard gate (new)**
- **T-B1.6.13 dev-loop smoke script (new)**

title: T-B1.4.6: L3 N=128 emulator test (heavy, ignored — owned by review script)
description: Full headline N=128 cross-bank streamed-output emulator test. Marked #[ignore] because heavy: ~2M product-accumulates, ~64 streamed tiles, ~1024 panel copies. Plain cargo test must not unexpectedly run it. Owned by ./scripts/review/f-b1/regen.sh.
close_reason: Implemented L3 streamed-output building blocks now proven by a single streaming ROM per size. Evidence: BringupStoragePlan/OutputBinding/HarnessTileStream and BringupArenaPlan non-overlap validation; tile_schedule covers every output tile exactly once; gbf-bench reassembles TileDump bytes into full matrices; build_l3_streaming_rom loops mt/nt/kt inside one ROM, reaches a repeated tile-safe PC, and host performs the existing DumpArena-shaped accumulator read/resume; N=128 byte-exact output is regenerated by cargo run -p gbf-test --bin f_b1_regen. ASM checklist: streaming ROM uses typed gbf-asm sections, BankLease lowering for panel copies, no raw MBC writes, and no harness/tile safe point while a lease is active. Open sibling bd-h5aj remains for ROM-side harness mailbox/control-plane pause-resume.

comments.author: bkase
bkase
comments.text:
## REVISION PASS — Cross-cutting testing & logging discipline applied

This bead now blocks on one or more of the new discipline beads added in the audit pass. Specifically:

- **T-B1.0.8** — test fixture infrastructure (synthetic AsmIR, frame traces, expected outputs).
- **T-B1.0.9** — typed tracing event vocabulary + subscriber-level test capture (per AGENTS.md "Logging helper closure must cite subscriber-level capture for event shape").

### Required additions for this bead

1. **Logging discipline**: every event this bead emits or consumes uses T-B1.0.9's typed `FB1TraceEvent` enum, never raw stringly fields. Tests use `CapturedTrace::capture(...)` to assert event shape, not just count.

2. **Test fixtures**: this bead consumes synthetic fixtures from T-B1.0.8 instead of reinventing them. If this bead introduces a new synthetic fixture, it should be added to T-B1.0.8's catalogue with a one-line description in the README.

4. **`run_latest.log.summary.json` cross-reference**: if this bead's behavior is observable in the heavy-gate run (i.e., it emits typed events that the run-log captures), the summary's `tracing_event_counts` field gains an entry for those event types. The diff in that file across runs is the regression signal.

### What did not change

The bead's original specification, types, validation rules, and acceptance gates remain authoritative. This comment adds the discipline overlay; it does not replace anything.

title: T5.1: Implement temporal switch penalty (L_switch)
description: Implement L_switch = (1/LT) sum_{l,t} (1 - dot(p_{l,t}, p_{l,t-1})) where p_{l,t} is the soft expert distribution. Requires access to consecutive token positions within a training batch. Must handle sequence boundaries (don't penalize switches across sequence boundaries). The gradient flows through the router's softmax output.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::switch passes; Identical consecutive dists -> loss=0; Orthogonal -> loss=1; Sequence boundaries handled

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/loss/switch.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "temporal switch penalty", "L_switch", "consecutive tokens", "bank-switch cost".

### Implementation Notes
- Temporal switch penalty: `L_switch = (1/LT) Σ_{l,t} (1 - ⟨p_{l,t}, p_{l,t-1}⟩)`
- Where p_{l,t} is the soft routing distribution at layer l, token t.
- Inner product between consecutive-token routing distributions penalizes switching.
- Must handle sequence boundaries: reset at start of each sequence (no penalty for first token).
- `pub fn temporal_switch_loss(routing_probs: &Tensor, sequence_mask: &Tensor) -> Tensor`
  - routing_probs shape: [batch, seq_len, n_layers, n_experts]
  - sequence_mask: marks sequence boundaries

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::switch         # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings        # No warnings
# Verify: identical consecutive distributions -> loss = 0
# Verify: orthogonal consecutive distributions -> loss = 1
# Verify: sequence boundaries correctly handled (no cross-sequence penalty)
# Verify: gradient flows correctly
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.2: Pure function — tensor in, scalar out
- §III.2: Property test — loss is in [0, 1] range

title: T10.1: Create tiny model test fixtures and test helper utilities
description: Create a test fixtures module with: (a) A micro MoE model config: d_model=8, d_ff=16, n_experts=2, n_layers=2, vocab=32, BoundedKv sequence, one MoE block at layer 1. (b) Factory functions: make_tiny_model(), make_tiny_artifact(), make_tiny_compile_request(), make_tiny_workload(). (c) A deterministic random tensor generator seeded by name (for reproducible test data). (d) Assertion helpers: assert_tensor_close(actual, expected, atol, rtol) with detailed diff output on failure, assert_bytes_equal(a, b) with hex diff on first divergent byte, assert_artifact_valid(artifact) that checks all manifest invariants. (e) A tiny prompt corpus (8 prompts of 4-16 tokens each) for pipeline tests. All fixtures must be constructible without any training — they use hand-set or random weights.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-test exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures passes; tiny_moe_config produces valid model; deterministic_tensor with same seed = identical output
close_reason:
## Closure Summary

Implemented deterministic tiny fixture infrastructure in `gbf-test`:

- `tiny_moe_config()` builds the requested micro config: `d_model=8`, `d_ff=16`, `n_experts=2`, `n_layers=2`, `vocab=32`, BoundedKv sequence config, dense layer 0, and MoE layer 1.
- `make_tiny_model()` owns the same embedding, dense FFN, router, and expert-block state consumed by artifact export, so fixture factories no longer recreate private model truth.
- `make_tiny_artifact()` returns a validated `ArtifactCore` built from the currently exportable tiny model surfaces. Sequence-state manifest semantics are not claimed while those contracts are still stubs.
- `make_tiny_compile_request()` and `make_tiny_workload()` return explicitly named placeholder types until `gbf-policy` and `gbf-workload` define final request/manifest contracts.
- Deterministic tensor generation supports numeric and name seeds.
- Assertion helpers cover tensor shapes/values, raw `f32` slices, byte diffs, and artifact-core tensor/quant invariants including content-hash self-consistency.

## Review Feedback Applied

- Left the unrelated `history/planv0.md` changes out of scope and unstaged.
- Made `TinyModel` the single source of fixture state used by artifact generation.
- Added dense FFN layer-0 export coverage to match the tiny topology where current export APIs exist.
- Renamed compile/workload fixture structs as placeholders and recorded bd-209g for final contract factories.
- Narrowed artifact validation to `ArtifactCore` and added a stale `content_hash` regression test.
- Added slice-based assertion helpers plus negative tests that pin diagnostic panic messages.

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Tiny MoE config has the requested dimensions, two layers, BoundedKv sequence config, and one MoE block at layer 1. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_tiny_moe_config_is_micro_and_instantiable` | Also verifies model fixture owns embedding, dense FFN, router, and experts. |
| Deterministic tensor generation repeats by seed and name. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_deterministic_tensor_repeats_for_same_seed_and_name` | Checks same seed/name equality, different seed/name inequality, and finite values. |
| Tiny prompt corpus has 8 small vocab-bounded prompts. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_prompt_corpus_is_tiny_and_vocab_bounded` | Guards the 4-16 token range and vocab size. |
| Tiny artifact core is valid, deterministic, and covers embedding/dense/router/expert exportable surfaces. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_make_tiny_artifact_is_valid_and_deterministic` | Does not claim full manifest or sequence-state artifact semantics. |
| Placeholder compile/workload factories are deterministic and internally consistent. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_make_compile_request_and_workload_are_consistent` | Placeholder names are explicit. |
| Assertion helpers accept matching inputs and expose raw-slice adapters. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_assert_helpers_accept_matching_inputs` | Covers `TestTensor`, raw tensor values, raw `f32` slices, and bytes. |
| Assertion helper diagnostics identify the first bad value/byte. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_assert_helpers_report_first_bad_value_and_byte` | Guards failure-message usefulness. |
| Artifact-core validation rejects stale tensor content hashes. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_artifact_core_validation_rejects_stale_tensor_content_hash` | Guards against accepting corrupted deserialized/manual cores. |
| The crate compiles and lints cleanly. | `cargo check -p gbf-test`; `cargo clippy -p gbf-test -- -D warnings` | Passed after review fixes. |

## Non-Goals

- No executable end-to-end forward model is claimed.
- No final `CompileRequest`, `WorkloadManifest`, `ArtifactManifest`, or sequence-state artifact schema is claimed.
- No performance benchmark is claimed beyond the tiny constants and test runtime.

## Feedback Harness Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Tiny model config and artifact factory had competing truth. | Artifact generation recreated QAT state privately instead of consuming a model fixture. | Added AGENTS fixture guidance: one tiny fixture source of truth. | `fixtures_make_tiny_artifact_is_valid_and_deterministic`; `fixtures_tiny_moe_config_is_micro_and_instantiable` | `AGENTS.md`; `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures` |
| Compile/workload stand-ins looked like final contracts. | Policy/workload modules are still stubs, but fixture names did not say placeholder. | Added AGENTS fixture guidance: name placeholders explicitly and record later contract work. | Explicit `TinyCompileRequestPlaceholder` / `TinyWorkloadManifestPlaceholder` names; bd-209g | `AGENTS.md`; bd-209g |
| Artifact validation overclaimed manifest invariants. | Helper name and bead text blurred `ArtifactCore` with future manifest contracts. | Added AGENTS fixture guidance: scope artifact assertions to the existing artifact type. | `fixtures_artifact_core_validation_rejects_stale_tensor_content_hash` | `AGENTS.md`; `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures` |
| Assertion helper diagnostics were only happy-path tested. | The first implementation did not test failure-message quality. | Keep diagnostic helper tests negative as well as positive. | `fixtures_assert_helpers_report_first_bad_value_and_byte` | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures` |
| Unrelated plan edits polluted review context. | Pre-existing dirty `history/planv0.md` was visible in diff reviews. | No action in code; explicitly keep unrelated dirty files unstaged and call them out in closure. | `git status --short` before staging | Session workflow |

Commands run:

- `cargo fmt --check --all`
- `git diff --check`
- `cargo check -p gbf-test`
- `cargo clippy -p gbf-test -- -D warnings`
- `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures`
- `cargo test -p gbf-test`
- `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`

## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-test`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-test/src/fixtures.rs` or `gbf-test/tests/fixtures/mod.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "tiny model", "test fixtures", "micro MoE".

### Implementation Notes
Create a micro MoE model config for testing:
```rust
pub fn tiny_moe_config() -> MoeCharConfig {
    MoeCharConfig {
        d_model: 8,
        d_ff: 16,
        n_experts: 2,
        n_layers: 2,
        vocab_size: 32,
        // ... other fields with minimal values
    }
}
```
Also provide:
- `pub fn deterministic_tensor(shape: &[usize], seed: u64) -> Tensor` — seeded RNG tensor generator
- `pub fn tiny_prompt_corpus() -> Vec<Vec<u8>>` — handful of byte sequences for testing
- `pub fn assert_tensor_close(a: &Tensor, b: &Tensor, atol: f32)` — assertion helper with informative error
- `pub fn create_tiny_model(config: &MoeCharConfig) -> Model` — factory function

All fixtures must be deterministic given a seed.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-test                         # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures               # Tests pass
# Verify: tiny_moe_config produces a valid, instantiable model
# Verify: deterministic_tensor with same seed produces identical output
# Verify: tiny_prompt_corpus has at least 3 sequences
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §II.1: Tiny model must be FAST — d_model=8 means tests run in milliseconds
- §IV.3: Deterministic fixtures — same seed, same tensors, always
- §III.2: These fixtures ARE the test infrastructure — get them right
