| QuantSpec can represent mixed precision with `Some(plan)` and `None` weight groups | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- quant` | none | Covers default expert plan, mixed precision, old-shape serde, and new-shape round-trip |
| ArtifactCore rejects missing or inconsistent quant metadata | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact` | none | Covers missing tensor refs, missing weight entries, missing ternary details, and plan mismatches |
| ExportVisitor populates weight quant metadata for ternary and full-precision weights | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` | none | Covers expert ternary, router/shared dense, embedding, and classifier entries |
| Default expert plan is applied by scalar/export default path | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::ternary qat::export` | none | Proves `TernaryLinearQat::canonical_weight_plan()` matches artifact default and export records it |
| gbf-model remains Burn-free and QAT backend seams stay contained | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Architecture invariant still passes |
| QAT closure/harness rules stay satisfied | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py` | none | Run after closure sync |

## No-Future Variant Rule

`WeightQuantEntry` is accepted only for behaviors validated today: `Some(plan)` with complete ternary metadata, or `None` with full-precision deployable weight tensors. Malformed combinations are rejected by `ArtifactCore::new`. The annealed threshold runtime scheduler is not claimed here and is owned by `bd-xpr7`.

title: T-B1.2.3: MatmulI8Node IR in gbf-ir::infer
description: Single-node IR: InferNode::MatmulI8(MatmulI8Node) | End. Fields: a/b/out TensorBindings, dim, tile_size. F-B1 invariants: exactly one MatmulI8 + one End; A/B read-only; output harness-streamed; no effect edges; no range/observation hooks; no quantization metadata. Type body documents M1-deferred items.
close_reason: Implemented L1 request/IR/lowering scaffold per F-B1 RFC. Evidence: ComputeBringupRequest validation/hash/import event, OperandLayout/RomBankId/RomBankOffset validation including nonzero-offset coverage, gbf-ir::infer MatmulI8 node, deterministic lower_request_to_ir/lower_request_to_asmir tests, and f_b1_l1_compiled_matmul_matches_reference emulator gate. ASM checklist: L1 AsmIR is symbolic pre-layout Section IR with typed BankLease/Yield ops; concrete ROM correctness is gated by the L0/L1 emulator byte-exact tests. Deferred: durable M1 CompileRequest remains out of scope.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.2.3: `MatmulI8Node` IR in `gbf-ir::infer`

**Target crate**: `gbf-ir`
**Target file**: `gbf-ir/src/infer.rs`

### Background

`gbf-ir::infer` is the inference IR that M1 will eventually populate with norm, activation, router, expert dispatch, embed, unembed, etc. F-B1 introduces the IR with exactly **one** node variant — `MatmulI8` — plus a terminator. The single-node graph forecloses any premature commitment to an effect-edge model M1 has not yet designed.

### Types

```rust
pub enum InferNode {
    MatmulI8(MatmulI8Node),
    End,
}

pub struct MatmulI8Node {
    pub a: TensorBinding,
    pub b: TensorBinding,
    pub out: TensorBinding,
    pub dim: SquareDim,
    pub tile_size: TileSize,
}
```

### Invariants F-B1 enforces

- The graph contains exactly one `MatmulI8` and one `End`.
- A and B are read-only.
- The output is harness-streamed (not written to a tensor binding M1 would observe).
- No effect-edge graph yet.
- No range annotations.
- No observation hooks.
- No quantization metadata.

### Deferred to M1 (do NOT add in F-B1)

- All other op kinds (`Norm`, `Activation`, `RouterTopK`, `ExpertDispatch`, `Embed`, `Unembed`, ...).
- Effect edges. F-B1's single-op IR has trivial effect ordering; M1 introduces the graph.
- `ObservationPlan` visitor hooks.
- `RangePlan` annotation slots.
- `LifetimeClass` on `TensorBinding`.

The type body must contain a clear `// M1: deferred` comment naming each missing field.

### Anti-pattern to refuse

- Generalizing `MatmulI8` to `Matmul<T>`. M1's quantized kernel is a different node type with a different effect profile (rescale, requantise, possibly fuse with norm). Generalising prematurely commits to the wrong axis.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-ir -- f_b1_matmul_ir_single_node_shape
cargo test -p gbf-ir -- f_b1_matmul_ir_rejects_multi_op_graph
cargo test -p gbf-ir -- f_b1_matmul_ir_a_b_are_read_only
cargo test -p gbf-ir -- f_b1_matmul_ir_no_effect_edges
```

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §7.4.

### Dependencies

- T-B1.0.1 (`SquareDim`).
- T-B1.2.1 (`TileSize`).

title: T2.6: Synthetic reference RuntimeChromeBudget for pre-runtime training (M1-M2)
description: Provide a deterministic synthetic RuntimeChromeBudget for use before a real runtime shell exists. Same schema as the real emission path (T2.2) so trainer is not branchy. Hash field uses a stable, explicitly-marked sentinel (e.g., runtime_nucleus_hash with a known SYNTHETIC_REFERENCE prefix and reference_shell_modules = minimal+UI set). Stored as a fixture under fixtures/calibration/ or similar. Real shell emission supersedes it.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate(s)**: `gbf-train` (loader + fixture path), `gbf-policy` (helper to construct), `fixtures/calibration/` (the actual JSON).
**Target file(s)**: `fixtures/calibration/synthetic_reference_budget.json` (new), `gbf-policy/src/synthetic.rs` (new — constructs / validates), `gbf-train/src/preflight.rs` (uses).

**Reference**: bd-1hv 2026-04-26 session ("Pre-runtime: synthetic budget path"), T2.2 refinement (bd-1g9), T2.4 (bd-37r).

### Implementation Notes

This task lets training begin at M1–M2, before a real `gbf-runtime` shell exists, by providing a deterministic synthetic `RuntimeChromeBudget`.

1. **Schema parity with real emission.**
   - The synthetic budget MUST round-trip through the same `serde` types as the real one (no parallel struct).
   - `reference_shell_modules` is populated with the minimal+UI set from `pinned_reference_shell()` (T2.4).
   - `runtime_nucleus_hash` uses a stable, explicitly-marked sentinel:
     ```
     0x53594E54__48455449435F5345544348__... // "SYNT" "HETI" "C_SE" "TCH..."
     ```
     Or simpler: `Hash256::from_str("synthetic-reference-shell-v1").into_marked()`. The point is that the value is recognizable in logs and impossible to confuse with a real hash.

2. **Concrete byte estimates.**
   - Bank0Free: estimate 8 KiB after minimal+UI nucleus (Bank 0 is 16 KiB; UI/scheduler/etc. estimated at 8 KiB).
   - CommonBank: 1 slot × 16 KiB usable_bytes − reserved_slack=512.
   - ExpertBank: 8 slots × 16 KiB usable_bytes − reserved_slack=384 each.
   - WramReserved: overlay=512, hot_arena_floor=4096, total=1536 (overlay + continuation + stack + small runtime slack).
   - sram_reserved: 1024 (sequence-state + commit metadata reservation under T2.4).
   - Future-module reservations from T2.4 are subtracted from the relevant slots and recorded.

   These numbers must be reviewed and updated as the real shell forms; they are CONSERVATIVE starting points so a model trained against them likely fits the eventual real shell.

3. **Loader.**
   - `gbf-train::preflight` falls back to the synthetic budget when no real `RuntimeChromeBudget` is available.
   - Trainer emits a structured log line at run start: `{ "synthetic_reference_budget": true, "hash": "0x...", "fixture": "fixtures/calibration/synthetic_reference_budget.json" }`.
   - If the user explicitly passes a real budget path, the synthetic is ignored (real wins).

4. **Marked nature.**
   - The CI drift gate (T2.5) MUST treat synthetic-marked hashes as a separate cohort — drift in synthetic is a fixture update (low ceremony), drift in real is a release-note-required promotion.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                                            # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- synthetic::round_trip                    # JSON round-trips
cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight::synthetic_fallback             # Trainer uses synthetic when no real budget present
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings                              # No warnings
# Verify: synthetic budget passes the same preflight as a real one (schema parity)
# Verify: `runtime_nucleus_hash` for synthetic is detectably synthetic (a structured check, not a string match)
# Verify: trainer log line clearly indicates synthetic-mode at run start
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: Same `RuntimeChromeBudget` type for synthetic and real eliminates "two paths" drift.
- §IV.3: Reproducible — checked-in JSON fixture means every run sees the same synthetic budget.
- §V.1: Structured log indicates synthetic-mode (machine-readable for CI).
- §VI.1: Fixture is in-repo at `fixtures/calibration/`.

title: F14: ModelSizeProfile registry — Toy0/Toy1/MoeTiny/UpperBankCandidate dim caps in gbf-policy
description: Named model size profiles with hard dim caps live in gbf-policy alongside CompileProfile. Profiles: Toy0 (d_model=16, d_ff=32, dense-only), Toy1 (d_model=32, d_ff=64, dense-only), MoeTiny (d_model=64, d_ff=128, n_experts=2|4 — first MoE bring-up at ~4.5KiB/expert), UpperBankCandidate (d_model=96|128, d_ff=192, n_experts=4 — risky upper). ModelTopologyConfig::from_profile validates dim caps against TernaryWeightPlan::compute_byte_cost vs the smallest ExpertBank slot. (d_model=128, d_ff=256) and (d_model=256, d_ff=512) are explicitly disallowed in this registry — they exceed 16 KiB for one expert's two matrices alone. Per planv0.md 'Session amendment 2026-05-06'.

title: T-B1.3.3: No-raw-MBC-writes static checker
description: Two-layer check: (1) gbf-asm effect classifier flags MBC-range writes as MBC effects; non-privileged sections fail. (2) F-B1 codegen-validator asserts the matmul kernel sections contain no MBC effects. Synthetic-rejection test ensures the validator is not silently a no-op. Operates on typed AsmIR, not bytes.
close_reason: Implemented L2 cross-bank BankLease path per F-B1 RFC. Evidence: BringupRomWindowPlan records Bank0 resources plus A/B switchable banks; generated L2/L3 streaming ROMs acquire/release BankLease via gbf-runtime banking lowering instead of raw MBC writes; validator rejects raw MBC writes, leaked/unknown/duplicate leases, and yield while a lease is active; emulator gates f_b1_l2_cross_bank_matmul_matches_reference and f_b1_l3_streaming_rom_nonzero_offsets_match_reference_n32 pass; N=64 byte-exact cross-bank run is in docs/review/f-b1/artifacts/realism_report.v1.json from gbf-test f_b1_regen. ASM checklist: symbolic pre-layout BankLease ops lower through audited runtime banking; raw MBC writes are excluded by typed effect validation and tests.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B1.3.3: No-raw-MBC-writes static checker

**Target crate**: `gbf-asm` (effect classification) and `gbf-codegen` (consumer)
**Target file**: `gbf-asm/src/effect_class.rs` (extends), `gbf-codegen/src/validators/no_raw_mbc.rs`

### Background

The audited runtime banking implementation is the **only** code allowed to write MBC5 registers (`$0000`, `$2000`, `$3000`, `$4000`). Any direct write from generated code violates the BankLease discipline and would let the kernel and the HRAM bank shadow drift apart.

This bead implements two complementary checks:

1. **At the AsmIR effect-classification layer**: a write to any address in the MBC range is classified as an MBC effect. Sections without privilege to perform MBC effects fail validation if they emit one.
2. **At the F-B1 codegen-validator layer**: the matmul kernel's emitted sections are explicitly checked to contain no MBC effects.

### Why two layers

The effect classifier (`gbf-asm`) is the structural mechanism. The F-B1-specific validator is the narrow *F-B1* assertion that the matmul kernel in particular contains no raw MBC writes. They serve different correctness questions and either alone is insufficient.

### Acceptance gates

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- effect_classification_flags_direct_mbc_writes
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_l2_generated_code_has_no_raw_mbc_writes
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- f_b1_l2_validator_rejects_synthetic_raw_mbc_write
```

The third test deliberately constructs a synthetic AsmIR section that performs a raw MBC write and asserts the validator rejects it. This guards against the validator silently regressing to a no-op.

### Anti-pattern

- Implementing the validator as a string match against generated bytes. The validator must operate on the typed AsmIR, not on encoded bytes.

### RFC reference

`history/rfcs/F-B1-compute-bringup.md` §3 (goals: narrow validator), §7.8.

### Dependencies

- T-B1.3.2 (lowering uses BankLease) — checker validates *that* output.
- F-A1 (`MachineEffect`/`PrivilegeClass` from gbf-asm).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## REVISION PASS — Cross-cutting testing & logging discipline applied

This bead now blocks on one or more of the new discipline beads added in the audit pass. Specifically:

- **T-B1.0.8** — test fixture infrastructure (synthetic AsmIR, frame traces, expected outputs).
- **T-B1.0.9** — typed tracing event vocabulary + subscriber-level test capture (per AGENTS.md "Logging helper closure must cite subscriber-level capture for event shape").

### Required additions for this bead

1. **Logging discipline**: every event this bead emits or consumes uses T-B1.0.9's typed `FB1TraceEvent` enum, never raw stringly fields. Tests use `CapturedTrace::capture(...)` to assert event shape, not just count.

2. **Test fixtures**: this bead consumes synthetic fixtures from T-B1.0.8 instead of reinventing them. If this bead introduces a new synthetic fixture, it should be added to T-B1.0.8's catalogue with a one-line description in the README.

4. **`run_latest.log.summary.json` cross-reference**: if this bead's behavior is observable in the heavy-gate run (i.e., it emits typed events that the run-log captures), the summary's `tracing_event_counts` field gains an entry for those event types. The diff in that file across runs is the regression signal.

### What did not change

The bead's original specification, types, validation rules, and acceptance gates remain authoritative. This comment adds the discipline overlay; it does not replace anything.

title: F-A1: gbf-asm — Typed LR35902 eDSL + Encoder + Cycle Model + Layout
description: Fill the ten module stubs in gbf-asm (isa, section, provenance, symbols, builder, cycle_model, layout, relax, encoder, listing) plus a small ROM builder. AsmIR is the only authoring layer for executable code per planv0.md engineering rule 1. ReachabilityValidation later relies on MachineEffect + PrivilegeClass typing introduced here.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-A1: `gbf-asm` — Typed LR35902 eDSL + Encoder + Cycle Model + Layout

### Background

`gbf-asm` is the Rust eDSL that authors **all** generated executable code in the system. Per the constitution-grade rule from `planv0.md` line 2492 ("All generated executable code originates from `AsmIR` / `Instr` / audited runtime builders, never from ad hoc byte pushes"), there is no other path to bytes-on-cartridge. `Raw(Vec<u8>)` exists only as a tightly audited escape hatch.

This feature fills the ten module stubs in `gbf-asm/src/`: `isa`, `section`, `provenance`, `symbols`, `builder`, `cycle_model`, `layout`, `relax`, `encoder`, `listing`, plus a small ROM-builder wrapper.

### How this serves overarching project goals

- Compiler-generated code (Stage 12 backend in Epic B) emits `AsmIR` and **must not** make raw MBC writes — the legal path is `BankLease` pseudo-ops that go through F-A4.
- The runtime nucleus (F-A5) is itself authored as Rust builders that emit `AsmIR` sections (`planv0.md` line 305: "this is the key interpretation of 'end-to-end Rust' on Game Boy").
- `ReachabilityValidation` (Epic B, deferred) computes residency / privilege classes by walking `AsmIR`'s `MachineEffect` and `PrivilegeClass` annotations. Without those types here, the validation pass can't be written later.
- `gbf-bench` (Epic E) reads cycle annotations from `AsmIR` to ground its calibration.

### The problem this solves

Without `gbf-asm`:
1. Anyone emitting bytes invents their own ad hoc encoder, which silently disagrees with anyone else's encoder.
2. There is no single place where "is this MBC write legal here?" can be asked.
3. Cycle models live as comments in random files.
4. Provenance — which compiler stage emitted which byte — is lost the moment bytes leave the compiler.

### Design

Module-level layout (matching the existing scaffold):

```
gbf-asm/src/
  isa.rs          # legal LR35902 instruction shapes, registers, addressing modes
  section.rs      # SectionRole, SectionId, MachineEffect, PrivilegeClass
  provenance.rs   # InstrProvenance — which compile stage emitted what
  symbols.rs      # symbol table, label resolution, .sym output
  builder.rs      # typed Builder eDSL emitting Instr + Db/Dw + pseudo-ops
  cycle_model.rs  # per-instruction M-cycle costs (taken/not-taken branches)
  layout.rs       # section placement, address assignment
  relax.rs        # branch relaxation (rel-jump <-> jp <-> far-call thunk)
  encoder.rs      # the only thing that turns Instr into bytes
  listing.rs      # human-readable .lst emitter
```

Plus a small `rom.rs` (or a top-level helper) that takes encoded sections + cartridge header and produces a `.gb` blob.

### Pseudo-ops the eDSL must support (planv0.md line 1511)

- `BankLease { bank, mode }` → consumed by F-A4.
- `BankRelease { lease }`
- `FarCall { target, lease_chain }`
- `Yield { kind: YieldKind }` — consumed by Epic B's slice scheduler.
- `TraceProbe { id, level }` — consumed by Epic D's trace pipeline.
- `AssertBank { expected }` — debugging assertion.
- `Db(u8)`, `Dw(u16)` — data directives. `Raw(Vec<u8>)` exists but is a code smell.

### Children (T-A1.1 ... T-A1.9)

1. **T-A1.1** ISA types + register/operand modeling.
2. **T-A1.2** SectionRole + SectionId + symbol table + provenance.
3. **T-A1.3** Builder eDSL (instructions + pseudo-ops + Db/Dw).
4. **T-A1.4** `MachineEffect` + `PrivilegeClass` typing on instructions/sections.
5. **T-A1.5** Cycle model.
6. **T-A1.6** Layout + branch relaxation.
7. **T-A1.7** Encoder.
8. **T-A1.8** Listing (.lst) emitter.
9. **T-A1.9** ROM builder (.gb assembler with cartridge header).

### Confidence: HIGH

Pure types and a deterministic encoder. The hardest part is getting `MachineEffect` / `PrivilegeClass` right because they are consumed by ReachabilityValidation later — the typing must be expressive enough that "this MBC write is privileged" / "this load is bank-dependent" can be asked locally.

## Agent Instructions (Feature)

**This is a feature bead.** Implement via child tasks T-A1.1–T-A1.9. Tasks should land roughly in the dep order; T-A1.7 (encoder) is the integration point.

### Feature Definition of Done

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-asm                                       # All module tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- encoder::round_trip                 # Encode then decode (where decoders exist) round-trips
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- cycle_model::known_instructions     # Cycle costs match Pan Docs for a curated set
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- layout::branch_relaxation           # Out-of-range rel-jump becomes jp; cross-bank becomes far-call thunk
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- listing::stable                     # Listings are byte-stable across runs (no nondeterminism)
cargo run -p gbf-asm --example tiny_rom                      # Produces a 32 KiB .gb that boots in an emulator
```

title: F15: Post-closure follow-ups (M2–M6) — extensions to F1/F3/F6 contracts
description: Catch-all feature housing tasks that extend the contracts of F1/F3/F6 (now closed) without re-opening them. Each child task is a discrete extension with its own milestone tag (M1/M2/M6) and acceptance criteria. The child task may add a new variant, a new export-side representation, or wire up a previously-deferred Burn adapter, all without changing the closed feature's contract surface. Existed as orphans before being adopted into the training epic on 2026-05-06.

title: F1: Reframe Burn as Training Front-End, gbf-model Owns Deployed Numerics
description: Burn provides backend portability, autodiff, optimizers, checkpoints, and metrics. gbf-model owns ternary projection, activation fake-quant, norm approximation, and export visitation via qat::{ternary, activation, norm, export} modules. Burn-native PTQ remains as baseline/ablation. Pin exact Burn version.
acceptance_criteria: All T1.x tasks closed; cargo check -p gbf-model -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat passes; gbf-model does NOT depend on burn
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

**Artifact contract:** F1 establishes that `gbf-model` owns deployable QAT numerics and artifact-facing export contracts. The implemented artifact boundary is `ExportVisitor` producing `ArtifactCore`, `QuantSpec`, `ExportFacts`, artifact metadata, and canonical tensors where applicable for ternary weights/scales, dense weights/biases, embeddings, classifiers, router/shared-dense branches, and norm LUTs. Activations export as range/quant facts, not tensors. Sequence-state semantics are owned by F12; F1 only ensures the QAT/export boundary composes with model-owned artifact types. Target compiler/runtime lowering and ArtifactOracle agreement are not claimed by F1; those remain downstream validation.

**differentiable Burn path:** Burn is contained in `gbf-train` behind the adapter boundary. Implemented Burn-backed paths cover finite-input ternary linear, activation fake-quant for fixed ranges, norm approximation, router, and expert block adapters with finite owned adapter state. The supported adapters use Burn tensors/autodiff for training while exporting back through model-owned scalar cores and `ExportVisitor`. Global non-finite error-boundary parity with scalar code is not claimed by F1 except where focused tests exist. Learned/EMA activation range state is intentionally not accepted on the Burn path yet; constructors reject it and `bd-1zv` owns that future behavior.

**Tests proving it:**

- `cargo check -p gbf-model -p gbf-train`
- `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat`
- `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat`
- `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -p gbf-train -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- -D warnings`
- `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`
- `./scripts/check_burn_pin.sh`
- `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`
- `if grep -q '^burn' gbf-model/Cargo.toml; then exit 1; fi`

**Acceptance movement:** Yes. The original F1 T1.1-T1.8 scope is complete. The later follow-up `bd-1zv` remains open for Burn-backed `ActivationRangeMode::Learned` and `ActivationRangeMode::Ema` state. Current Burn activation adapters reject those dynamic modes with a focused test, so F1 closes with no future dynamic range behavior accepted.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| QAT module namespace and model-owned scalar numerics | supported | not applicable | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat`; export support is via model-owned contracts |
| Finite-input `TernaryLinearQat` scalar forward/export | supported | supported | supported | `qat_ternary_forward_and_export_share_projection`; `burn_ternary_forward_export_and_gradients_share_owned_tensors` |
| Finite-input `ActFakeQuant` fixed activation ranges | supported | supported | supported | `qat_activation_forward_clamps_and_quantizes_in_range`; `burn_activation_forward_matches_core_scalar_projection`; export emits activation quant/range facts |
| `ActivationRangeMode::Learned` scalar/export support and Burn rejection | supported | rejected | supported | `burn_activation_rejects_dynamic_range_modes_until_state_is_owned`; export support is scalar range only; `bd-1zv` owns Burn state |
| `ActivationRangeMode::Ema` scalar/export support and Burn rejection | supported | rejected | supported | `burn_activation_rejects_dynamic_range_modes_until_state_is_owned`; export support is scalar range only; `bd-1zv` owns Burn state |
| Finite-input `NormApproxQat` scalar/Burn/export behavior | supported | supported | supported | `qat_norm_affine_clip_lut_forward_and_export_match`; `burn_norm_export_matches_artifact_contract_from_owned_tensors`; export is metadata or LUT tensor where applicable |
| Finite-input `Top1RouterQat` scalar/Burn behavior | supported | supported | supported | `qat_router_hard_top1_returns_single_expert_and_aux_losses`; `burn_router_forward_matches_scalar_router_outputs`; `qat_export_visitor_extracts_router_shared_dense_and_norm_lut_tensors` |
| Finite-input `ExpertBlockQat` scalar/Burn behavior | supported | supported | supported | `qat_expert_composes_up_activation_down_and_residual`; `burn_expert_forward_matches_scalar_expert_block`; `qat_export_visitor_records_all_qat_module_kinds_and_activation_ranges` |
| `ExportVisitor` artifact assembly | supported | not applicable | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` |
| Direct Burn dependency in `gbf-model` | not applicable | rejected | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`; direct Cargo grep |
| Burn version pin and adapter containment | not applicable | supported | not applicable | `adapter::tests::adapter_constants_match_pinned_requirement`; `adapter::tests::burn_static_boundary_from_env_root_passes` |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| F1 T1.1-T1.8 implementation children are closed | `br show bd-2q4 bd-3p5 bd-pk3 bd-2fi bd-ubm bd-g90 bd-1mv bd-1ptv bd-2fy` checked manually | none | `bd-1zv` is a later P3 dynamic-range follow-up, not accepted as part of T1.1-T1.8 |
| `gbf-model` owns QAT scalar numerics and export-facing contracts | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat` | none | Covers ternary, activation, norm, router, expert, and export visitor paths |
| Burn training logic is behind `gbf-train` adapters and remains out of `gbf-model` | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`; `if grep -q '^burn' gbf-model/Cargo.toml; then exit 1; fi` | none | Architecture test also checks direct source imports |
| Implemented Burn adapters execute and differentiate where expected | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat` | `burn-adapter` | Covers finite-path ternary, activation, norm, router, and expert adapters; global non-finite parity is not claimed |
| Burn adapter code is warning-clean under the feature gate | `cargo clippy -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- -D warnings` | `burn-adapter` | Plain clippy without the feature does not lint adapter implementation |
| Fixed activation range Burn adapter matches scalar projection | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::activation::tests::burn_activation_forward_matches_core_scalar_projection` | `burn-adapter` | Also covered by full QAT train adapter gate |
| Learned/EMA activation range Burn state is not silently accepted | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::activation::tests::burn_activation_rejects_dynamic_range_modes_until_state_is_owned` | `burn-adapter` | Future implementation remains with `bd-1zv` |
| Export visitor assembles artifact-facing QAT state | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` | none | Does not claim target-specific lowering or ArtifactOracle agreement |
| Burn version pin and static drift check pass | `./scripts/check_burn_pin.sh` | none | CI/static pin entry point for the Burn boundary |
| QAT closure and architecture sensors remain clean | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Run before closure |

## No-future

F1 accepts no future QAT/Burn variants without an executable path and tests. Learned and EMA activation ranges exist in the scalar model contract and artifact range export, but Burn-owned learned params/live EMA state are rejected today and moved to `bd-1zv`. Target-specific lowering, ArtifactOracle agreement, production packed bytes, and train-to-export pipeline proof are outside F1 and remain owned by downstream beads.

## Feedback Harness Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| F1 has a later open child (`bd-1zv`) despite T1.1-T1.8 being complete | Follow-up dynamic range work was added after the original feature scope | Close F1 only with an explicit support matrix row that rejects/moves dynamic Burn range state | `burn_activation_rejects_dynamic_range_modes_until_state_is_owned` | `bd-1zv`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat` |
| Risk of overclaiming Burn support for scalar-only variants | Scalar `ActFakeQuant` supports Learned/EMA, but Burn adapter state does not | Path-specific support rows must distinguish scalar, Burn, and artifact/export paths | QAT closure matrix plus harness moved-owner check | `qat-bead-closure`; `scripts/qat_harness_checks.py` |
| Risk of closing architecture feature without proving Burn containment | Feature definition is architectural, not just test-count based | Include both runtime tests and static architecture gates for direct Burn dependency containment | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`; direct Cargo grep | F1 closure gate |
| Future feature closures can miss lower-priority open follow-ups | `br ready` prioritization shows feature beads before P3 follow-ups | Before closing a feature, list child statuses and classify open children as blocking or moved | Manual child status check; no cheap general sensor yet | Beads workflow |
| Closure wording overclaimed activation tensors and sequence-state ownership | Artifact terminology from downstream export work leaked into the F1 ownership claim | Say "artifact metadata plus canonical tensors where applicable" and keep F12 sequence-state semantics out of F1 closure | Review packet; QAT closure checklist | `qat-bead-closure` |
| Burn/scalar parity was broader than the tests prove | Finite-path Burn tests do not prove every scalar non-finite error boundary | Scope Burn adapter claims to finite inputs and finite adapter state unless rejection guards exist | Future non-finite Burn adapter tests if parity is required | Burn adapter hardening; `bd-1zv` for dynamic range state |

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F1: Reframe Burn as Training Front-End

### Background

The original plan said "Burn-fronted with custom fake-quant / STE layers because built-in QAT is currently not supported." This is true but incomplete — it doesn't say WHERE those custom modules live or WHO owns the numeric contracts that cross the training/artifact boundary.

Burn's docs position it as a Rust training framework with swappable backends. Its value is: autodiff, optimizer implementations (Adam, AdaFactor, etc.), checkpoint serialization, metric logging, and backend portability (wgpu, tch, ndarray, candle). What Burn does NOT currently offer is QAT, ternary/binary projection, or hardware-aware fake quantization.

### The problem this solves

Without this reframing, the first implementer faces an ownership ambiguity: does `gbf-model` contain Burn `Module` types that happen to have quantization, or does `gbf-train` contain quantization logic that wraps Burn modules? The answer matters because:

1. The export boundary (what goes into ArtifactCore) must be defined by whoever owns the quantization modules
2. The fake-quant forward pass (used during training) must match the real quantization (used during export) — if they live in different crates, drift is inevitable
3. The ExportVisitor pattern (walk the model, extract canonical tensors + scales + metadata) is tightly coupled to the quantization module structure

### Design decision

`gbf-model` owns a `qat` module namespace containing:
- `TernaryLinearQat` — wraps a Burn Linear, adds learnable/annealed thresholds and per-row scales, provides both a fake-quant forward (for training) and an export method (for artifact generation)
- `ActFakeQuant` — activation clipping/quantization at expert input/output boundaries
- `NormApproxQat` — deployable norm approximation (AffineClipLut or TileRmsThenAffineClip)
- `Top1RouterQat` — low-rank top-1 router with z-loss and export support
- `ExpertBlockQat` — composes the above for a complete MoE expert block
- `ExportVisitor` — walks a trained model, extracts all canonical tensors, scales, metadata, and emits ArtifactCore + TernaryWeightPlan

Burn stays as the training host: you derive `Module` on your model struct, Burn gives you `.forward()`, `.backward()`, `.step()`, `.save()`. But the numeric semantics of what gets exported are defined in gbf-model's qat modules.

### Why Burn-native PTQ is kept as ablation path

Even though QAT is the production path, Burn's native PTQ (post-training quantization, if/when available) should remain as a baseline for:
- Shared modules (embedding, router, classifier) that may stay at higher precision
- Ablation experiments comparing PTQ vs QAT quality
- Quick iteration when you want to test a model architecture change without running the full QAT schedule

### The adapter layer

`gbf-train` should contain a thin adapter layer that:
- Pins the exact Burn version (e.g., `burn = "=0.16.0"`)
- Wraps any Burn API that might change between versions
- Provides a stable internal interface that gbf-model's QAT modules program against
- Includes a CI check that warns on Burn version bumps

### Confidence level: VERY HIGH

This is the single best framing correction in the whole patch. The ownership ambiguity it resolves would otherwise cause real implementation paralysis.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:

gbf-model owns: ternary projection, activation fake-quant, norm approximation, export visitation.

title: T-A5.5: Keyboard input + on-screen layout
description: On-screen keyboard per RFC section 3E. Ships the M0 4x10 ASCII layout as KeyboardLayoutSpec with KeyboardCell entries and a host-serializable KeyboardLayoutManifest; Shift and Cancel are reserved SpecialKey variants not present in the default layout. Each step reads cached and previous joypad state from the joypad module, moves/clamps the cursor, stages cursor/text redraws as UiCommitOp queue entries, writes accepted characters to the F-A5-owned M0 prompt buffer, and sets PROMPT_SUBMITTED_FLAG_ADDR on Start. It must not read JOYP directly or signal harness protocols.
acceptance_criteria:
cargo check -p gbf-runtime
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- keyboard::default_layout
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- keyboard::cursor_movement
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- keyboard::special_keys
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- keyboard::step_emits_only_queue_ops

Gates: scripts/review/f-a5/verify-packet.sh; cargo test -p gbf-runtime --lib keyboard::. Reviews: Gemini/Claude/Codex loops addressed.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A5.5: Keyboard input + layout

**Target crate**: `gbf-runtime`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-runtime/src/keyboard.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 1626 + `KeyboardLayoutSpec` (line 703).

### Background

The on-screen keyboard is how users input prompts. It's a grid of glyphs with a cursor; D-pad moves the cursor, A presses, B backspaces, Start submits. The layout is data-driven from `LexicalSpec.charset` (Epic G) so character set changes don't touch keyboard code.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub fn build_keyboard_section() -> Section;

pub struct KeyboardLayoutSpec {
    pub rows: u8,
    pub columns: u8,
    pub characters: &'static [u8],                  // grid laid out row-major
    pub special_keys: &'static [SpecialKey],
}

pub enum SpecialKey {
    Backspace,
    Submit,
    Shift,                                          // toggles charset slice (e.g., letters <-> punct)
    Cancel,
}

pub struct KeyboardState {
    pub cursor_row: u8,
    pub cursor_col: u8,
    pub charset_slice: u8,
}

/// Emit AsmIR: read joypad cached state, advance cursor / commit character.
pub fn emit_keyboard_step<B: Builder>(b: &mut B);
```

Keyboard state lives in WRAM. Each step:
1. Read joypad (T-A5.3 cached state).
2. If just-pressed direction: move cursor (text re-render via T-A5.4).
3. If just-pressed A: emit current character into the prompt buffer.
4. If just-pressed B: backspace.
5. If just-pressed Start: signal `HarnessOp::SetSession` or whatever the prompt protocol is.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-runtime
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- keyboard::default_layout            # Sane DMG-sized layout exists
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- keyboard::cursor_movement            # D-pad moves cursor; bounds clamp
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- keyboard::special_keys               # Backspace/Submit work
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: SpecialKey is enumerated.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## RFC F-A5 alignment - 2026-05-03

Source of truth: history/rfcs/F-A5-bank0-runtime.md section 3E. This comment supersedes earlier bead text where inconsistent.

Corrections:
- The default M0 layout is exactly 4 rows by 10 columns: a-z, 0-9, period, space, Backspace, Submit.
- Use KeyboardCell::Char, KeyboardCell::Special, and KeyboardCell::Empty plus KeyboardLayoutManifest for host serialization.
- Shift and Cancel are reserved enum variants and are not in the M0 default layout.
- Start sets PROMPT_SUBMITTED_FLAG_ADDR in the F-A5-owned prompt buffer. Do not wire HarnessOp or F-D2 protocols here.
- Keyboard reads JOYPAD_CACHED_STATE_ADDR and JOYPAD_PREV_STATE_ADDR from joypad; it never reads JOYP directly.
- Rendering work is staged through UiCommitOp queue entries only; no direct VRAM access.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: Post-emulator correctness note: keyboard accept/backspace redraws now derive screen x/y from the prompt cursor over the DMG 20-column visible text layout. Backspace also clears the logical prompt-buffer byte before enqueueing the blank glyph, so WRAM prompt state and the video_commit redraw remain in sync.

title: F-G1: Corpus pipeline — gbf-data governance
description: CorpusManifest with source provenance, deterministic normalization, dedup policy, contamination check vs WorkloadManifests, license metadata, deterministic train/val/test splits. Data lineage as reproducibility peer of build lineage.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-G1: Corpus pipeline

### What this is

`gbf-data` corpus governance: `CorpusManifest` with source provenance, deterministic normalization, dedup policy, contamination check vs `WorkloadManifest`s, license/usage metadata, deterministic train/val/test splits.

### Why this matters

Engineering rule + planv0.md line 2552: data lineage is a reproducibility peer of build lineage. Without governance, training corpus drift masquerades as model or compiler regressions.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct CorpusManifest {
    pub id: CorpusId,
    pub sources: Vec<CorpusSource>,
    pub normalization: NormalizationPolicy,
    pub dedup_policy: DedupPolicy,
    pub split: TrainValTestSplit,
    pub canary_prompts: Vec<PromptCase>,
}

pub fn check_contamination(corpus: &CorpusManifest, workloads: &[WorkloadManifest])
    -> ContaminationReport;
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-E1 WorkloadManifest (contamination target)
- F-G2 LexicalSpec (normalization)

### Confidence: HIGH

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Normalization rules — locked to Tier 2 charset

Decision date: 2026-04-27. Owner: bkase. Charset spec lives on bd-2ym0 (F-G2); this bead owns the deterministic pipeline that turns raw corpus into the 80-token stream.

### Deterministic normalization order

Apply in exactly this order — order is part of `NormalizationSpec`'s identity:

1. **Unicode NFC** normalize the input.
2. **Strip combining accents** via NFD-decompose-and-filter (`café` → `cafe`, `naïve` → `naive`).
3. **Preserve case as-is** — mixed case is load-bearing.
4. **Fold quotation/dash variants** to ASCII:
   - `“` `”` `«` `»` → `"`
   - `‘` `’` `‚` → `'`
   - `—` (em-dash) → `--`  (TranscriptSpec maps `--` back to em-dash on render)
   - `–` (en-dash) → `-`
   - `…` (ellipsis) → `...`
5. **Whitespace**: tab → single space; collapse runs of internal ASCII spaces to one; preserve `\n` exactly; trim trailing spaces on each line; CRLF → LF.
6. **Unmappable codepoint handling**: if any character outside the 79-printable-token set survives 1–5, replace with `<unk>`. If > 2 % of the post-normalization characters in a single training example are `<unk>`, drop the example.

### Drop policy

Drop reason recorded in CorpusManifest's per-source provenance:
- `unmappable_density` — too many `<unk>`s.
- `length_below_min` / `length_above_max` — bounds set by NormalizationSpec.
- `dedup_collision` — handled separately by DedupPolicy (still owned here).

### Sampling / splits

- Deterministic train/val/test split keyed by stable hash of the source-record id (NOT by a content hash, which would shift on normalization changes).
- `split_hashes: BTreeMap<SplitName, Hash256>` exported with the artifact for reproducibility.
- Canary prompts are an explicit list in CorpusManifest, NOT auto-sampled.

### Acceptance for closure

- A reference normalization run on a small fixture (~100 paragraphs covering accents, smart quotes, em-dashes, ellipses, tabs, CRLF, unmappables) with byte-exact expected output committed under fixtures/.
- Idempotence test: `normalize(normalize(x)) == normalize(x)` over the fixture.
- Determinism test: same input + same NormalizationSpec → byte-identical output across two runs.
- Drop-policy test: example with > 2 % unmappable density is dropped, recorded with reason.
- CorpusManifest serde + content-hash regression test.
- Contamination check vs WorkloadManifest is **not** part of v1 closure — moved to F-E1 (bd-1rao) integration. State this explicitly in closure if the cross-check is stubbed.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-G2 (bd-2ym0) — LexicalSpec / charset (this bead consumes it).
- F-E1 (bd-1rao) — WorkloadManifest (contamination check target). Stubbed in v1.
- F-G3 (bd-a4du) — Decode policy is independent.

### Genre intent

Hard-sci-fi corpus, Stross / *Accelerando* register. Pre-1928 public-domain hard-sci-fi (Asimov, Clarke, etc. once out of copyright) and license-clean modern equivalents are acceptable v1 sources. License metadata is mandatory in CorpusManifest's per-source provenance.

title: T12.2: Implement LinearState sequence block under SequenceSemanticsSpec
description: Implement a recurrent / linear-attention-like sequence block satisfying SequenceBlock. State fits in WRAM ping-pong, no growth with context. Includes QAT-compatible numerics (activation fake-quant at boundaries; norm via NormApproxQat). Config-gated; tiny-model fixture instantiates it.
close_reason:
Implemented `LinearStateBlock` as the backend-independent scalar sequence block under `gbf-model::sequence::linear_state`.

QAT closure questions:

- Artifact contract: `SequenceSemanticsSpec::LinearState(state_bytes_per_layer)` is carried into `SequenceState`, `SequenceExportFacts`, and `ArtifactCore`. `ExportVisitor::visit_linear_state_block` now walks the block and emits real `CanonicalTensor`s for the input norm LUT plus both ternary projections; sequence tensor handles are recorded from the emitted tensor IDs and tested against `ArtifactCore`. This does not claim compiler/runtime byte execution agreement.
- Differentiable Burn path: moved to `bd-2bm4`. `gbf-model` remains Burn-free; the new bead owns the LinearState Burn adapter, recurrence gradient smoke, and TrainPhase hardness propagation once `bd-ptk`/`bd-2uw` exist.
- Tests: see claim-to-gate matrix below.
- Moved acceptance: gradient flow through the recurrence and `TrainPhaseSpec`/`QuantHardness::Soft` tiny instantiation are moved to `bd-2bm4`/`bd-ptk`/`bd-2uw`; training scaffold and A/B fixture adoption remain owned by `bd-3jv`.

Support matrix:

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `LinearStateBlock` fixed-state recurrence | supported | moved to `bd-2bm4` | supported for `ArtifactCore` tensor materialization | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::linear_state`; `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` |
| Single shared `SequenceState` buffer | supported for `batch == 1` | moved to `bd-2bm4` | n/a | `linear_state_forward_rejects_multi_batch_until_state_is_batch_shaped` |
| `batch > 1` forward | rejected | moved to `bd-2bm4` | n/a | `linear_state_forward_rejects_multi_batch_until_state_is_batch_shaped` |
| Norm/activation/ternary QAT boundaries | supported with scalar QAT kernels | moved to `bd-2bm4`/`bd-2uw` | supported via visitor for norm/projection metadata | `linear_state_constructor_rejects_incompatible_norm_tile_width`; `qat_export_visitor_walks_linear_state_block_and_records_real_sequence_handles` |
| F4 Off/Soft/Hard `TrainPhaseSpec` hardness | not implemented in this bead | moved to `bd-ptk`, `bd-2uw`, `bd-2bm4` | n/a | owner beads exist and name the behavior |

Claim-to-gate matrix:

Feedback-harness reflection:

| Batch rows shared one state buffer implicitly | `SequenceState` had one byte buffer while `SequenceActivation` allowed batches | Added AGENTS sequence-state rule: reject `batch > 1` unless state is batch-shaped | `linear_state_forward_rejects_multi_batch_until_state_is_batch_shaped` | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::linear_state` |

| Scalar recurrence proof was too weak | Tests checked mutation, not the update law | Added AGENTS sequence-state rule requiring a literal multi-token oracle | `linear_state_forward_matches_literal_two_token_recurrence_oracle` | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::linear_state` |
| Gradient/TrainPhase acceptance had no owner | Bead mixed scalar `gbf-model` work with future Burn/phase work | Added AGENTS sequence-state rule separating scalar kernels, Burn adapters, phase hardness, and A/B scaffold claims | New `bd-2bm4`; existing `bd-ptk`, `bd-2uw`, `bd-3jv` | owner beads |

Commands run:

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-model/src/sequence/linear_state.rs` (new).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search "LinearState", "fixed-state recurrent", "linear-attention". Also F12 (bd-144) and T12.1 (bd-ctl).

### Implementation Notes

Implement a `LinearStateBlock` that satisfies `SequenceBlock`:

- **State shape**: fixed-size per layer, no growth with context.
- **Update rule**: a recurrent / linear-attention-like update. Initial implementation can mirror a simple linear-attention or RWKV-lite variant — concrete choice can be revisited; the contract is that state size is bounded and per-token compute is O(state_size).
- **Numerics**: QAT-compatible. Use the QAT modules from F1 (TernaryLinearQat, ActFakeQuant, NormApproxQat) at the appropriate boundaries — expert input/output, residual boundaries, deployable norm. Hardness controlled by `TrainPhaseSpec`.
- **State location at deploy**: WRAM ping-pong (no SRAM paging required). State is checkpointed to SRAM at semantic checkpoint boundaries via the persistence record protocol; this is checkpoint behavior, not hot-path behavior.
- **Export facts**: populate `SequenceExportFacts` with measured `state_bytes_per_layer`, the canonical tensor handles for any per-block parameters, and the SequenceSemanticsSpec::LinearState variant.

### What "good enough for v1" looks like

- Tiny tests instantiate it with `state_bytes_per_layer = 64` and run a few forward passes.
- Gradient flows through the recurrence (smoke test).
- ExportVisitor (T1.6) can walk it and produce CanonicalTensors for its parameters.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                                   # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::linear_state           # Forward + grad smoke tests
cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence_block::linear_state     # Implements SequenceBlock trait
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings                    # No warnings
# Verify: state_size().bytes_per_token == 0 (state does not grow with tokens)
# Verify: state_size().bytes_per_layer matches the configured constant
# Verify: A tiny LinearStateBlock instantiates under a TrainPhaseSpec with QuantHardness::Soft
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: Implementing `SequenceBlock` means the type system enforces all required operations are present.
- §III.1: Bounded-state invariant is a type-level guarantee (bytes_per_token == 0).
- §VI.1: Block lives in `gbf-model::sequence::linear_state`, not scattered across training code.

title: F-F2: Certificates — range, arena, window, reachability, resource_state
description: Machine-checkable certificates per engineering rule 23. Each cert carries pass version, inputs hash, outputs hash, and structured proof. gbf-verify re-checks the structured claim independently.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-F2: Certificates

### What this is

Machine-checkable certificates per planv0.md correction: `certs/range.cert.json`, `certs/arena.cert.json`, `certs/window.cert.json`, `certs/reachability.cert.json`, `certs/resource_state.cert.json`. Each consumed by `gbf-verify` as proof.

### Why this matters

Engineering rule 23: "Critical safety passes emit machine-checkable certificates consumed by `gbf-verify`; human-readable reports are not the only source of truth for pass validity." Without certificates, "ReachabilityValidation passed" is just a log line.

### Design sketch

Each certificate carries the pass version, inputs hash, outputs hash, and a structured proof artifact. `gbf-verify` re-checks the structured claim independently.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B5 RangePlan -> certs/range
- F-B8 RomWindowPlan -> certs/window
- F-B10 ArenaPlan -> certs/arena
- F-B11 ResourceStateValidation -> certs/resource_state
- F-B13 ReachabilityValidation -> certs/reachability
- F-F3 gbf-verify checker

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T1.5: Implement Top1RouterQat and ExpertBlockQat
description: Top1RouterQat: low-rank top-1 router with z-loss, expert dropout, temporal smoothness. ExpertBlockQat: composes TernaryLinearQat (up + down projections), ActFakeQuant, residual connection, optional shared dense branch. Both must support the phased QAT hardness control from F4.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router passes; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert passes; Top-1 routing with aux losses; Two-matrix expert composition
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

- Artifact contract: this bead implements the pre-export scalar/model contract in gbf-model. Top1RouterQat owns the higher-precision low-rank router core, top-1 expert selection, routing weights, explicit dropout/jitter inputs, previous-token temporal smoothing state, and typed RouterAuxLosses. ExpertBlockQat owns validated two-matrix expert composition, TernaryLinearQat up projection -> ActFakeQuant boundary -> TernaryLinearQat down projection, residual addition, and an optional alpha-gated shared dense branch. Final ArtifactCore export materialization is moved to bd-g90, and first-class tensor materialization for exported scale tensors is moved to bd-209.
- Where is the differentiable Burn path: existing differentiable Burn paths for TernaryLinearQat and ActFakeQuant remain in gbf-train QAT adapters. The concrete router/expert Burn adapter path is owned by bd-1ptv; this bead exposes backend-independent scalar cores and phase-control options.
- Tests proving it: cargo check -p gbf-model; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat; cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings; python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py; cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture.
- Acceptance moved: the bead text's Tensor-shaped forward signature is implemented here as the repository's current backend-independent slice API, matching the existing TernaryLinearQat and ActFakeQuant scalar cores. The richer temporal ring-window and sequence-boundary training policy is moved to bd-122. Export/artifact lowering is moved to bd-g90 and bd-209; router/expert Burn adapters are moved to bd-1ptv. This closure does not claim exact compiler/runtime lowering agreement.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Low-rank Top1RouterQat projection with nonzero default rank | supported | moved | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router`; bd-1ptv; bd-g90 |
| HardTop1 router selection | supported | moved | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router`; bd-1ptv; bd-g90 |
| SoftTop1 router distribution | supported | moved | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router`; bd-1ptv |
| Router dropout mask and logit jitter | supported | moved | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router`; bd-1oc |
| Router z-loss, token balance proxy, and temporal smoothness loss components | supported | moved | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router`; bd-1ptv; bd-122 |
| ExpertBlockQat hard ternary two-matrix path | supported | moved | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert`; bd-1ptv; bd-g90 |
| ExpertBlockQat full-precision phase path | supported | moved | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert`; bd-1ptv |
| Optional shared dense branch | supported | moved | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert`; bd-wsd; bd-g90 |
| Canonical artifact tensor export | moved | moved | moved | bd-g90; bd-209 |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Top1RouterQat validates model dimension, expert count, rank, projection shapes, finite weights, and nonzero default rank. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router` | none | Covered by router invalid-contract and default-rank tests. |
| HardTop1 returns one expert index with one-hot routing weights and typed aux losses. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router` | none | Covered by hard-top1 and weighted-aux tests. |
| SoftTop1, explicit dropped-expert masks, and logit jitter are available without embedding RNG policy in gbf-model. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router` | none | Stochastic policy integration is left at the training boundary. |
| Temporal smoothing state updates only at the forward boundary and can be reset at sequence boundaries. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router` | none | Full contiguous-window policy is moved to bd-122. |
| ExpertBlockQat validates expert dimensions and composes TernaryLinearQat -> ActFakeQuant -> TernaryLinearQat with residual addition. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert` | none | Covered by expert composition and activation-boundary tests. |
| ExpertBlockQat supports hard-quantized and full-precision phase paths. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert` | none | Scalar phase control only; Burn wrappers remain at the adapter boundary. |
| Optional shared dense branch is explicit, alpha-gated, and can initialize to expert-only behavior. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert` | none | Configuration/profile evolution remains with bd-wsd. |
| gbf-model remains free of direct Burn imports and test-only QAT backend seams. | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Architecture sensor passed. |
| QAT closure shape and moved-acceptance ownership satisfy the harness. | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py` | none | Harness passed after bd-1ptv replaced the old generic Burn owner. |
| The gbf-model QAT implementation is formatted, compiles, and has no clippy warnings. | `cargo fmt --check --all`; `cargo check -p gbf-model`; `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings` | none | All passed. |

## No-future Variant Acceptance

- Accepted public router modes are only SoftTop1 and HardTop1, and both are covered by scalar tests.
- Accepted public expert phase modes are only FullPrecision and HardQuantized, and both are covered by scalar tests.
- Top-2 routing, stochastic dropout policy generation, soft ternary STE behavior, final ArtifactCore materialization, and first-class exported tensor materialization are not accepted by this bead. They are either rejected at current boundaries, not exposed as public variants, or moved to bd-g90, bd-209, bd-1ptv, bd-1oc, bd-122, or bd-wsd as listed above.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**:
- `gbf-model/src/qat/router.rs` — Top1RouterQat
- `gbf-model/src/qat/expert.rs` — ExpertBlockQat

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "Top1RouterQat", "ExpertBlockQat", "top-1 routing", "z-loss", "expert dropout", "temporal smoothness", "low-rank router".

### Implementation Notes

**Top1RouterQat**:
- Low-rank top-1 router: `d_model -> rank -> n_experts` (rank = min(n_experts/4, 8))
- Both projection matrices stay at higher precision (not ternary)
- Training features: z-loss, expert dropout with jitter, temporal smoothness regularization
- `forward(&self, x: Tensor) -> (expert_index: Tensor, routing_weights: Tensor, aux_losses: RouterAuxLosses)`
- Must track previous-token routing distribution for temporal smoothness (buffer)

**ExpertBlockQat**:
- Composes: TernaryLinearQat (up proj) -> ActFakeQuant -> TernaryLinearQat (down proj)
- Plus: residual connection, optional shared dense branch
- `forward(&self, x: Tensor, expert_id: usize) -> Tensor`

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router          # Router tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert          # Expert tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings        # No warnings
# Verify: Top1RouterQat returns single expert index (top-1)
# Verify: ExpertBlockQat composes TernaryLinearQat + ActFakeQuant correctly
# Verify: aux losses (z-loss, balance) are computed and returned
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: RouterAuxLosses as a struct, not loose floats — typed loss components
- §I.2: Pure routing math; temporal buffer state update at boundary
- §III.1: Type-check that router output matches expert dispatch expectations

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: PARALLELISM NOTE: This task bundles Top1RouterQat and ExpertBlockQat. These are in separate files (router.rs, expert.rs) and could be split into T1.5a and T1.5b for parallel execution. T7.5 (low-rank router) only needs Top1RouterQat, so splitting would let T7.5 start earlier. Consider splitting if agent throughput is a bottleneck on this path.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Activation boundary placement clarification

`bd-pk3` provides the standalone activation fake-quant module. This bead owns actual expert input/output boundary placement through `ExpertBlockQat` composition, including tests proving `TernaryLinearQat -> ActFakeQuant -> TernaryLinearQat` is wired at the intended expert boundaries.
